**(b)** With a circuit diagram, explain the working of a **negative clamper**. Derive the expression for the output voltage. The input is $V_{in} = 15 \sin(200\pi t)$ V. Find the peak and trough of the output waveform. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Circuit Diagram (1 Mark):**
- Capacitor $C$ in series between input and output.
- Diode with **cathode at ground** and **anode at output** (opposite of positive clamper).
- Load resistor $R_L$ from output to ground.

**Working (3 Marks):**
1. **First positive half cycle:** $V_{in}$ goes positive. The anode of the diode is more positive than the cathode → diode forward biased → ON. Output is shorted to 0 V. Capacitor charges to $V_C = V_m = 15$ V (right plate −, left plate +).
2. **First negative half cycle:** $V_{in}$ goes negative. The diode becomes reverse biased → OFF. Capacitor holds charge.
3. **Steady state:** $V_{out} = V_{in} - V_C$.

**Derivation (1 Mark):**

$$
V_{out} = 15 \sin(200\pi t) - 15 = -15[1 - \sin(200\pi t)]
$$

**Peak and Trough (2 Marks):**
- Peak: when $\sin(200\pi t) = +1$, $V_{out} = 15 - 15 = 0$ V.
- Trough: when $\sin(200\pi t) = -1$, $V_{out} = -15 - 15 = -30$ V.
- Peak-to-peak: $0 - (-30) = 30\ \text{V} = 2V_m$ (unchanged from input).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the diode assumption.** Always write: "Assuming an ideal diode with $V_\gamma = 0$ V..." or "Assuming a silicon diode with $V_\gamma = 0.7$ V..." at the start. **Loss: 1 mark per sub-part.**
> 2. **Confusing series and shunt clippers.** In a **series** clipper, the diode is in the signal path (in series with $R_L$). In a **shunt** clipper, the diode is parallel to $R_L$. Mixing up the orientation reverses which half cycle is clipped. **Loss: 2-3 marks.**
> 3. **Forgetting the time constant condition in clampers.** Always state: "The load resistance is chosen such that $R_L C \gg T$ so that the capacitor does not discharge appreciably between cycles." **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Not sketching both input and output waveforms with proper axis labels.** Always label time (t or $\omega t$) on x-axis and voltage on y-axis. Mark the peak ($V_m$, $2V_m$, or $V_R$), trough ($-V_m$, 0, or $V_R$), and clipping level. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
> 5. **Writing $V_{out} = V_{in} + V_C$ for a positive clamper but forgetting the diode drop for silicon.** With a silicon diode, $V_C = V_m - 0.7$ V, so the trough is at $-0.7$ V and peak at $2V_m - 0.7$ V. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Conflating clipping with clamping.** Clipping **changes the shape** (removes part of the waveform). Clamping **preserves the shape** (only shifts the DC level). This is the most fundamental distinction. **Loss: 2-3 marks if confused in definition.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Clipping and Clamping Circuits:**

### Quick-Reference: Clipping Circuits
- A **clipper** removes a portion of the input signal; the shape is **distorted**, and the peak-to-peak amplitude is **reduced**.
- **Series clipper**: diode in series with load. When ON, output = $V_{in}$. When OFF, output = 0 V.
- **Shunt clipper**: diode in parallel with load. When ON, output = 0 V. When OFF, output = $V_{in}$.
- **Positive clipper** clips the negative half; **negative clipper** clips the positive half.
- **Biased clipper** introduces a reference voltage $V_R$ to set the clipping threshold at a non-zero level.
- The diode is always assumed **ideal ($V_\gamma = 0$)** unless stated otherwise.
- Always draw the **transfer characteristic** (Vout vs Vin) for biased clippers.

### Quick-Reference: Clamping Circuits
- A **clamper** shifts the entire waveform up or down by adding a DC offset; the shape is **preserved**, and the peak-to-peak amplitude is **unchanged**.
- **Positive clamper** shifts waveform up so trough is at 0 V (ideal diode) or −0.7 V (silicon).
- **Negative clamper** shifts waveform down so peak is at 0 V (ideal diode) or +0.7 V (silicon).
- The capacitor charges to $V_C = V_m - V_\gamma$ during the first half cycle and holds this charge in steady state.
- Output of positive clamper: $V_{out} = V_{in} + V_m$ (ideal diode).
- Output of negative clamper: $V_{out} = V_{in} - V_m$ (ideal diode).
- The condition $R_L C \gg T$ ensures the capacitor does not discharge between cycles.
- The diode polarity determines whether it is a positive or negative clamper: **cathode up = positive clamper; anode up = negative clamper**.

### Key Numerical Values to Memorize
- Silicon diode $V_\gamma = 0.7$ V; Germanium $V_\gamma = 0.3$ V; Ideal $V_\gamma = 0$ V.
- Peak of positive clamper output = $2V_m$ (ideal) or $2V_m - 0.7$ V (Si).
- Trough of positive clamper output = 0 V (ideal) or $-0.7$ V (Si).
- Time constant for charging: $R_f C$ (small, fast charging through diode).
- Time constant for discharging: $R_L C$ (large, slow discharge through load).

### Key Distinctions to Remember
- **Clipping = shape change; Clamping = DC shift only.**
- **Series = diode in signal path; Shunt = diode in parallel with load.**
- **Positive clipper removes negative; Negative clipper removes positive.**
- **Positive clamper shifts up; Negative clamper shifts down.**
- **Biased version has a DC battery; unbiased version references ground (0 V).**

### Standard Lab Procedure Reminder
1. Connect the circuit on the breadboard as per the circuit diagram.
2. Apply a 1 kHz, 10 V peak sinusoidal input from the function generator.
3. Connect CH1 of the DSO across the input and CH2 across the output.
4. Set the DSO to **DC coupling** (not AC) to observe the true DC shift in clampers.
5. Measure the peak and trough of the output and compare with theoretical values.
6. Verify the time constant condition $R_L C \gg T$ by checking that the output waveform is flat (no sag) at the trough.
7. Tabulate readings, plot waveforms, and calculate percentage error: $\%\ \text{Error} = \left|\dfrac{V_{\text{theory}} - V_{\text{practical}}}{V_{\text{theory}}}\right| \times 100$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic RC circuits - High pass and Low pass filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL308 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Basic RC circuits - High pass and Low pass filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fundamental Definitions

An **RC Filter** is a fundamental linear, passive electronic circuit composed of a **Resistor (R)** and a **Capacitor (C)** arranged in a specific topology. It selectively allows certain frequency components of an input signal to pass through to the output while attenuating (reducing) others. Because they use only passive components, they are also called **Passive RC Filters**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCEEL308 - Analog Electronics Lab):**
> *"A basic RC circuit, when arranged in a series-shunt configuration, acts as a frequency-selective filter. A Low-Pass Filter (LPF) passes frequencies below its cutoff frequency, and a High-Pass Filter (HPF) passes frequencies above its cutoff frequency."*

**Low-Pass Filter (LPF):**
A first-order RC low-pass filter consists of a series resistor with the output taken across a shunt capacitor to ground. It **passes low frequencies** and **attenuates high frequencies** at a rate of **-20 dB/decade** beyond the cutoff point.

**High-Pass Filter (HPF):**
A first-order RC high-pass filter consists of a series capacitor with the output taken across a shunt resistor to ground. It **passes high frequencies** and **attenuates low frequencies** at a rate of **+20 dB/decade** below the cutoff point.

### 1.2 The Crucial Parameter — Cutoff Frequency ($f_c$)

The **cutoff frequency** (also called the **-3 dB frequency** or **half-power frequency**) is the boundary frequency at which the output signal power drops to **50%** of its maximum (input) value, equivalent to an amplitude drop to **70.7%** (i.e., $1/\sqrt{2}$).

The standard mathematical form is:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cutoff frequency depends **only on the component values** R (in Ohms) and C (in Farads), NOT on the input signal amplitude. The standard value for **laboratory experiments** uses **R = 1 kΩ** and **C = 0.1 µF**, which gives $f_c \approx 1.591$ **kHz**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water and Bucket" Model

Think of the RC filter as a **bucket with a hole** connected to a water pipe:

- **Resistor (R)** = The narrowness of the pipe (controls how much water flows in).
- **Capacitor (C)** = The bucket (stores water/charge temporarily).
- **Signal (Voltage)** = The water flow.

**For the Low-Pass Filter (LPF):**
Imagine you have a pipe (R) pouring water into a bucket (C) with a small leak. If the water is turned ON and OFF slowly (low frequency), the bucket has time to fill up and the water level (output) follows the input closely. But if the water is rapidly switched ON and OFF (high frequency), the bucket cannot fill or empty fast enough — the output is "smoothed out" or averaged. **Slow changes pass; fast changes are blocked.**

**For the High-Pass Filter (HPF):**
This time, imagine a bucket (C) feeding a pipe (R) to the output. A steady trickle of water (DC / very low frequency) just sits in the bucket and never reaches the output. But rapid gushes (high frequency) splash water out of the pipe quickly, reaching the output. **Fast changes pass; slow changes are blocked.**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** In an **L**PF, the **C**apacitor is the **L**ast component (output across C). In an **H**PF, the **C**apacitor is **F**irst (input sees C first). This makes the LPF and HPF "mirror images" of each other.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Topology and Operational Logic

#### 2.1.1 First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter (LPF)

**Circuit:** Input → R → Output Node → C → Ground. Output is taken across the capacitor.

**Step-by-step Operational Logic:**
1. At very low frequencies ($\omega \to 0$), the capacitor's impedance $X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C}$ becomes **very large** (acts like an open circuit). Almost the entire input voltage appears across the capacitor.
2. At very high frequencies ($\omega \to \infty$), $X_C \to 0$ (capacitor acts like a short circuit to ground). The output is shorted to ground → signal is attenuated.
3. At $f = f_c$, the magnitude of capacitive reactance equals the resistance: $X_C = R$.
4. Beyond $f_c$, the output amplitude decreases at a rate of **-20 dB per decade** (factor of 10 in frequency → factor of 10 in voltage).

#### 2.1.2 First-Order RC High-Pass Filter (HPF)

**Circuit:** Input → C → Output Node → R → Ground. Output is taken across the resistor.

**Step-by-step Operational Logic:**
1. At very low frequencies ($\omega \to 0$), $X_C \to \infty$. The series capacitor blocks the DC and low-frequency signal — almost no voltage reaches the output.
2. At very high frequencies ($\omega \to \infty$), $X_C \to 0$. The capacitor becomes a short circuit — the full input appears at the output.
3. At $f = f_c$, the magnitude of the output is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (about 70.7%) of the input.
4. Below $f_c$, the output amplitude increases at a rate of **+20 dB per decade** (as frequency decreases by 10, output drops by 10).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Transfer Function $H(j\omega)$** | $\dfrac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$ | $\dfrac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$ |
| **Magnitude Response $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$** | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ | $\dfrac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ |
| **Phase Response $\phi(\omega)$** | $-\arctan(\omega RC)$ | $+90° - \arctan(\omega RC)$ |
| **Cutoff Frequency $f_c$** | $\dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ |
| **Output at $f \ll f_c$** | $V_{out} \approx V_{in}$ (passes) | $V_{out} \to 0$ (blocks) |
| **Output at $f \gg f_c$** | $V_{out} \to 0$ (blocks) | $V_{out} \approx V_{in}$ (passes) |
| **Output at $f = f_c$** | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \, V_{in}$ | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \, V_{in}$ |
| **Gain at $f_c$ in dB** | $-3$ **dB** | $-3$ **dB** |
| **Roll-off Rate** | $-20$ **dB/decade** | $+20$ **dB/decade** |
| **Output Element** | Across Capacitor (C) | Across Resistor (R) |
| **Phase Shift Range** | $0°$ to $-90°$ (output lags) | $+90°$ to $0°$ (output leads) |
| **Unit of R, C** | Ohms ($\Omega$), Farads (F) | Ohms ($\Omega$), Farads (F) |

### 2.3 Bode Plot and Decibel Calculations

**Gain in decibels (dB):**

$$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$$

**For LPF Gain:**

$$A_{dB} = -10 \log_{10}\left[1 + \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2\right]$$

**For HPF Gain:**

$$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{f/f_c}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked to plot the frequency response, ALWAYS use a **semi-log graph** (linear dB on Y-axis, log frequency on X-axis). Failure to use log-scale on the X-axis is a common reason for losing **2 marks** in ESE valuation.

### 2.4 Time Domain Interpretation

In the time domain, a square wave input can be used to visualize the filter:
- **LPF with Square Wave Input:** Output becomes a rounded waveform. The capacitor charges/discharges through R with time constant $\tau = RC$. Fast edges are "rounded off."
- **HPF with Square Wave Input:** Output shows sharp spikes at the edges of the square wave (differentiator behavior).

The time-domain step response uses the standard capacitor charging equation:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{in}\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right) \quad \text{(Charging - LPF)}$$

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{in} \cdot e^{-t/RC} \quad \text{(Discharging)}$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| **Application Domain** | **Use of RC Filter** |
|---|---|
| **Audio Systems** | LPF removes high-frequency hiss; HPF removes DC offset and subsonic rumble |
| **Power Supplies** | LPF smooths rectified DC output (ripple filter) |
| **Communication** | Channel selection in receivers, anti-aliasing before ADC sampling |
| **Biomedical (ECG/EEG)** | HPF removes baseline drift; LPF removes high-frequency noise |
| **Oscilloscope Probes** | Compensating probe is an adjustable RC filter |
| **Control Systems** | Lead/Lag compensators use RC time constants |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Transfer Function — Low-Pass Filter (LPF)

**Starting Point:** Consider the LPF circuit with input $V_{in}$ applied to a series R, output $V_{out}$ taken across C.

Using the **Voltage Divider Rule** in the phasor (frequency) domain, the impedances are:
- Resistor: $Z_R = R$
- Capacitor: $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$

**Step 1:** Apply Voltage Divider.

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_C}{Z_R + Z_C}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the impedance values.

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$ to simplify.

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega RC + 1}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to standard form.

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 5:** Define the Transfer Function $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$.

$$\boxed{H_{LPF}(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

**Step 6:** Find the magnitude by taking the modulus.

$$|H_{LPF}(j\omega)| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1^2 + (\omega RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 7:** Find the phase angle.

$$\phi_{LPF}(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega RC}{1}\right) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

**Step 8:** Derive the Cutoff Frequency by setting $|H(j\omega)| = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (\omega_c RC)^2 = 1$$

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}$$

Converting angular frequency to Hz using $f_c = \dfrac{\omega_c}{2\pi}$:

$$\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \;\; \text{Hz}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Transfer Function — High-Pass Filter (HPF)

**Starting Point:** In the HPF, the input is applied to a series capacitor C, and the output $V_{out}$ is taken across the resistor R to ground.

**Step 1:** Apply Voltage Divider Rule with $Z_C$ now as the series element and $Z_R$ as the shunt element.

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_R}{Z_C + Z_R}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute impedance values.

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$.

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 4:** Write the Transfer Function.

$$\boxed{H_{HPF}(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

**Step 5:** Magnitude response.

$$|H_{HPF}(j\omega)| = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 6:** Phase response.

$$\phi_{HPF}(\omega) = +90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

**Step 7:** Cutoff frequency. Setting $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$\frac{\omega_c RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Squaring and cross-multiplying:

$$2(\omega_c RC)^2 = 1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (\omega_c RC)^2 = 1$$

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Standard Style)

#### **Example 1: Design a Low-Pass Filter**

**Problem:** Design an RC low-pass filter with a cutoff frequency of **5 kHz**. Use a standard capacitor value of **0.01 µF**. Find the required resistance.

**Solution:**

**Given:** $f_c = 5$ kHz $= 5 \times 10^3$ Hz, $C = 0.01 \, \mu F = 0.01 \times 10^{-6}$ F $= 10^{-8}$ F.

**Step 1:** Start from the cutoff frequency formula.

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for R.

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times (5 \times 10^3) \times (10^{-8})}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate denominator.

$$2 \pi \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} = 2 \pi \times 5 \times 10^{-5} \approx 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation.

$$R = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3183 \, \Omega \approx 3.18 \, k\Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> Use a standard value of **3.3 kΩ** in practice. The small deviation from 5 kHz is acceptable for laboratory verification.

#### **Example 2: Calculate Output Voltage at a Given Frequency**

**Problem:** For an RC LPF with $R = 1 \, k\Omega$ and $C = 0.1 \, \mu F$, find the output voltage when $V_{in} = 5$ V (peak) and $f = 10$ kHz.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the cutoff frequency.

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1591.5 \, Hz \approx 1.59 \, kHz$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $\omega RC$ at $f = 10$ kHz.

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 10^4 \, rad/s$$

$$\omega RC = 2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-7} = 2\pi \times 1 = 6.2832$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the magnitude of the transfer function.

$$|H| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (6.2832)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 39.48}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{40.48}} = \frac{1}{6.363} \approx 0.1572$$

**Step 4:** Calculate output voltage.

$$V_{out} = |H| \times V_{in} = 0.1572 \times 5 = 0.786 \, V$$

**Step 5:** Express in dB.

$$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.1572) \approx -16.07 \, dB$$

> [!TIP]
> Since $f = 10$ kHz is roughly one decade above $f_c \approx 1.59$ kHz, the attenuation should be approximately **-20 dB**. The calculated -16.07 dB is consistent because the frequency is only 6.3× above $f_c$, not exactly 10×.

#### **Example 3: Verify Cutoff Frequency Experimentally**

**Problem:** In a lab experiment, the input is 1 V peak sine wave. The measured output at $f_c$ is found to be **0.71 V**. Verify the cutoff frequency.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Theoretical ratio at cutoff.

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{0.71}{1.0} = 0.71 \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707$$

**Step 2:** Since the ratio matches the theoretical value within experimental tolerance (typically ±5%), the experimentally observed frequency IS the cutoff frequency.

**Step 3:** Express the relationship in dB.

$$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.71}{1.0}\right) = 20 \times (-0.2977) \approx -2.99 \, dB \approx -3 \, dB$$

This confirms the **-3 dB point** definition.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Experimental Setup Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram shows the complete experimental setup for testing RC filters in the laboratory, mapping signal flow from function generator through the filter circuit to the oscilloscope.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FGen["FUNCTION GENERATOR"] -->|"Vin Sine Wave 1Vpp"| CH1["OSCILLOSCOPE CH1"]
    FGen -->|"Input Signal"| Filter["RC FILTER CIRCUIT LPF OR HPF"]
    Filter -->|"Vout Filtered Signal"| CH2["OSCILLOSCOPE CH2"]
    Filter -->|"Ground Reference"| GND["COMMON GROUND"]
    
    style FGen fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Filter fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CH1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CH2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style GND fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 RC Low-Pass Filter Circuit Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vin["INPUT Vin"] --> R1["R = 1 kOhm"]
    R1 --> NodeA["OUTPUT NODE Vout"]
    NodeA --> C1["C = 0.1 uF"]
    C1 --> Gnd["GROUND"]
    NodeA --> Out["TO OSCILLOSCOPE CH2"]
    
    style Vin fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style NodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Gnd fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Out fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the cutoff frequency of an RC filter. Derive the expression for the cutoff frequency of a first-order RC low-pass filter.**

**Model Answer:**

The cutoff frequency ($f_c$) of an RC filter is defined as the frequency at which the output signal power drops to 50% of its maximum (input) value, equivalently where the output amplitude is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ times the input amplitude. It is also called the **-3 dB frequency** or **half-power frequency**.

**Derivation:** For an RC LPF, the transfer function is:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

The magnitude is $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$.

At the cutoff frequency, $\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \omega_c RC = 1$$

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \Rightarrow \boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \;\; \text{Hz}}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between a first-order RC low-pass filter and a high-pass filter. Mention one application for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Feature** | **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Output Element** | Across Capacitor (C) | Across Resistor (R) |
| **Passes** | Low frequencies | High frequencies |
| **Blocks** | High frequencies | Low frequencies and DC |
| **Roll-off** | -20 dB/decade above $f_c$ | -20 dB/decade below $f_c$ |
| **Phase** | Output lags (0° to -90°) | Output leads (+90° to 0°) |

**Applications:**
- **LPF:** Power supply ripple filter (smoothing rectifier output).
- **HPF:** Coupling capacitor in audio amplifiers (blocks DC offset).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a first-order RC low-pass filter. Derive its transfer function and plot the frequency response. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design an RC low-pass filter for a cutoff frequency of 10 kHz. Take $C = 0.01 \, \mu F$. Calculate the gain in dB at $f = 50$ kHz. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Working of RC Low-Pass Filter (7 Marks):**

**Circuit Diagram:** Input $V_{in}$ is applied to a series resistor R. The output $V_{out}$ is taken across the shunt capacitor C connected to ground. (Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram.)

**Working Principle:**
- At very low frequencies, the capacitor offers very high reactance ($X_C = 1/\omega C \to \infty$). Hence, almost the entire input voltage appears across the capacitor (output).
- At very high frequencies, $X_C \to 0$ (capacitor acts as a short circuit). The output is shorted to ground.
- At the cutoff frequency, $X_C = R$, and the output amplitude is 0.707 times the input.

**Transfer Function Derivation:**
Using voltage divider in phasor domain:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1/(j\omega C)}{R + 1/(j\omega C)} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Magnitude:** $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ | **Phase:** $\phi = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$

**Frequency Response (Bode Plot):**
- For $f \ll f_c$: Gain = 0 dB (flat)
- For $f = f_c$: Gain = -3 dB
- For $f \gg f_c$: Gain decreases linearly at -20 dB/decade

**(b) Design Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Find R for $f_c = 10$ kHz with $C = 0.01 \, \mu F$.

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1591.5 \, \Omega \approx 1.59 \, k\Omega$$

Use standard value **1.5 kΩ** or **1.6 kΩ**.

**Step 2:** Calculate $\omega RC$ at $f = 50$ kHz.

$$\omega RC = 2\pi \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 1591.5 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} = 2\pi \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 1.5915 \times 10^{-8} = 5.0$$

**Step 3:** Calculate magnitude of gain.

$$|H| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (5.0)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{26}} = \frac{1}{5.099} \approx 0.1961$$

**Step 4:** Convert to dB.

$$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.1961) = 20 \times (-0.7073) \approx -14.15 \, dB$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Many students forget to convert $0.01 \, \mu F$ to Farads correctly. The unit conversion gives $0.01 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-8}$ F. A wrong conversion (e.g., $0.01$ F) leads to a wildly wrong answer and loss of **3 marks**.
> 2. Forgetting the formula $20\log_{10}$ and using $10\log_{10}$ (power formula) is a common error causing loss of **2 marks**.
> 3. Not specifying the roll-off rate of **-20 dB/decade** in the Bode plot description leads to loss of **1 mark**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a first-order RC high-pass filter. Derive its transfer function and draw the frequency response showing the -3 dB point. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An RC high-pass filter has $R = 4.7 \, k\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \, \mu F$. Calculate (i) the cutoff frequency, (ii) the output voltage at 100 Hz when $V_{in} = 2$ V peak, and (iii) the output voltage at 10 kHz. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Working of RC High-Pass Filter (7 Marks):**

**Circuit Diagram:** Input is applied to a series capacitor C. Output is taken across a shunt resistor R to ground. (Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram.)

**Working Principle:**
- At DC and very low frequencies, $X_C \to \infty$, blocking the signal from reaching the output.
- At high frequencies, $X_C \to 0$, so the full input appears at the output.
- The HPF is essentially a **differentiator** for low-frequency sinusoidal input.

**Transfer Function:**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R}{R + 1/(j\omega C)} = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Frequency Response:**
- For $f \ll f_c$: Gain rises at +20 dB/decade slope
- For $f = f_c$: Gain = -3 dB
- For $f \gg f_c$: Gain = 0 dB (flat passband)

**Phase Response:** $\phi = +90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$, meaning the output **leads** the input between +90° and 0°.

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $R = 4.7 \, k\Omega = 4.7 \times 10^3 \, \Omega$, $C = 0.01 \, \mu F = 10^{-8}$ F, $V_{in} = 2$ V peak.

**(i) Cutoff Frequency:**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.7 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{2.953 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3386 \, Hz \approx 3.39 \, kHz$$

**(ii) Output Voltage at 100 Hz:**

First, calculate $\omega RC$ at 100 Hz:

$$\omega RC = 2\pi \times 100 \times 4.7 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} = 2.953 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00295$$

Now apply the HPF magnitude formula:

$$|H| = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} = \frac{0.00295}{\sqrt{1 + (0.00295)^2}} \approx \frac{0.00295}{1.0000044} \approx 0.00295$$

$$V_{out} = |H| \times V_{in} = 0.00295 \times 2 \approx 0.0059 \, V = 5.9 \, mV$$

(This is heavily attenuated because 100 Hz is well below $f_c = 3.39$ kHz.)

**(iii) Output Voltage at 10 kHz:**

$$\omega RC = 2\pi \times 10^4 \times 4.7 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} = 2.953$$

$$|H| = \frac{2.953}{\sqrt{1 + (2.953)^2}} = \frac{2.953}{\sqrt{9.72}} = \frac{2.953}{3.118} \approx 0.947$$

$$V_{out} = 0.947 \times 2 = 1.894 \, V \approx 1.89 \, V$$

(Since 10 kHz is well above $f_c = 3.39$ kHz, the output is close to the input.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning for HPF Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up LPF and HPF formulas** is the most common error. For HPF, the magnitude is $\dfrac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2}}$, NOT $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2}}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\omega RC$ factor in the numerator** of the HPF transfer function results in a complete loss of **4-5 marks** as the entire transfer function becomes wrong.
> 3. **Confusing LPF and HPF circuit diagrams** (R and C positions swapped) — a minor visual error but examiners may deduct **1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Common Viva-Voce Questions (Lab Exam Special)

| **Question** | **Expected Short Answer** |
|---|---|
| What is the significance of the -3 dB point? | The frequency at which output power is half the input power (or voltage is 0.707 times). |
| Why is the LPF called a "low-pass" filter? | It passes low frequencies and attenuates frequencies above $f_c$. |
| What happens to a DC signal applied to an HPF? | DC is blocked — output is zero (capacitor blocks DC). |
| Can an RC filter have infinite roll-off? | No, a first-order RC filter has only -20 dB/decade. Higher order filters are needed. |
| How do you measure the cutoff frequency experimentally? | Find the frequency where the output amplitude is 0.707 times the input amplitude. |
| What is the time constant $\tau$ of an RC circuit? | $\tau = RC$, measured in seconds. |
| What is the relationship between $\tau$ and $f_c$? | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau}$ |

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- [ ] **LPF Circuit:** Series R, Output across C. **HPF Circuit:** Series C, Output across R.
- [ ] **Cutoff Frequency Formula:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — SAME for both LPF and HPF.
- [ ] **Magnitude at Cutoff:** $V_{out} = 0.707 \, V_{in}$ (i.e., -3 dB).
- [ ] **Roll-off Rate:** **-20 dB/decade** for both filters (one above $f_c$ for LPF, one below $f_c$ for HPF).
- [ ] **LPF Transfer Function:** $H_{LPF}(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{1+j\omega RC}$
- [ ] **HPF Transfer Function:** $H_{HPF}(j\omega) = \dfrac{j\omega RC}{1+j\omega RC}$
- [ ] **Phase Shift:** LPF: $0°$ to $-90°$ (output lags); HPF: $+90°$ to $0°$ (output leads).
- [ ] **Standard Lab Values:** $R = 1$ kΩ, $C = 0.1$ µF → $f_c \approx 1.59$ kHz.
- [ ] **Bode Plot Axes:** dB on Y-axis (linear), Frequency on X-axis (logarithmic).
- [ ] **DC Behavior:** LPF passes DC; HPF blocks DC.
- [ ] **Time Constant:** $\tau = RC$ determines capacitor charging/discharging speed.
- [ ] **Key Term to Remember:** **-3 dB = Half Power = 0.707 in Voltage Ratio**.
- [ ] **Practical Tip:** In the lab, always use shielded cables and a common ground to avoid 50 Hz noise pickup while measuring low-amplitude signals.
- [ ] **Examiner's Favorite Question:** "Design a filter with $f_c = X$ kHz given C = Y µF" — solve for R using $R = 1/(2\pi f_c C)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RC coupled amplifier using BJT in CE configuration - Measurement of gain, BW and plotting of frequency response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL308 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** RC coupled amplifier using BJT in CE configuration - Measurement of gain, BW and plotting of frequency response # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **RC Coupled Amplifier using BJT in Common Emitter (CE) Configuration** is a small-signal voltage amplifier in which a Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) is biased in the active region with the input signal applied between the base and emitter, and the amplified output taken between the collector and emitter. **Resistance–Capacitance (RC) coupling** refers to the use of coupling capacitors ($C_{in}$, $C_{out}$) and an emitter bypass capacitor ($C_E$) to connect the source, the transistor stage, and the load, while blocking DC bias voltages between stages. The experiment aims to: 1. Design and wire a single-stage CE amplifier with proper biasing. 2. Measure the **midband voltage gain** ($A_{v,m}$) at a representative mid-frequency. 3. Determine the **lower cutoff frequency** ($f_L$) and **upper cutoff frequency** ($f_H$) where gain falls to **0.707** of its midband value (i.e., **−3 dB** point). 4. Compute **Bandwidth** $BW = f_H - f_L$. 5. Plot the **frequency response curve** (gain in dB vs. frequency on a semi-log scale). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The lab record must explicitly tabulate measured values, plot the Bode-magnitude plot, and compute the **bandwidth in Hz** and **midband gain in dB** for full credit in the continuous evaluation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of the RC coupled amplifier as a **chain of water buckets connected by narrow pipes**: - The **BJT** is the *pump* — a small trickle of water at the input becomes a strong stream at the output, but only while the pump is running at the right speed (proper bias). - The **Coupling Capacitors** ($C_{in}$, $C_{out}$) are like *one-way valves* — they let the AC "water" (signal) pass between buckets (stages) but block the DC "pressure" that would otherwise flood the next bucket and destroy the bias. - The **Emitter Bypass Capacitor** ($C_E$) is like *opening a relief valve* — at signal frequencies it short-circuits the emitter resistor, preventing the AC signal from being "wasted" as a negative feedback drop. - The **Frequency Response curve** is a plot of "how efficiently the pump delivers water" at different pumping speeds (frequencies). The pump works best in a *midband* and loses efficiency at very low or very high speeds. > [!NOTE] > At **very low frequencies**, the coupling capacitors behave like high impedances ($\frac{1}{\omega C} \rightarrow \infty$), so the signal is partially dropped across them. > At **very high frequencies**, internal transistor capacitances (like $C_{bc}$) shunt the signal away from the output — they behave like low impedances ($\frac{1}{\omega C} \rightarrow 0$). ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - Thermal voltage at room temperature (300 K): $V_T \approx \mathbf{26 \text{ mV}}$. - Midband gain measurement frequency (typical): $f_m = \mathbf{1 \text{ kHz}}$. - Cutoff definition: gain drops to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx \mathbf{0.707}$ of midband value. - Cutoff in decibels: $-\mathbf{3 \text{ dB}}$. - Standard input signal amplitude for testing: $V_{in} = \mathbf{10 \text{ mV}}_{p-p}$ to $\mathbf{50 \text{ mV}}_{p-p}$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The CE Amplifier Step-by-Step The RC coupled CE amplifier works through the following sequence of events: 1. **DC Biasing (via $V_{BB}$ and $R_B$ for base, $V_{CC}$ and $R_C$ for collector):** - The base–emitter junction is forward-biased; the base–collector junction is reverse-biased. - This sets the **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)** with collector current $I_C$ and collector–emitter voltage $V_{CE}$ in the active region. 2. **AC Signal Injection (via $C_{in}$):** - The small AC input signal $v_{in}$ rides on top of the DC bias at the base. - $C_{in}$ blocks any DC from the source from disturbing the bias. 3. **Signal Amplification (BJT action):** - A small change in base current $\Delta i_B$ produces a large change in collector current $\Delta i_C = \beta \cdot \Delta i_B$. - The collector current change flows through $R_C$, producing an amplified, **180° phase-inverted** voltage at the collector. 4. **AC Output Delivery (via $C_{out}$ to load $R_L$):** - The amplified signal at the collector passes through $C_{out}$ (blocking the DC $V_{CE}$) to the load $R_L$. - The effective AC load at the collector is $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$. 5. **Emitter Stabilization with Bypass ($R_E$ and $C_E$):** - $R_E$ provides DC thermal stability but would reduce AC gain; therefore $C_E$ shorts $R_E$ at signal frequencies (midband and above), preserving high gain. 6. **Frequency-Dependent Behavior:** - At **low frequencies** ($\omega$ small): $\vert Z_C \vert = \frac{1}{\omega C}$ is large → signal drops across capacitors → gain falls. - At **midband frequencies**: capacitors behave as shorts; $C_{bc}$/$C_{be}$ are opens → gain is constant and maximum. - At **high frequencies** ($\omega$ large): transistor's internal capacitances ($C_{bc}$, $C_{be}$) shunt the signal → gain falls. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Midband Voltage Gain (CE) | $A_{v,m} = -g_m \,(R_C \parallel R_L)$ | dimensionless | Negative sign → 180° phase shift | | 2 | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | Siemens (A/V) | $V_T \approx 26$ mV at 300 K | | 3 | Current Gain Relation | $g_m = \dfrac{\beta}{r_\pi}$ | Siemens | $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$ | | 4 | Input Resistance (BJT) | $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Small-signal parameter | | 5 | AC Load at Collector | $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L = \dfrac{R_C \, R_L}{R_C + R_L}$ | Ohms | Effective load for AC | | 6 | Gain in Decibels | $A_{v(dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ | dB | Standard for Bode plots | | 7 | Lower Cutoff (input coupling) | $f_{L1} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi \,(R_S + r_\pi)\, C_{in}}$ | Hz | Due to $C_{in}$ | | 8 | Lower Cutoff (output coupling) | $f_{L2} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi \,(R_C \parallel R_L)\, C_{out}}$ | Hz | Due to $C_{out}$ | | 9 | Lower Cutoff (emitter bypass) | $f_{LE} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi \, R_E \, C_E}$ | Hz | Should be $\ll f_m$ | | 10 | Overall Lower Cutoff | $f_L \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{LE}^2}$ | Hz | Dominant pole approximation | | 11 | Upper Cutoff (Miller) | $f_H \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi \, R_{eq} \, C_{in,eff}}$ | Hz | $C_{in,eff}$ includes Miller effect | | 12 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | Hz | Flat-top width of response | | 13 | Q-point Collector Current | $I_C = \beta \, I_B$ | A | Set by bias network | | 14 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBP = \vert A_{v,m} \vert \times BW$ | Hz | Often approximately constant | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note on dB:** The midband gain is ALWAYS a positive dB value (we take magnitude). The negative sign in $A_{v,m}$ indicates phase inversion only — it does NOT make the gain negative in dB. > $$\boxed{A_{v(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) \right\vert = 20 \log_{10}\left[ g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) \right]}$$ ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Audio amplifiers**: Multi-stage RC coupled CE amplifiers are the *backbone* of audio pre-amplifiers and public address systems because they can be cascaded with each stage isolated by capacitors. - **Communication receivers**: IF (Intermediate Frequency) amplifier stages in superheterodyne receivers often use RC coupled CE stages for their wide bandwidth. - **Sensor signal conditioning**: Small signals from sensors (microphones, piezoelectric pickups, photodiodes with transimpedance) need precisely this kind of low-noise, moderate-gain amplification. - **Oscilloscope vertical amplifiers**: Use RC coupled broadband stages to faithfully display waveforms across decades of frequency. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Small-Signal AC Equivalent of the CE Stage The full amplifier reduces to the small-signal hybrid-π model at midband: $$ v_{in} \xrightarrow{C_{in}\ (\text{short})} v_{be} \longrightarrow \boxed{g_m v_{be}\ (\text{current source})} \longrightarrow (R_C \parallel R_L) \xrightarrow{C_{out}\ (\text{short})} v_{out} $$ **Key transition:** Because $C_E$ is a short at midband, the emitter is grounded for AC, so the emitter resistor $R_E$ disappears from the AC picture. ## 3.2 Derivation of Midband Voltage Gain Starting from the small-signal collector current: $$ i_c = g_m \, v_{be} $$ The output voltage across the parallel combination $R_C \parallel R_L$: $$ v_{out} = -i_c \, (R_C \parallel R_L) = -g_m \, v_{be} \, (R_C \parallel R_L) $$ Since $v_{in} = v_{be}$ (the input coupling capacitor is a short and $r_\pi$ draws negligible voltage from an ideal source): $$ A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{-g_m \, v_{be} \, (R_C \parallel R_L)}{v_{be}} $$ $$ \boxed{A_{v,m} = -g_m \, (R_C \parallel R_L)} $$ **In dB form:** $$ \boxed{A_{v,m(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left[ g_m \, (R_C \parallel R_L) \right]} $$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Problem) **Given:** - $V_{CC} = 12$ V, $V_{EE} = 0$ V (single supply) - $R_B = 470$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 2.2$ k$\Omega$, $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$ - $R_L = 4.7$ k$\Omega$ (load), $C_{in} = C_{out} = 10$ $\mu$F, $C_E = 100$ $\mu$F - BJT: $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE(on)} = 0.7$ V - Midband test frequency: $f_m = 1$ kHz, $V_{in} = 20$ mV$_{p-p}$ ### Step 1: Compute Q-point Base Current Apply KVL on base–emitter loop: $$ V_{CC} = I_B R_B + V_{BE} + I_E R_E $$ With $I_E \approx I_B (1 + \beta) \approx 101 \, I_B$: $$ 12 = I_B (470\,\text{k}) + 0.7 + (101 \cdot I_B)(1\,\text{k}) $$ $$ 12 - 0.7 = I_B [470{,}000 + 101{,}000] $$ $$ 11.3 = I_B \cdot 571{,}000 $$ $$ \boxed{I_B = 19.79\ \mu\text{A}} $$ ### Step 2: Compute Q-point Collector Current $$ I_C = \beta \cdot I_B = 100 \cdot 19.79\ \mu\text{A} = \boxed{1.979\ \text{mA}} $$ ### Step 3: Compute $V_{CE}$ (verify active region) $$ V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E \approx V_{CC} - I_C (R_C + R_E) $$ $$ V_{CE} = 12 - (1.979\,\text{mA})(2.2\,\text{k}\Omega + 1\,\text{k}\Omega) $$ $$ V_{CE} = 12 - (1.979\,\text{mA})(3.2\,\text{k}\Omega) = 12 - 6.33 = \boxed{5.67\ \text{V}} $$ This is well-centered in the active region (between 0 V and 12 V), confirming good bias design. ### Step 4: Compute Transconductance $g_m$ $$ g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1.979 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = \boxed{0.0761\ \text{S}} = 76.1\ \text{mS} $$ ### Step 5: Compute AC Load Resistance $$ R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{(2.2\,\text{k})(4.7\,\text{k})}{2.2\,\text{k} + 4.7\,\text{k}} $$ $$ R_{ac} = \frac{10{,}340}{6{,}900} = \boxed{1.499\ \text{k}\Omega} $$ ### Step 6: Compute Midband Voltage Gain (Analytical) $$ A_{v,m} = -g_m \cdot R_{ac} = -(0.0761)(1{,}499) = \boxed{-114.0} $$ $$ A_{v,m(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(114.0) = \boxed{41.14\ \text{dB}} $$ ### Step 7: Predict Output Voltage (Analytical) $$ V_{out,p-p} = \vert A_{v,m} \vert \cdot V_{in,p-p} = 114 \cdot 20\ \text{mV} = \boxed{2.28\ \text{V}_{p-p}} $$ > [!NOTE] > This output must be less than the unclipped swing limit. The maximum unclipped output swing is roughly $2 \times \min(V_{CE} - V_{CE(sat)}, V_{CC} - V_{CE})$. With $V_{CE} = 5.67$ V, the available swing on one side is $\approx 5$ V. Our $2.28$ V$_{p-p}$ output is well within safe limits. ### Step 8: Compute Lower Cutoff Frequency For the dominant output coupling capacitor: $$ f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{ac} \, C_{out}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (1499)(10 \times 10^{-6})} $$ $$ f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (1499)(10^{-5})} = \frac{1}{0.09421} = \boxed{10.62\ \text{Hz}} $$ For the emitter bypass capacitor: $$ f_{LE} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_E \, C_E} = \frac{1}{2\pi (1000)(100 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{0.6283} = \boxed{1.59\ \text{Hz}} $$ Since $f_{L2} \gg f_{LE}$, $f_{L2}$ is the **dominant lower cutoff**. The overall $f_L \approx \boxed{12\text{–}15\ \text{Hz}}$ (depending on summing the contributions). ### Step 9: Bandwidth Assuming $f_H \approx 1$ MHz (typical for a general-purpose BJT like BC107/2N2222A): $$ BW = f_H - f_L \approx 1{,}000{,}000 - 15 \approx \boxed{0.999985\ \text{MHz} \approx 1\ \text{MHz}} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How the Capacitors Set the Cutoffs | Capacitor | Role at Low $f$ | Role at Midband | Role at High $f$ | Sets | |----------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------|------| | $C_{in}$ | High impedance → drop | Short circuit | Short circuit | $f_{L1}$ | | $C_{out}$ | High impedance → drop | Short circuit | Short circuit | $f_{L2}$ | | $C_E$ | High impedance → drop | Short circuit (removes $R_E$ from AC) | Short circuit | $f_{LE}$ | | $C_{be}$, $C_{bc}$ | Open circuit | Open circuit | Low impedance → shunt | $f_H$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Complete RC-Coupled CE Amplifier Circuit (Schematic Block Flow) ```mermaid graph TD source[VS: Signal Generator
Vin = 10 to 50 mVpp] --> Cin Cin[Coupling Capacitor Cin
10 uF] --> nodeBase nodeBase[Base Node B
DC Bias from RB] RB[Bias Resistor RB
470 kohm to VCC] -.DC bias.-> nodeBase nodeBase --> bjtB bjtB[(BJT BC107
or 2N2222A)] bjtB --> bjtE[Emitter] bjtB --> bjtC[Collector Node C] RC[Collector Resistor RC
2.2 kohm to VCC] --> bjtC bjtE --> RE[Emitter Resistor RE
1 kohm to GND] CE[Bypass Capacitor CE
100 uF] -.parallel to RE.-> RE bjtC --> Cout Cout[Coupling Capacitor Cout
10 uF] --> nodeOut nodeOut[Output Node] --> RL[Load Resistor RL
4.7 kohm to GND] nodeOut --> cro[Oscilloscope CH2
Measure Vout pp] source -.Input probe.-> cro1[Oscilloscope CH1
Measure Vin pp] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Midband vs. Low vs. High Frequency) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph LOW_F[LOW FREQUENCY REGION] LF1[Cin acts as
HIGH Z drop]:::warn LF2[Cout acts as
HIGH Z drop]:::warn LF3[CE acts as
HIGH Z drop]:::warn end subgraph MID_F[MIDBAND REGION] MF1[Cin = short]:::ok MF2[Cout = short]:::ok MF3[CE = short
RE bypassed]:::ok MF4[Av = max
flat region]:::ok end subgraph HIGH_F[HIGH FREQUENCY REGION] HF1[Cbc Miller
shunts output]:::warn HF2[Cbe shunts
input signal]:::warn end IN[Input Signal] --> LOW_F --> MID_F --> HIGH_F --> OUT[Output Signal] ``` ```mermaid %%{init: {'theme':'base'}}%% xychart-beta title "Typical Frequency Response of RC Coupled CE Amplifier" x-axis "Frequency (Hz) [log scale]" [10 100 1000 10000 100000 1000000 10000000] y-axis "Gain (dB)" 0 --> 50 line [5 15 35 41 41 41 38 30 10] ``` ## 4.3 Tabular Measurement Format (For Lab Record) | Sl. No. | Frequency $f$ (Hz) | $V_{in}$ (V$_{p-p}$) | $V_{out}$ (V$_{p-p}$) | $A_v = V_{out}/V_{in}$ | Gain (dB) $20\log_{10}\vert A_v\vert$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 10 | 0.020 | 0.040 | 2.00 | 6.02 | | 2 | 50 | 0.020 | 0.380 | 19.0 | 25.6 | | 3 | 100 | 0.020 | 0.900 | 45.0 | 33.1 | | 4 | 1 000 | 0.020 | 2.280 | 114.0 | **41.14 (mid)** | | 5 | 10 000 | 0.020 | 2.260 | 113.0 | 41.06 | | 6 | 100 000 | 0.020 | 1.800 | 90.0 | 39.1 | | 7 | 1 000 000 | 0.020 | 0.220 | 11.0 | 20.8 | - **Midband gain**: 41.14 dB at $f_m = 1$ kHz - **0.707 × midband** $V_{out}$: $0.707 \times 2.28 = 1.612$ V$_{p-p}$ - From the table: $V_{out} \approx 1.612$ V$_{p-p}$ at $f \approx 50$ Hz → **$f_L \approx 50$ Hz** - $V_{out} \approx 1.612$ V$_{p-p}$ at $f \approx 300$ kHz → **$f_H \approx 300$ kHz** - **Bandwidth** $= f_H - f_L = 300{,}000 - 50 \approx \mathbf{299{,}950\ Hz \approx 300\ kHz}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plotting Tip for KTU Lab Record:** Use a **semi-log graph** — frequency on the log-scale x-axis, gain in dB on the linear y-axis. Mark $f_L$ and $f_H$ with dotted vertical lines and label the **−3 dB bandwidth** explicitly between them. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define midband gain of an RC coupled amplifier. Why is the gain considered constant in the midband region?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The midband gain $A_{v,m}$ of an RC coupled CE amplifier is the constant voltage gain observed over the **flat central region** of the frequency response, where all coupling and bypass capacitors behave as short circuits and internal transistor capacitances behave as open circuits. It is given by: $$ A_{v,m} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) $$ In this region, the reactive impedances of $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, and $C_E$ are negligibly small compared to the resistive impedances they are in series with, while $C_{bc}$ and $C_{be}$ have such high reactance that they do not load the circuit. Hence, the gain becomes **independent of frequency** and is determined purely by the resistive components. **(3 marks)** ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **What is the significance of the −3 dB frequency in an amplifier's frequency response?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The −3 dB frequencies are the **lower cutoff** ($f_L$) and **upper cutoff** ($f_H$) at which the amplifier's voltage gain falls to **0.707 (i.e., $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$)** of its midband value. In decibel terms, this corresponds to a drop of **3 dB** below the midband gain level. These two frequencies define the **usable bandwidth** of the amplifier: $$ BW = f_H - f_L $$ The −3 dB points are significant because they represent the **half-power frequencies** — at these frequencies, the output power delivered to the load drops to exactly **half** of the midband power. The band between $f_L$ and $f_H$ is the range over which the amplifier amplifies faithfully. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme] **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier using BJT. Identify the role of each coupling and bypass capacitor. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For an RC coupled CE amplifier with $\beta = 100$, $I_C = 2$ mA, $V_T = 26$ mV, $R_C = 2.2$ k$\Omega$, $R_L = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, compute (i) the transconductance $g_m$, (ii) the midband voltage gain $A_{v,m}$, and (iii) the midband gain in dB. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A **(a) Circuit and Operation (7 marks)** The single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier consists of: - **BJT** (Q-point in active region): Input applied between base and emitter, output taken from collector. - **Bias resistor $R_B$**: Provides base bias current $I_B = (V_{CC} - V_{BE})/R_B$. - **Collector resistor $R_C$**: Converts collector current variations into voltage variations; DC load. - **Emitter resistor $R_E$**: Provides DC thermal stability. - **Input coupling capacitor $C_{in}$**: Blocks DC from the source, allows AC to reach the base. Sets $f_{L1}$. - **Output coupling capacitor $C_{out}$**: Blocks DC collector voltage, passes amplified AC to load. Sets $f_{L2}$. - **Emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$**: AC-short-circuits $R_E$ at signal frequencies, preventing AC negative feedback. Sets $f_{LE}$. **Operation:** The small AC input $v_{in}$ injected via $C_{in}$ modulates the base–emitter voltage around the Q-point. This modulates the collector current as $i_c = g_m v_{be}$, and the resulting voltage drop across $R_C \parallel R_L$ produces an amplified, 180° phase-inverted output at the collector, which is delivered to the load through $C_{out}$. **(7 marks)** **(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)** **(i) Transconductance:** $$ g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = \boxed{0.0769\ \text{S}} = 76.9\ \text{mS} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} $$ **(ii) Midband voltage gain:** AC load resistance: $$ R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{(2200)(4700)}{2200 + 4700} = \frac{10{,}340{,}000}{6900} = 1498.55\ \Omega \quad \textbf{(1 mark)} $$ Midband gain: $$ A_{v,m} = -g_m \cdot R_{ac} = -(0.0769)(1498.55) = \boxed{-115.27} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} $$ **(iii) Gain in decibels:** $$ A_{v,m(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\vert -115.27\vert = 20 \log_{10}(115.27) $$ $$ \log_{10}(115.27) = 2.0617 $$ $$ \boxed{A_{v,m(dB)} = 20 \times 2.0617 = 41.23\ \text{dB}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} $$ --- ### Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Define the lower cutoff frequency $f_L$ and upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ of an amplifier. Derive an expression for $f_L$ due to the output coupling capacitor in an RC coupled CE amplifier. **(7 marks)** **(b)** During a lab experiment on an RC coupled CE amplifier, the following readings were obtained with $V_{in} = 20$ mV$_{p-p}$. Plot (describe) the frequency response and determine $f_L$, $f_H$, midband gain, and bandwidth. | $f$ (Hz) | 50 | 100 | 500 | 1 k | 10 k | 50 k | 100 k | 500 k | 1 M | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $V_{out}$ (mV$_{p-p}$) | 250 | 1200 | 2200 | 2280 | 2270 | 2150 | 1900 | 700 | 250 | **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B **(a) Cutoff definitions and derivation (7 marks)** **Definitions:** - **Lower cutoff frequency $f_L$:** The frequency below which the gain falls below 0.707 (i.e., −3 dB) of the midband value. Set by the coupling and bypass capacitors. **(2 marks)** - **Upper cutoff frequency $f_H$:** The frequency above which the gain falls below 0.707 of the midband value. Set by the internal transistor capacitances $C_{be}$, $C_{bc}$. **(2 marks)** **Derivation of $f_L$ due to $C_{out}$:** The output coupling capacitor $C_{out}$ is in series with the parallel combination $R_C \parallel R_L$ as seen by the load. At low frequencies, $C_{out}$ introduces a series impedance $\frac{1}{j\omega C_{out}}$. The voltage transfer to the load is: $$ \frac{V_{out}}{V_c} = \frac{R_L}{R_L + \frac{1}{j\omega C_{out}} + R_C} $$ Simplifying (treating $R_L$ in series with the impedance of $C_{out}$ and the rest of the circuit represented by the Thévenin resistance $R_C$): $$ \frac{V_{out}}{V_c} = \frac{R_L}{R_L + R_C + \frac{1}{j\omega C_{out}}} $$ The cutoff is when $\vert X_C \vert = R_C \parallel R_L$: $$ \frac{1}{\omega_L C_{out}} = R_C \parallel R_L $$ Solving for $f_L$: $$ \boxed{f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C \parallel R_L) C_{out}}} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)} $$ **(b) Experimental analysis (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Compute gain in dB for each frequency:** $$ A_v = V_{out}/V_{in}, \quad V_{in} = 20\ \text{mV}_{p-p} $$ | $f$ (Hz) | $V_{out}$ (mV$_{p-p}$) | $A_v$ | Gain (dB) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 50 | 250 | 12.50 | 21.94 | | 100 | 1200 | 60.00 | 35.56 | | 500 | 2200 | 110.00 | 40.83 | | **1 000** | **2 280** | **114.00** | **41.14 (mid)** | | 10 000 | 2270 | 113.50 | 41.10 | | 50 000 | 2150 | 107.50 | 40.63 | | 100 000 | 1900 | 95.00 | 39.55 | | 500 000 | 700 | 35.00 | 30.88 | | 1 000 000 | 250 | 12.50 | 21.94 | **Step 2 — Identify midband:** Constant gain region from 500 Hz to 10 kHz. **Midband gain = 41.14 dB at $f_m = 1$ kHz. (1 mark)** **Step 3 — Determine $f_L$ and $f_H$:** 0.707 × midband $V_{out}$ = 0.707 × 2280 = **1611.96 mV$_{p-p}$** - From table: $V_{out} = 1612$ mV$_{p-p}$ at $f \approx 130$ Hz (interpolating between 100 Hz and 500 Hz) → **$f_L \approx 130$ Hz (1.5 marks)** - $V_{out} = 1612$ mV$_{p-p}$ at $f \approx 320$ kHz (interpolating between 100 kHz and 500 kHz) → **$f_H \approx 320$ kHz (1.5 marks)** **Step 4 — Bandwidth:** $$ BW = f_H - f_L = 320{,}000 - 130 = \boxed{319{,}870\ \text{Hz} \approx 320\ \text{kHz}} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)} $$ **Step 5 — Frequency response sketch:** Plot is **flat-topped**, peaking at 41.14 dB between 500 Hz and 10 kHz, falling off as 20 dB/decade on both sides (high-pass on left, low-pass on right). **(2 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > 1. **Sign in dB mistake:** Students often write $A_{v(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(-115)$ — this is a fatal error. Always take the **magnitude** before applying the logarithm: $20 \log_{10}\vert -115 \vert = 20 \log_{10}(115)$. > > 2. **Forgetting the bypass capacitor role:** Many students explain that $C_E$ "increases the gain" — actually $C_E$ **prevents the gain from being reduced** at midband by short-circuiting $R_E$ at signal frequencies. Without $C_E$, $A_v$ would be approximately $-g_m R_C / (1 + g_m R_E)$, much smaller. > > 3. **Confusing $f_L$ and $f_H$:** $f_L$ is on the **low-frequency side** (capacitors short-circuiting too little), $f_H$ is on the **high-frequency side** (transistor internal capacitors shunting). > > 4. **Ignoring DC offset on output:** Always measure $V_{out(p-p)}$ with the oscilloscope in **AC-coupled mode**, otherwise the DC bias at the collector will appear as an offset and you'll read wrong peak-to-peak values. > > 5. **Not verifying the active region:** If $V_{CE(Q)}$ is too close to 0 V or $V_{CC}$, the output waveform will be clipped — the gain measurement will be wrong. Always check the Q-point first. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **CE Configuration**: Input at base–emitter, output at collector–emitter, common emitter terminal; provides **voltage gain** with **180° phase inversion**. - **RC coupling** uses capacitors to pass AC signals between stages while blocking DC bias voltages. - **Three coupling/bypass capacitors** set the low-frequency response: $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, $C_E$. - **Transistor internal capacitances** ($C_{be}$, $C_{bc}$) set the high-frequency response. - **Midband gain formula**: $A_{v,m} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$. - **Transconductance**: $g_m = I_C / V_T \approx I_C / 26\text{ mV}$. - **Gain in dB**: $A_{v(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_v \vert$ — always take magnitude first. - **−3 dB points** = **0.707 × midband voltage** = **half-power frequencies**. - **Bandwidth**: $BW = f_H - f_L$. - **Frequency response plot**: Semi-log, with 20 dB/decade roll-off on each side. - **Bypass capacitor $C_E$** is essential for high midband gain; without it, gain drops by factor $(1 + g_m R_E)$. - **Q-point must be in active region** for linear amplification; typically $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}/2$. - **Input signal must be small** (a few tens of mV) to keep the BJT in the linear small-signal regime. - **Output is 180° out of phase** with input — verify on oscilloscope using dual-trace. - **GBP (Gain-Bandwidth Product)** is approximately constant for a given transistor — increasing $R_C$ increases gain but reduces $f_H$ (Miller effect). - **Miller's theorem**: $C_{bc}$ appears as $(1 + \vert A_v \vert) C_{bc}$ at the input, drastically reducing $f_H$.

topics.title: Emitter Follower Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL308 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Emitter Follower Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Emitter Follower Amplifier** is a single-stage **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)** amplifier configured in the **Common Collector (CC)** topology, in which the input signal is applied to the **base**, the output is taken from the **emitter**, and the **collector** is tied directly to the supply voltage $V_{CC}$ (acting as a common terminal for both input and output AC signals).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCEEL308 - Module 2):**
> *"The Emitter Follower (also called a Common Collector amplifier) is a unity-voltage-gain buffer stage whose output voltage at the emitter 'follows' the base input voltage with a constant base-emitter offset of approximately 0.7 V. It exhibits high input impedance, low output impedance, and is primarily used for impedance matching between a high-impedance source and a low-impedance load."*

The amplifier derives its name from the fact that the **emitter voltage $V_E$ tracks (follows) the base voltage $V_B$**, displaced only by the constant junction drop $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$.

$$V_E = V_B - V_{BE}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Voltage Echo" with Strength:**
> 
> Imagine a small water tap (high-impedance source) trying to fill a large bucket (low-impedance load) — the water flow is weak and the bucket fills slowly. Now, place a **booster pump** (the Emitter Follower) between them. The booster doesn't change the water **pressure level** (voltage), but it dramatically multiplies the **flow capacity** (current). Whatever pressure comes in, an almost identical pressure comes out — but now with much more "muscle" behind it.
> 
> The base is the **control input** (the controller sets the pressure level), and the emitter delivers the **same pressure level** to the load, but with far greater current capability.

### Key Functional Characteristics (Lab-Ready View)

| Property | Emitter Follower (CC) | Standard CE Amplifier |
|----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Voltage Gain $A_v$ | $\approx 0.99$ (Unity) | Large (50–500) |
| Current Gain $A_i$ | High $(\beta+1)$ | Moderate $(\beta)$ |
| Power Gain | Moderate | High |
| Input Impedance $Z_{in}$ | **Very High** (kΩ–MΩ) | Low–Moderate |
| Output Impedance $Z_{out}$ | **Very Low** (few Ω–tens of Ω) | High (kΩ) |
| Phase Inversion | **None** (In-phase) | 180° Inverted |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam:** *"Emitter Follower = EF = **E**cho **F**ollower = output follows input (minus 0.7 V)."*

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{26 \text{ mV}}$ at $T = 300 \text{ K}$
- **Base–Emitter Junction Drop:** $V_{BE} \approx \mathbf{0.7 \text{ V}}$ (silicon, active region)
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
- **Electronic Charge:** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
- **Standard Lab BJT:** 2N2222 / BC547 (NPN, $\beta \approx 100$–300)
- **Standard Supply:** $V_{CC} = +12 \text{ V}$ (typical KTU lab)
- **Function Generator Frequency:** $f = 1 \text{ kHz}$ (standard test tone)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The Emitter Follower operates on the principle of **100% series-voltage negative feedback**, applied internally through the emitter resistor $R_E$. The following structured logic explains *why* and *how* it works:

### Step 1: DC Biasing (Q-Point Establishment)
- The base is biased through the voltage divider $R_1$–$R_2$ from $V_{CC}$.
- The emitter sits at $V_E = V_B - V_{BE} \approx V_B - 0.7 \text{ V}$.
- The quiescent emitter current is set by $R_E$:

$$I_E = \frac{V_E - 0}{R_E} = \frac{V_B - 0.7}{R_E}$$

- The collector sits at $V_C = V_{CC}$ (since $R_C$ is absent — collector is directly tied to $V_{CC}$).

### Step 2: Small-Signal AC Operation
- A small AC signal $v_s$ is applied at the base.
- The base-emitter junction sees a varying voltage $v_{be}$, modulating the emitter current.
- Because $R_E$ converts this current into a voltage, the emitter voltage $v_e$ closely tracks $v_b$.

### Step 3: Why Voltage Gain ≈ 1?
- The voltage gain from base to emitter is:

$$A_{vb} = \frac{v_e}{v_b} = \frac{(\beta+1)(R_E \parallel R_L)}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)(R_E \parallel R_L)}$$

- Since $r_\pi$ (a few hundred Ω) is **much smaller** than $\beta \cdot (R_E \parallel R_L)$ (typically tens of kΩ):

$$A_{vb} \approx 1 \quad \text{(slightly less than unity)}$$

### Step 4: Current Gain — The Real "Power" of the Emitter Follower
- Although voltage gain is ~1, the **current gain** is approximately $(\beta+1)$.
- This means a small base current is amplified into a much larger emitter current — the load gets a "boosted" version of the input signal.

### Step 5: Impedance Transformation
- The high base impedance is reflected to the input as a very high $Z_{in}$.
- The low emitter impedance is seen by the load as a very low $Z_{out}$.
- This **impedance bridging** is the primary engineering utility.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **exam-critical formulas**. Memorize these — they form the backbone of any Emitter Follower problem in KTU lab examinations.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / Conditions |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|--------------------|
| 1 | Quiescent Emitter Voltage | $V_E = V_B - V_{BE}$ | V | $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V (Si) |
| 2 | DC Emitter Current | $I_E = \dfrac{V_E}{R_E}$ | mA | For $V_E \gg V_{BE}$ |
| 3 | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} \approx \dfrac{I_E}{26 \text{ mV}}$ | S or A/V | Depends on Q-point |
| 4 | Base–Emitter Resistance | $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$ | Ω | Small-signal parameter |
| 5 | Voltage Gain (Base to Emitter) | $A_{vb} = \dfrac{(\beta+1)(R_E \parallel R_L)}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)(R_E \parallel R_L)}$ | dimensionless | Typically 0.95–0.99 |
| 6 | Overall Voltage Gain (Source to Load) | $A_v = A_{vb} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + R_s}$ | dimensionless | Includes source loading |
| 7 | Input Impedance | $Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel [r_\pi + (\beta+1)(R_E \parallel R_L)]$ | Ω | Very high (kΩ–MΩ) |
| 8 | Output Impedance | $Z_{out} = R_E \parallel \left[ \dfrac{r_\pi + (R_s \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2)}{\beta+1} \right]$ | Ω | Very low (few Ω) |
| 9 | Lower Cut-off Frequency | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{eq,C} \cdot C_{eq})}$ | Hz | Dominant coupling/bypass cap |
| 10 | Power Gain | $G_p \approx 10 \log_{10}(\beta+1)^2$ | dB | Approximation, ideal case |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Specific Reminder:** The vertical bar `$\vert$` is used (not `|`) for parallel combinations to prevent LaTeX table conflicts.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Emitter Follower is **the workhorse of analog signal conditioning** and is used in virtually every commercial electronic system:

1. **Audio Equipment:** Output buffer between pre-amplifier (high $Z_{out}$) and speaker driver (low $Z_{in}$).
2. **Oscilloscope Probes (×10):** Uses a follower-like architecture to minimize circuit loading.
3. **Cable Drivers / Line Drivers:** Drives long cables with minimal signal loss.
4. **Op-Amp Internal Stages:** Many op-amp output stages use a complementary pair of emitter followers (push-pull).
5. **RF Front-Ends:** Impedance matching between a high-impedance antenna and a low-impedance LNA.
6. **Power Supply Regulation:** The classic **7805 linear regulator** uses a Darlington-pair emitter follower for voltage regulation.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question mentions *"impedance matching,"* *"buffer stage,"* or *"driving a low-impedance load from a high-impedance source,"* — the answer is the **Emitter Follower**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Small-Signal Voltage Gain $A_{vb}$

### Given Circuit
A BJT with base at AC potential $v_b$, emitter connected to $R_E \parallel R_L$ (called $R_{E,\text{eff}}$), collector AC-grounded to $V_{CC}$.

### Step 1 — Hybrid-π Small-Signal Model
Replace the BJT with its hybrid-π equivalent: a resistance $r_\pi$ between base and emitter, a current source $g_m v_{be}$ from collector to emitter, and the emitter is the output node.

The current source at the collector does not flow into the output (since the collector is at AC ground), so the **relevant loop** is base → $r_\pi$ → emitter → $R_{E,\text{eff}}$ → ground.

### Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Emitter Node
The current through $r_\pi$ equals the current through $R_{E,\text{eff}}$:

$$\frac{v_b - v_e}{r_\pi} = \frac{v_e}{R_{E,\text{eff}}}$$

### Step 3 — Algebraic Manipulation (Solving for $v_e / v_b$)
Cross-multiply the equation:

$$(v_b - v_e) \cdot R_{E,\text{eff}} = v_e \cdot r_\pi$$

Expand the left-hand side:

$$v_b \cdot R_{E,\text{eff}} - v_e \cdot R_{E,\text{eff}} = v_e \cdot r_\pi$$

Collect $v_e$ terms on one side:

$$v_b \cdot R_{E,\text{eff}} = v_e \cdot r_\pi + v_e \cdot R_{E,\text{eff}} = v_e (r_\pi + R_{E,\text{eff}})$$

Solve for the ratio:

$$\frac{v_e}{v_b} = \frac{R_{E,\text{eff}}}{r_\pi + R_{E,\text{eff}}}$$

### Step 4 — Account for the $(\beta+1)$ Effect on $r_\pi$
When emitter current $i_e = (\beta+1) i_b$ flows, the effective resistance seen at the base is **multiplied by $(\beta+1)$**. So we substitute:

$$R_{E,\text{eff (reflected to base)}} = (\beta+1) R_{E,\text{eff}}$$

This gives the final, exam-standard formula:

$$A_{vb} = \frac{v_e}{v_b} = \frac{(\beta+1)(R_E \parallel R_L)}{r_\pi + (\beta+1)(R_E \parallel R_L)}$$

### Step 5 — Limit Analysis (Sanity Check)
- If $R_E \parallel R_L \to \infty$: $A_{vb} \to 1$ ✓ (pure follower, no loading)
- If $R_E \parallel R_L \to 0$: $A_{vb} \to 0$ (output shorted) ✓
- Typical values: $r_\pi \approx 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $(\beta+1) R_{E,\text{eff}} \approx 50 \text{ k}\Omega$ → $A_{vb} \approx 0.98$ ✓

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Input Impedance $Z_{in}$

### Step 1 — Look Into the Base
The resistance seen looking into the base (without bias resistors) is:

$$Z_{b,\text{look-in}} = \frac{v_b}{i_b} = r_\pi + (\beta+1)(R_E \parallel R_L)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $(\beta+1)$?** The emitter current is $(\beta+1)$ times the base current, so the voltage drop across $R_E$ is $(\beta+1)$ times larger than what $i_b$ alone would produce. The base therefore "sees" the emitter resistance multiplied by $(\beta+1)$.

### Step 2 — Combine with Bias Network
The total input impedance is the bias divider in parallel with the base-look-in resistance:

$$Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel [r_\pi + (\beta+1)(R_E \parallel R_L)]$$

### Step 3 — Typical Numerical Evaluation
Assume: $R_1 = 47 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $R_E = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $I_C = 1 \text{ mA}$.

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} \approx 38.5 \text{ mA/V}$$

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.0385} \approx 2.6 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_E \parallel R_L = 1\text{k} \parallel 1\text{k} = 0.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$(\beta+1)(R_E \parallel R_L) = 101 \times 500 = 50.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$Z_{b,\text{look-in}} = 2.6\text{k} + 50.5\text{k} = 53.1 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{47 \times 10}{47 + 10} = \frac{470}{57} \approx 8.25 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$Z_{in} = 8.25\text{k} \parallel 53.1\text{k} = \frac{8.25 \times 53.1}{8.25 + 53.1} = \frac{438.1}{61.35} \approx 7.14 \text{ k}\Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> The bias resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ **dominate** $Z_{in}$ here. To increase $Z_{in}$, use a **bootstrap** technique or a **Darlington configuration** (which squares the $\beta$ effect).

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Output Impedance $Z_{out}$

### Step 1 — Concept
The output impedance is found by setting the source $v_s = 0$ and applying a test voltage $v_x$ at the output (emitter), then measuring the current $i_x$ drawn from it. $Z_{out} = v_x / i_x$.

### Step 2 — KCL at the Emitter
With $v_s = 0$, the base is at AC ground. The test current splits between $R_E$ and the BJT path:

$$i_x = \frac{v_x}{R_E} + \text{(current into emitter of BJT)}$$

### Step 3 — BJT Path Current
The base-emitter voltage is $v_{be} = -v_x$ (since base is grounded, emitter is at $v_x$). The base current is:

$$i_b = \frac{v_{be}}{r_\pi + (R_s \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2)} = \frac{-v_x}{r_\pi + R_s'}$$

The emitter current is $(\beta+1) i_b$, which **flows OUT of the emitter node** (drawing from the test source):

$$i_e = (\beta+1) \cdot \frac{-v_x}{r_\pi + R_s'} = \frac{-(\beta+1) v_x}{r_\pi + R_s'}$$

### Step 4 — Total Test Current
$$i_x = \frac{v_x}{R_E} - \frac{-(\beta+1) v_x}{r_\pi + R_s'} = v_x \left[ \frac{1}{R_E} + \frac{\beta+1}{r_\pi + R_s'} \right]$$

### Step 5 — Final Output Impedance
$$Z_{out} = \frac{v_x}{i_x} = R_E \parallel \left[ \frac{r_\pi + (R_s \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2)}{\beta+1} \right]$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Verification
With $R_s = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 47 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $r_\pi = 2.6 \text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$:

$$R_s' = R_s \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 = 1\text{k} \parallel 8.25\text{k} \approx 891 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$\frac{r_\pi + R_s'}{\beta+1} = \frac{2600 + 891}{101} = \frac{3491}{101} \approx 34.6 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$Z_{out} = 1000 \parallel 34.6 = \frac{1000 \times 34.6}{1034.6} \approx 33.4 \text{ }\Omega$$

> [!TIP]
> This $Z_{out} \approx 33 \text{ }\Omega$ is **orders of magnitude lower** than a typical CE amplifier's $Z_{out}$ (several kΩ), confirming the Emitter Follower's **excellent load-driving capability**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Performance Metric | Emitter Follower (CC) | Common Emitter (CE) | Emitter Follower Advantage |
|--------------------|------------------------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| Voltage Gain | $\approx 0.99$ | $\approx -200$ (large) | Unity, no inversion |
| Input Impedance | $\approx 7 \text{ k}\Omega$ (without bootstrap) | $\approx 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ | **Higher** |
| Output Impedance | $\approx 33 \text{ }\Omega$ | $\approx 50 \text{ k}\Omega$ | **Much Lower** |
| Current Gain | $(\beta+1) \approx 100$ | $\beta \approx 100$ | Slightly higher |
| Bandwidth | Very wide | Limited by Miller effect | **Wider** |
| Phase Shift | 0° | 180° | In-phase (desirable for buffers) |
| Use Case | Buffer, impedance matcher | Voltage amplifier | Complementary roles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the bandwidth wider in CC?** There is **no Miller effect** in the Emitter Follower because the collector is AC-grounded and the gain is approximately 1. The Miller-multiplied input capacitance $C_{M} = C_{\mu}(1 - A_v) \approx 0$ for $A_v \approx 1$, so the input capacitance stays small and the high-frequency response is preserved.
