- This current flows through $R_f$ to the output: $I_f = \dfrac{V_- - V_o}{R_f} = -\dfrac{V_o}{R_f}$
- Equating: $C \cdot \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -\dfrac{V_o}{R_f}$
- Solving: $V_o = -R_f C \cdot \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Pitfall — The Ideal Differentiator is Noisy!** The differentiator's gain increases with frequency ($\propto f$), amplifying high-frequency noise. The lab version often includes a **small input resistor** $R_1$ in series with $C$ and a **small feedback capacitor** $C_f$ in parallel with $R_f$ to form a **practical differentiator** that rolls off above a certain frequency.

### Real-World Utility
- **Edge detectors** in digital pulse circuits (output a spike at the rising/falling edge)
- **FM demodulators**
- **PID controllers** (the D-term)
- **Waveform shaping** (squaring a triangle wave gives a square wave)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Circuit | Output Equation | Key Constant | Units | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scale Changer (Inverting) | $V_o = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_{in}$ | $A_{CL} = -R_f/R_1$ | dimensionless | $R_1, R_f > 0$ |
| 2 | Scale Changer (Non-Inverting) | $V_o = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{in}$ | $A_{CL} = 1 + R_f/R_1$ | dimensionless | $R_1, R_f > 0$ |
| 3 | Adder (N-Input Inverting) | $V_o = -R_f \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{V_i}{R_i}$ | Weight $w_i = R_f/R_i$ | dimensionless | All $R_i > 0$ |
| 4 | Adder (Unity) | $V_o = -(V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_N)$ | — | V | $R_f = R_i = R$ |
| 5 | Integrator (Ideal) | $V_o(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\displaystyle\int_0^t V_{in}(\tau)\, d\tau + V_o(0)$ | Time const. $\tau = RC$ | seconds | Ideal: no $R_f$ |
| 6 | Integrator (Practical) | $V_o \approx -\dfrac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\, dt$ for $f \gg f_c$ | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$ | Hz | $R_f \gg R$ |
| 7 | Differentiator (Ideal) | $V_o(t) = -R_f C \cdot \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ | Time const. $\tau = R_f C$ | seconds | Ideal: no $R_1$ |
| 8 | Differentiator (Practical) | $V_o \approx -R_f C \cdot \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ for $f \ll f_a$ | $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C}$ | Hz | $R_1 \ll R_f$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Verification Rule:** For a sine input $V_{in}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$:
> - Integrator output: $V_o(t) = \dfrac{V_m}{\omega RC} \sin(\omega t - 90°)$ (90° lag, amplitude $\div \omega RC$)
> - Differentiator output: $V_o(t) = \omega R_f C \cdot V_m \cos(\omega t)$ (90° lead, amplitude $\times \omega R_f C$)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Scale Changer (Inverting Amplifier)

We start with the **circuit equations** and apply both golden rules of op-amp analysis.

**Step 1:** Identify the node potentials. The non-inverting input is grounded:
$$V_+ = 0 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the virtual short rule. The op-amp forces:
$$V_- = V_+ = 0 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the virtual open rule. Since $I_- = 0$, the current entering the inverting node through $R_1$ must equal the current leaving through $R_f$ (Kirchhoff's Current Law at the inverting node):
$$I_1 = I_f$$

**Step 4:** Express each current using Ohm's law. For $I_1$ (flowing from $V_{in}$ to $V_-$):
$$I_1 = \frac{V_{in} - V_-}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

**Step 5:** For $I_f$ (flowing from $V_-$ to $V_o$):
$$I_f = \frac{V_- - V_o}{R_f} = \frac{0 - V_o}{R_f} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into $I_1 = I_f$:
$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $V_o$ by cross-multiplication:
$$V_o = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_{in}$$

> The negative sign indicates **phase inversion** (180° phase shift) — a hallmark of the inverting configuration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Three-Input Summing Amplifier

**Step 1:** Identify the node potentials. The non-inverting input is grounded:
$$V_+ = 0 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the virtual short rule:
$$V_- = 0 \, \text{V (virtual ground)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply KCL at the inverting node. Three input currents sum and equal the feedback current:
$$I_1 + I_2 + I_3 = I_f$$

**Step 4:** Express each input current. Each input is at potential $V_i$ and the inverting node is at $0$ V:
$$I_1 = \frac{V_1}{R_1}, \quad I_2 = \frac{V_2}{R_2}, \quad I_3 = \frac{V_3}{R_3}$$

**Step 5:** Express the feedback current (flowing from $V_- = 0$ to $V_o$):
$$I_f = \frac{0 - V_o}{R_f} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute:
$$\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $V_o$:
$$V_o = -R_f \left( \frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} \right)$$

**Step 8 (Special Case):** If all resistors are equal ($R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_f = R$):
$$V_o = -\left( V_1 + V_2 + V_3 \right)$$

This is the **equal-weight summer** — the most commonly asked KTU question on the adder.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ideal Integrator

**Step 1:** Identify the node potentials. The non-inverting input is grounded:
$$V_+ = 0 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the virtual short rule:
$$V_- = 0 \, \text{V (virtual ground)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the virtual open rule. All current through $R$ flows into the capacitor (no current into op-amp):
$$I_R = I_C$$

**Step 4:** Express $I_R$ using Ohm's law:
$$I_R = \frac{V_{in} - V_-}{R} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R} = \frac{V_{in}}{R}$$

**Step 5:** Express $I_C$ using the capacitor i-v relationship. The current flowing **into** the capacitor from the $V_-$ side:
$$I_C = C \cdot \frac{d(V_- - V_o)}{dt} = C \cdot \frac{d(0 - V_o)}{dt} = -C \cdot \frac{dV_o}{dt}$$

**Step 6:** Equate $I_R = I_C$:
$$\frac{V_{in}}{R} = -C \cdot \frac{dV_o}{dt}$$

**Step 7:** Rearrange to isolate the derivative of the output:
$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = -\frac{1}{RC} \cdot V_{in}$$

**Step 8:** Integrate both sides with respect to time, from $0$ to $t$:
$$\int_0^t \frac{dV_o}{d\tau} \, d\tau = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_0^t V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 9:** Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus on the left side:
$$V_o(t) - V_o(0) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_0^t V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 10:** Final form (assuming initial condition $V_o(0) = 0$):
$$V_o(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_0^t V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

> **Sign convention check:** A positive DC input produces a **negative-going ramp** at the output, which is consistent with the inverting topology.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Ideal Differentiator

**Step 1:** Identify the node potentials. The non-inverting input is grounded:
$$V_+ = 0 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the virtual short rule:
$$V_- = 0 \, \text{V (virtual ground)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the virtual open rule. All current through $C$ flows into $R_f$:
$$I_C = I_{R_f}$$

**Step 4:** Express $I_C$ using the capacitor i-v relationship. The current flowing **from $V_{in}$ into the virtual ground node**:
$$I_C = C \cdot \frac{d(V_{in} - V_-)}{dt} = C \cdot \frac{d(V_{in} - 0)}{dt} = C \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

**Step 5:** Express $I_{R_f}$ using Ohm's law. Current flowing from virtual ground ($0$ V) to $V_o$:
$$I_{R_f} = \frac{V_- - V_o}{R_f} = \frac{0 - V_o}{R_f} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}$$

**Step 6:** Equate $I_C = I_{R_f}$:
$$C \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $V_o$:
$$V_o = -R_f C \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

> **Sign convention check:** A positive-going ramp input produces a **negative constant** at the output, which is consistent with the inverting topology.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Response to Standard Test Inputs

| Input Waveform | Mathematical Form | Integrator Output | Differentiator Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Step** $V_s$ | $V_{in}(t) = V_s \cdot u(t)$ | $V_o(t) = -\dfrac{V_s}{RC} \cdot t$ (ramp) | $V_o(t) = -R_f C \cdot V_s \cdot \delta(t)$ (impulse) |
| **Square Wave** $\pm V_m$ | Periodic $\pm V_m$ | **Triangular wave** of amplitude $\dfrac{V_m \cdot T/2}{RC}$ | **Spike train** at each transition |
| **Triangle Wave** | $\pm$ linear ramps | **Parabolic** segments | **Square wave** of amplitude $\pm R_f C \cdot \dfrac{2V_m}{T/2}$ |
| **Sine Wave** $V_m \sin(\omega t)$ | Continuous | $\dfrac{V_m}{\omega RC}\sin(\omega t - 90°)$ | $\omega R_f C \cdot V_m \cos(\omega t)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Viva Question:** *"What will the CRO display if you feed a **square wave** into an integrator?"*
> **Answer:** A **triangular wave**, because integrating a constant (the high or low level of the square wave) gives a linear ramp.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Common Op-Amp Symbol Convention (All Four Circuits)

All four circuits use the same standard op-amp symbol with the following pin connections (KTU lab standard):
- **Pin 2:** Inverting input ($V_-$)
- **Pin 3:** Non-inverting input ($V_+$)
- **Pin 6:** Output ($V_o$)
- **Pin 7:** $+V_{CC} = +12 \, \text{V}$
- **Pin 4:** $-V_{EE} = -12 \, \text{V}$

The non-inverting terminal ($V_+$) is **always grounded** in all four circuits of this experiment.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Scale Changer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Vin Source] -->|R1| nodeA[Inverting Node V minus]
    nodeA -->|Rf| outputA[Vout]
    nodeA -.->|feedback path| outputA
    gndA[Ground] --> vplusA[Non inverting V plus]
    vplusA --> opampA[Op Amp IC 741]
    opampA --> outputA
```

> **Block Reading:** $V_{in}$ enters via $R_1$ into $V_-$; the op-amp output feeds back through $R_f$ to $V_-$. The non-inverting input is at ground. Output is inverted and scaled by $R_f/R_1$.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Three-Input Adder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    v1[V1 Input] -->|R1| nodeB[Virtual Ground Node]
    v2[V2 Input] -->|R2| nodeB
    v3[V3 Input] -->|R3| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Rf| outB[Vout]
    nodeB -.feedback.-> outB
    gndB[Ground] --> vplusB[Non inverting V plus]
    vplusB --> opampB[Op Amp IC 741]
    opampB --> outB
```

> **Block Reading:** Three independent voltage sources $V_1, V_2, V_3$ feed the virtual ground node through their respective input resistors. The single feedback resistor $R_f$ sums the currents and converts them to output voltage.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Integrator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vinC[Vin Source] -->|R input| nodeC[Inverting Node]
    nodeC -->|C feedback| outC[Vout]
    nodeC -.feedback capacitor.-> outC
    gndC[Ground] --> vplusC[Non inverting V plus]
    vplusC --> opampC[Op Amp IC 741]
    opampC --> outC
```

> **Block Reading:** The input resistor $R$ converts input voltage to input current. The feedback capacitor $C$ integrates this current to produce a voltage at the output. The time constant $\tau = RC$ determines integration speed.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Differentiator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vinD[Vin Source] -->|C input| nodeD[Inverting Node]
    nodeD -->|Rf feedback| outD[Vout]
    nodeD -.feedback resistor.-> outD
    gndD[Ground] --> vplusD[Non inverting V plus]
    vplusD --> opampD[Op Amp IC 741]
    opampD --> outD
```

> **Block Reading:** The input capacitor $C$ produces a current proportional to the rate of change of $V_{in}$. The feedback resistor $R_f$ converts this current back to a voltage at the output. The time constant $\tau = R_f C$ scales the differentiation.

## 4.6 Functional Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Op Amp Circuits Module 2] --> branchA{Input Source Type}
    branchA -->|Single DC or AC| scalerA[Scale Changer - Gain Set by Rf over R1]
    branchA -->|Multiple Sources| adderA[Adder - Weighted Sum]
    branchA -->|Time Varying Signal| timedomainA{Time Domain Operation}
    timedomainA -->|Integrate over dt| integratorA[Integrator - Capacitor in Feedback]
    timedomainA -->|Differentiate over dt| differentiatorA[Differentiator - Capacitor at Input]
    scalerA --> outputA[Output to CRO for Observation]
    adderA --> outputA
    integratorA --> outputA
    differentiatorA --> outputA
```

> **Block Reading:** The four circuits branch logically based on the type of input signal and the desired mathematical operation. All four share the same op-amp IC 741 as the active element and produce an output observable on the CRO.

## 4.7 Pin Configuration Reference — IC 741

| Pin Number | Label | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Offset Null | Used to trim DC offset |
| 2 | Inverting Input ($V_-$) | Signal input for inverting circuits |
| 3 | Non-Inverting Input ($V_+$) | Reference input (grounded in this experiment) |
| 4 | $V_{EE}$ ($-V_{CC}$) | Negative supply, **$-12 \, \text{V}$** |
| 5 | Offset Null | Used to trim DC offset |
| 6 | Output | The amplified signal |
| 7 | $V_{CC}$ ($+V_{CC}$) | Positive supply, **$+12 \, \text{V}$** |
| 8 | NC | No Connection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Tip — Always connect Pins 7 and 4 first** (power supply), and check for heating of the IC. If the IC gets hot within 5 seconds, you have a **short circuit** — switch off immediately and re-check wiring.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define virtual ground and virtual short in the context of an ideal op-amp. Why are these concepts useful in analyzing op-amp circuits?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Virtual Short (1.5 marks):** For an ideal op-amp, the open-loop gain $A_{OL} \to \infty$. Since $V_{out} = A_{OL}(V_+ - V_-)$ is finite, it must follow that $V_+ - V_- = V_{out}/A_{OL} \to 0$. Therefore $V_+ = V_-$, which is called a **virtual short** — the two input terminals appear to be at the same potential even though they are not physically shorted.

**Virtual Open (1 mark):** The input impedance of an ideal op-amp is infinite, so the current flowing into either input terminal is zero: $I_+ = I_- = 0$. This is called a **virtual open**.

**Utility (0.5 marks):** These two assumptions reduce a complex op-amp circuit to simple Kirchhoff's voltage and current law problems at the inverting node, making analysis straightforward and accurate.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the difference between an integrator and a differentiator? State one practical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Integrator (1.5 marks):** An op-amp integrator uses a resistor $R$ at the input and a capacitor $C$ in the feedback loop. The output voltage is the **negative time integral** of the input voltage, scaled by the time constant $RC$: $V_o(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\, dt$. It is used in **analog computers** to solve differential equations and in **oscilloscope ramp generators**.

**Differentiator (1.5 marks):** An op-amp differentiator uses a capacitor $C$ at the input and a resistor $R_f$ in the feedback loop. The output voltage is the **negative time derivative** of the input voltage, scaled by $R_f C$: $V_o(t) = -R_f C \cdot \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$. It is used as an **edge detector** in digital pulse circuits and as the **D-term in PID controllers**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** is the #1 mistake in KTU papers. Every inverting op-amp circuit output equation must include the minus sign.
> 2. **Confusing integrator and differentiator component placement** is the #2 mistake. Remember: **C in feedback = integrator; C at input = differentiator**.
> 3. **Not stating both golden rules** explicitly before starting the derivation costs 1 mark in most 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Missing units** (e.g., Hz for frequency, s for time constant) costs 0.5 marks per occurrence.
> 5. **Forgetting initial conditions** in the integrator equation (writing $V_o = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\, dt$ without the $+V_o(0)$ term) may lose 1 mark in rigorous valuation.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Design an op-amp scale changer (inverting amplifier) using IC 741 to have a closed-loop gain of $-10$. Draw the circuit diagram and explain its operation. Assume a $1 \, \text{kHz}$, $1 \, \text{V}_{pp}$ sine wave is applied at the input. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the output equation of a **three-input inverting summing amplifier** using an op-amp. If $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 10 \, \text{k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 20 \, \text{k}\Omega$, calculate the output voltage when $V_1 = 1 \, \text{V}$, $V_2 = 2 \, \text{V}$, $V_3 = 3 \, \text{V}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Design (1 mark):** For a gain of $-10$:
$$A_{CL} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} = -10 \Rightarrow \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 10$$

Choose $R_1 = 10 \, \text{k}\Omega$, then $R_f = 100 \, \text{k}\Omega$.

**Circuit Description (2 marks):** The circuit consists of IC 741 with the non-inverting input (Pin 3) connected to ground. The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting input (Pin 2) through $R_1 = 10 \, \text{k}\Omega$. A feedback resistor $R_f = 100 \, \text{k}\Omega$ is connected between the output (Pin 6) and the inverting input (Pin 2). Pin 7 is connected to $+12 \, \text{V}$ and Pin 4 to $-12 \, \text{V}$.

**Operation (2 marks):** By virtual short, $V_- = V_+ = 0$ V (virtual ground). By virtual open, the current through $R_1$ equals the current through $R_f$:
$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f} \Rightarrow V_o = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_{in} = -10 V_{in}$$

**Output Calculation (2 marks):** For $V_{in} = 1 \, \text{V}_{pp}$ sine wave at 1 kHz:
$$V_o = -10 \times 1 \, \text{V}_{pp} = 10 \, \text{V}_{pp} \text{ at 1 kHz with 180° phase inversion}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Circuit Diagram (1 mark):** Three input voltages $V_1, V_2, V_3$ enter through $R_1, R_2, R_3$ respectively, all joining at the inverting node. A single feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output back to the inverting node. The non-inverting input is grounded.

**Derivation (4 marks):** Apply KCL at the inverting node (at $0$ V virtual ground):
$$\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}$$

$$V_o = -R_f \left( \frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} \right)$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):** Substituting $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 10 \, \text{k}\Omega$, $R_f = 20 \, \text{k}\Omega$, $V_1 = 1 \, \text{V}$, $V_2 = 2 \, \text{V}$, $V_3 = 3 \, \text{V}$:
$$V_o = -20 \text{k} \left( \frac{1}{10\text{k}} + \frac{2}{10\text{k}} + \frac{3}{10\text{k}} \right) = -20 \text{k} \times \frac{6}{10\text{k}} = -12 \, \text{V}$$

The output saturates at approximately $-11 \, \text{V}$ in practice (since supply is $\pm 12 \, \text{V}$).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the output voltage of an **op-amp integrator**. If $R = 10 \, \text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1 \, \mu\text{F}$, calculate the output voltage at $t = 5 \, \text{ms}$ when a DC input of $2 \, \text{V}$ is applied (assume zero initial condition). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the output voltage of an **op-amp differentiator**. Sketch the input and output waveforms when a **triangular wave** of peak amplitude $5 \, \text{V}$ and period $4 \, \text{ms}$ is applied. Take $R_f = 10 \, \text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1 \, \mu\text{F}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Circuit Diagram (1 mark):** Input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting input (Pin 2) through resistor $R = 10 \, \text{k}\Omega$. A capacitor $C = 0.1 \, \mu\text{F}$ is connected between the output (Pin 6) and the inverting input. The non-inverting input (Pin 3) is grounded.

**Derivation (4 marks):** Using the virtual ground at $V_- = 0$:
$$I_R = I_C \Rightarrow \frac{V_{in}}{R} = -C\frac{dV_o}{dt}$$

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = -\frac{V_{in}}{RC} \Rightarrow V_o(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_0^t V_{in}(\tau) d\tau$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):** Time constant $RC = 10^4 \times 10^{-7} = 10^{-3} \, \text{s} = 1 \, \text{ms}$.

For a constant DC input $V_{in} = 2 \, \text{V}$:
$$V_o(5 \text{ ms}) = -\frac{1}{1 \text{ ms}} \int_0^{5 \text{ ms}} 2 \, d\tau = -\frac{2 \times 5 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = -10 \, \text{V}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Circuit Diagram (1 mark):** Input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting input (Pin 2) through capacitor $C = 0.1 \, \mu\text{F}$. A feedback resistor $R_f = 10 \, \text{k}\Omega$ is connected between the output (Pin 6) and the inverting input. The non-inverting input (Pin 3) is grounded.

**Derivation (3 marks):** Using virtual ground and KCL:
$$C\frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f} \Rightarrow V_o = -R_f C \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

**Waveform Sketch & Calculation (3 marks):** A triangular wave of period $T = 4 \, \text{ms}$ swings between $\pm 5 \, \text{V}$. The slope during the rising edge (from $-5$ V to $+5$ V over $T/2 = 2 \, \text{ms}$):
$$\text{Slope} = \frac{10 \, \text{V}}{2 \, \text{ms}} = 5 \times 10^3 \, \text{V/s}$$

The output is a **square wave** of amplitude:
$$V_o = -R_f C \cdot \text{Slope} = -(10^4)(10^{-7})(5 \times 10^3) = -5 \, \text{V}$$

The differentiator output for a triangular input is a **square wave of amplitude $\pm 5 \, \text{V}$** (with 180° phase shift — negative slope gives positive output, positive slope gives negative output).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 Op-Amp Circuits**

- **Golden Rules:** (1) $V_+ = V_-$ (virtual short); (2) $I_+ = I_- = 0$ (virtual open). State these first in every derivation.
- **Scale Changer:** $V_o = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_{in}$. The ratio $R_f/R_1$ sets the gain. Always inverts.
- **Adder:** $V_o = -R_f \left( \dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{V_3}{R_3} \right)$. Equal resistors = unity summer. Used as a mixer or DAC building block.
- **Integrator:** $V_o(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\displaystyle\int_0^t V_{in}(\tau) d\tau + V_o(0)$. Square wave input → triangular output. **C in feedback.**
- **Differentiator:** $V_o(t) = -R_f C \cdot \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$. Triangular input → square output. **C at input.**
- **Negative Sign:** Always include the minus sign in inverting topologies. Missing it is the #1 valuation error.
- **Component Placement Mnemonic — "I" before "D" alphabet, "C" moves from feedback to input:**
  - **I**ntegrator: **C** is in fee**D**back
  - **D**ifferentiator: **C** is at **I**nput
- **DC Operating Point:** For IC 741 lab, use $\pm 12 \, \text{V}$ dual supply. Pins 7 and 4 must be connected to power **before** applying any input signal.
- **Saturation:** Output cannot exceed $V_{CC} - 2 \, \text{V} \approx 10 \, \text{V}$ for a $\pm 12 \, \text{V}$ supply. Always check whether your calculated $V_o$ exceeds the saturation limit.
- **CRO Connection:** Use **CH1** for input and **CH2** for output to compare phase relationship directly.
- **Frequency Response:** Differentiator's gain $\uparrow$ with frequency (high-pass); Integrator's gain $\downarrow$ with frequency (low-pass). This is why differentiators are noisy and integrators need a feedback resistor for stability.
- **Lab Viva Favorites:**
  1. Why is the non-inverting input grounded in all four circuits?
  2. What is the difference between practical and ideal integrator?
  3. Why can't you use a differentiator directly on a noisy signal?
  4. Draw the output waveform for a square wave input to the integrator.
  5. What happens if you swap $R$ and $C$ in the integrator circuit?
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Precision rectifier using Op-amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL308 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Precision rectifier using Op-amp**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Precision Rectifier** (also called a **Super Diode** or **Active Rectifier**) is an active electronic circuit that uses an operational amplifier in conjunction with one or more diodes to perform rectification of AC signals with **near-ideal accuracy**, even for input voltages whose magnitude is **smaller than the diode cut-in voltage** (typically **0.6 V to 0.7 V** for silicon diodes).

The op-amp compensates for the non-linear forward voltage drop ($V_F \approx 0.7$ V) of the conventional diode by placing the diode **inside the feedback loop**, thereby making the rectifier's effective threshold voltage equal to $V_F / (1 + A_{OL} \beta)$, which is negligibly small.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEEL308 - Module 2):**
> The lab experiment requires the student to **design, build, and test** a precision half-wave and a precision full-wave rectifier using an op-amp (typically **µA741** or **TL081**), observe the input and output waveforms on a CRO/DSO, and verify that the circuit rectifies signals as low as **50 mV (peak)** without distortion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to bail water out of a small boat using a bucket that has a **thick rubber lip** — every time you try to pour out even a small amount of water, a lot of it gets caught and held back by the lip (this is the diode's **forward voltage drop**, around 0.6 V). You cannot bail out small amounts of water accurately.

Now imagine you have a **mechanical assistant (the op-amp)** who holds the bucket and applies extra force on your behalf. The assistant senses how much water is being held back and **multiplies your effort by a huge factor (the open-loop gain, $A_{OL} \approx 10^5$ to $10^6$)**. Now, even a tiny input signal is enough to make the assistant pour out the water with perfect accuracy — the rubber lip's effect is reduced by the amplification factor.

> **In short:** The diode still exists, but the op-amp **divides the diode's voltage drop by its enormous gain**, making the rectifier behave almost like an *ideal* switch with zero threshold.

### 1.3 Why a Normal Diode Rectifier Fails for Small Signals

For a silicon diode, the Shockley diode equation is:

$$i_D = I_S \left( e^{v_D / \eta V_T} - 1 \right)$$

where $V_T \approx 26$ mV at room temperature, and the diode starts conducting appreciably only when $v_D \approx 0.6$ V. Any AC signal with a peak less than ~0.6 V will pass through the diode **without being rectified** — the output will simply be a heavily distorted, attenuated version of the input.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metric:** In instrumentation, biomedical signal processing (e.g., ECG, EMG), and sensor signal conditioning, signals of magnitude **a few millivolts to a few hundred millivolts** are common. Precision rectifiers are the only practical way to rectify such low-level AC signals faithfully.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Super Diode" Concept

A single diode $D_1$ is placed in the **feedback path** of an inverting op-amp, with the cathode at the output node and the anode tied to the inverting input. A second diode $D_2$ is added from the inverting input to the op-amp's output to keep the feedback loop closed during the *off* half-cycle and prevent the op-amp from **saturating**.

**Operating Cycle — Half-Wave Precision Rectifier:**

**Positive Half-Cycle ($V_{in} > 0$):**
- Op-amp output $V_{OA}$ tries to swing **negative** (inverting amplifier action).
- Diode $D_1$ becomes **forward-biased (ON)**.
- Virtual short condition holds: $V^- \approx V^+ = 0$ V.
- Diode $D_2$ is **reverse-biased (OFF)**.
- Since no current can flow into the op-amp's output through $D_2$, and $D_1$ is shunting current directly to the inverting node, the **load output $V_{out} = 0$ V**.

**Negative Half-Cycle ($V_{in} < 0$):**
- Op-amp output $V_{OA}$ tries to swing **positive**.
- Diode $D_1$ becomes **reverse-biased (OFF)**.
- Diode $D_2$ becomes **forward-biased (ON)** → op-amp output is now connected to the load through $R_f$.
- The circuit behaves as a standard **inverting amplifier**:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_{in} \quad (V_{in} < 0)$$

Since $V_{in}$ is negative in this half-cycle, $V_{out}$ is **positive**, yielding a **rectified** output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "precision" comes from the fact that to forward-bias $D_1$, the op-amp output only needs to reach **$V_F / (1 + A_{OL}\beta)$** in magnitude. With $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$ and $\beta = 1$, the effective threshold is $0.6 / (1 + 2 \times 10^5) \approx 3 \, \mu V$ — essentially **zero**.

### 2.2 Full-Wave Precision Rectifier (Absolute Value Circuit)

A precision full-wave rectifier is usually built with **two op-amps**:

- **Op-amp 1 (Precision Half-Wave Rectifier)** — provides either a positive or zero output depending on input polarity.
- **Op-amp 2 (Summing Amplifier)** — combines the original input with the first stage's output to produce the **full-wave rectified (absolute value)** waveform:

$$V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert \quad \text{(for unity-gain configuration)}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No** | **Quantity / Parameter** | **Formula** | **Boundary Condition / Notes** | **Unit** |
|----------|--------------------------|-------------|--------------------------------|----------|
| 1 | Diode forward voltage drop | $V_F \approx 0.6$ to $0.7$ | Silicon diode at $I_F = 10$ mA | V |
| 2 | Thermal voltage at 27°C | $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ | $k$ = Boltzmann const., $q$ = electron charge | mV |
| 3 | Half-wave output (positive half) | $V_{out} = 0$ | $V_{in} > 0$ | V |
| 4 | Half-wave output (negative half) | $V_{out} = -(R_f/R_1) \cdot V_{in}$ | $V_{in} < 0$ (yields $V_{out} > 0$) | V |
| 5 | Effective diode threshold | $V_{th,eff} = V_F / (1 + A_{OL} \beta)$ | $A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^5$ for $\mu$A741 | V |
| 6 | Full-wave output (unity gain) | $V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert$ | $R_f = R_1$ for all four resistors in classic 2-op-amp design | V |
| 7 | Slew-rate-limited max frequency | $f_{max} = SR / (2\pi V_{pk})$ | $SR = 0.5$ V/µs for $\mu$A741 | Hz |
| 8 | Peak inverse voltage on $D_1$ | $PIV = V_{sat}$ | Approx. equal to op-amp saturation voltage | V |
| 9 | Input bias current effect on $V_{out}$ | $\Delta V = I_{bias} \cdot R_f$ | Choose $R_f \leq 100$ kΩ to minimize drift | V |
| 10 | Closed-loop bandwidth | $f_{BW,CL} = f_t / (1 + A_{CL})$ | $f_t$ = unity-gain bandwidth of op-amp | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> In all the table rows above, any absolute-value bars or magnitude operators have been written using the keyword **"abs"** (e.g., $V_{out} = \text{abs}(V_{in})$) to comply with the markdown table pipe-restriction rule. The mathematical meaning is identical to $\vert \cdot \vert$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Area** | **Use of Precision Rectifier** |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|
| Biomedical instrumentation | Rectifying low-amplitude ECG / EMG / EEG signals (mV range) |
| AC voltmeters and multimeters | True-RMS and average-responding meters need accurate rectification |
| Signal demodulation | AM demodulation in communication receivers |
| Sensor signal conditioning | AC output from LVDTs, strain-gauge bridges with AC excitation |
| Audio processing | Detecting envelope of audio signals in compressors / AGC circuits |
| Phase-sensitive detectors (Lock-in amplifiers) | Synchronous detection of weak signals buried in noise |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Half-Wave Precision Rectifier Output

**Step 1 — Define the circuit nodes.**

Let the non-inverting input be grounded ($V^+ = 0$). The inverting input is node $A$, the op-amp output (before the diode network) is $V_{OA}$, and the final output is $V_{out}$ taken across $R_f$ (after $D_2$).

**Step 2 — Apply the ideal op-amp assumptions.**

Because of the **virtual short**, $V^- = V^+ = 0$ V, and no current flows into the op-amp input terminals.

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the inverting node during the negative half-cycle ($V_{in} < 0$).**

During this half-cycle, $D_1$ is OFF and $D_2$ is ON. The current through $R_1$ must equal the current through $R_f$:

$$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}$.**

$$V_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{R_1} = -V_{out} \cdot \frac{1}{R_f}$$

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_{in}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the sign condition.**

For $V_{in} < 0$, if we let $V_{in} = -\vert V_{in} \vert$:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot (-\vert V_{in} \vert) = +\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \vert V_{in} \vert \quad (\text{positive value})$$

**Step 6 — Summary of piecewise transfer function.**

$$V_{out} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } V_{in} \geq 0 \\[6pt] -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_{in} & \text{if } V_{in} < 0 \end{cases}$$

This is the **rectified half-wave output**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Full-Wave (Two Op-Amp) Precision Rectifier

**Circuit Topology:**

- Op-amp 1: A precision half-wave rectifier producing either $+V_{in}$ (when $V_{in} < 0$) or $0$ (when $V_{in} > 0$), at its output, but inverted. So output of Op-amp 1 is $\text{abs}(V_{in})$ when $V_{in} < 0$ else $0$.
- Op-amp 2: A summing (difference) amplifier that adds $V_{in}$ and (twice) the output of Op-amp 1, scaled by resistors.

**Standard design (with $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_f = R$):**

- For $V_{in} > 0$: Op-amp 1 output = $0$ V → Op-amp 2 output = $-(R_f/R) \cdot V_{in} - (R_f/R) \cdot (0) = -V_{in}$, but since $V_{in} > 0$, output = $-(V_{in})$ — this is negative, not rectified! 
- Therefore, an **additional inverting stage** OR a sign inversion in the second op-amp is included in practice.

A more common topology uses Op-amp 2 as a **difference amplifier** that yields $V_{out} = \text{abs}(V_{in})$ when all four resistors are equal:

$$V_{out} = -V_{in} - 2 \cdot V_{o1}$$

where $V_{o1}$ is the output of the first op-amp. For $V_{in} > 0$: $V_{o1} = 0$ → $V_{out} = -V_{in}$ (still negative!).

To get $V_{out} = +\text{abs}(V_{in})$, the first stage's transfer is arranged to be **non-inverting** for one polarity, and the second stage inverts the result while adding the input. With proper resistor choices ($R_f = 2R_1$ in the second stage), one obtains:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert}$$

### 3.3 Derivative Analysis: Effective Diode Threshold

Let the op-amp's open-loop gain be $A_{OL}$. When the diode is about to conduct, the voltage at the inverting node is $V_d$ (the diode drop), and the op-amp output is $V_{OA}$. Then:

$$V_{OA} = A_{OL} \cdot (V^+ - V^-) = A_{OL} \cdot (0 - V_{in})$$

The diode conducts when $V_{OA} \leq -V_F$ (for $D_1$ to turn on). The threshold input voltage $V_{in,th}$ is therefore:

$$V_{in,th} = \frac{V_F}{A_{OL}}$$

For $\mu$A741, $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$, $V_F = 0.6$ V:

$$V_{in,th} = \frac{0.6}{2 \times 10^5} = 3 \, \mu V$$

This is the **fundamental reason** the circuit is called a "precision" rectifier.

### 3.4 Slew-Rate Limitation — Maximum Useful Frequency

The op-amp's output cannot change faster than its slew rate $SR$. For a sinusoid of peak $V_{pk}$ and frequency $f$:

$$\left. \frac{dv}{dt} \right|_{max} = 2\pi f \cdot V_{pk}$$

To avoid slew-induced distortion:

$$2\pi f \cdot V_{pk} \leq SR$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{pk}}$$

For $\mu$A741 ($SR = 0.5$ V/µs) and $V_{pk} = 1$ V:

$$f_{max} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 1} \approx 79.6 \, \text{kHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For higher-frequency applications, use a **fast op-amp** such as **TL081** ($SR = 13$ V/µs) or **LF351** ($SR = 13$ V/µs), which raises $f_{max}$ to over **2 MHz** at the same signal level.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Half-Wave Precision Rectifier

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["VIN AC SOURCE"] --> nodeB["R1 INPUT RESISTOR"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["OPAMP INVERTING INPUT NODE"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["OPAMP UA741"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["DIODE D1 IN FEEDBACK"]
    nodeE --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeF["DIODE D2 TO OUTPUT"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["VOUT RECTIFIED HALF WAVE"]
    nodeH["GROUND"] --> nodeI["OPAMP NON INVERTING INPUT"]
    nodeI --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeJ["RF FEEDBACK RESISTOR"]
    nodeJ --> nodeG
```

### 4.2 Signal-Flow Diagram of Half-Wave Operation

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["VIN POSITIVE"] --> nodeB["D1 FORWARD BIASED ON"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["D2 REVERSE BIASED OFF"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["VOUT EQUALS ZERO"]
    nodeE["VIN NEGATIVE"] --> nodeF["D1 REVERSE BIASED OFF"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["D2 FORWARD BIASED ON"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["VOUT EQUALS POSITIVE RF BY R1 TIMES VIN MAG"]
```

### 4.3 Two Op-Amp Full-Wave Precision Rectifier (Absolute Value Circuit)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["VIN AC INPUT"] --> nodeB["OPAMP1 PRECISION HALF WAVE STAGE"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["NODE X INTERMEDIATE"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["SUMMING INPUT TO OPAMP2"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["INPUT VIA R3 TO OPAMP2"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["OPAMP2 SUMMING DIFFERENCE STAGE"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["VOUT ABSOLUTE VALUE OF VIN"]
    nodeH["GROUND"] --> nodeB
    nodeI["GROUND"] --> nodeF
```

### 4.4 Component-Level Pin Map (for µA741 8-Pin DIP)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["PIN 1 OFFSET NULL"] --> nodeB["CONNECT TO 10K POT"]
    nodeC["PIN 2 INVERTING INPUT"] --> nodeD["CONNECT TO R1 AND D1 ANODE"]
    nodeE["PIN 3 NON INVERTING INPUT"] --> nodeF["CONNECT TO GROUND"]
    nodeG["PIN 4 VCC MINUS 15V"] --> nodeH["CONNECT TO NEGATIVE RAIL"]
    nodeI["PIN 5 OFFSET NULL"] --> nodeB
    nodeJ["PIN 6 OUTPUT"] --> nodeK["CONNECT TO D2 ANODE AND D1 CATHODE"]
    nodeL["PIN 7 VCC PLUS 15V"] --> nodeM["CONNECT TO POSITIVE RAIL"]
    nodeN["PIN 8 NC"] --> nodeO["LEAVE UNCONNECTED"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above are simplified functional block diagrams (per the fallback rule). In the actual lab experiment, the student should draw the **standard circuit schematic** (op-amp triangle with $D_1$ in feedback and $D_2$ from inverting input to output) in their lab record using standard IEEE/ANSI symbols.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is a conventional diode rectifier unsuitable for signals below 0.6 V? How does an op-amp-based precision rectifier overcome this limitation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A conventional silicon diode has a forward voltage drop ($V_F$) of approximately **0.6 V**. Signals with peak amplitude below 0.6 V cannot forward-bias the diode, so the diode remains in the cut-off region and the output is essentially zero or severely distorted. In an op-amp-based precision rectifier, the diode is placed inside the feedback loop. The op-amp's large open-loop gain ($A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^5$ for µA741) divides the diode's forward drop by this factor, making the effective threshold voltage equal to $V_F / A_{OL} \approx 3 \, \mu V$. This allows faithful rectification of signals as small as a few millivolts. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the function of diode $D_2$ in a precision half-wave rectifier circuit? What happens if it is removed?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Diode $D_2$ is connected between the inverting input and the op-amp output, with polarity opposite to $D_1$. Its function is to **provide a feedback path during the half-cycle when $D_1$ is reverse-biased**, thereby **preventing the op-amp from going into saturation**. If $D_2$ is removed, the op-amp output would saturate at $\pm V_{sat}$ during every negative half-cycle, the recovery time from saturation would distort the output, and the effective switching speed of the rectifier would be drastically reduced. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **precision half-wave rectifier** using an op-amp. Derive the expression for the output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The precision half-wave rectifier consists of an op-amp (µA741), a feedback diode $D_1$, a clamping diode $D_2$, an input resistor $R_1$, and a feedback resistor $R_f$. The non-inverting input is grounded. The inverting input is connected to $V_{in}$ through $R_1$. Diode $D_1$ is connected between the inverting input and the op-amp output, with its cathode at the op-amp output. Diode $D_2$ is connected with its anode at the inverting input and cathode at the op-amp output.

**Working — Positive Half-Cycle ($V_{in} > 0$):**
- The op-amp output attempts to swing negative.
- $D_1$ becomes forward-biased (ON) → feedback loop closed.
- Virtual short gives $V^- = 0$ V.
- $D_2$ is reverse-biased (OFF) → no current to load.
- $V_{out} = 0$ V.

**Working — Negative Half-Cycle ($V_{in} < 0$):**
- The op-amp output attempts to swing positive.
- $D_1$ is reverse-biased (OFF).
- $D_2$ becomes forward-biased (ON) → feedback through $R_f$.
- The circuit behaves as an inverting amplifier:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_{in} \quad (V_{in} < 0)$$

Since $V_{in}$ is negative, $V_{out}$ is **positive and proportional to the input magnitude**. Thus the negative half-cycle appears as a positive output — the signal is rectified. **(7 marks — full marks for circuit diagram, working, and derivation)**

---

**(b)** Design a precision half-wave rectifier to rectify a **100 mV peak, 1 kHz** sine wave using a µA741 op-amp and 1N4148 diodes. Choose appropriate component values and verify the slew-rate limitation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Choose Gain:** Since the input is only 100 mV peak, even unity-gain rectification (gain = 1) gives a usable output of 100 mV peak. Choose $R_f / R_1 = 1$ → use $R_1 = R_f = 10$ kΩ (standard value, keeps input bias current effect small).

**Step 2 — Choose Diodes:** 1N4148 is a small-signal fast switching diode with $V_F \approx 0.6$ V and reverse recovery time $\approx 4$ ns. Suitable for this low-frequency (1 kHz) application.

**Step 3 — Power Supply:** Use $\pm 15$ V dual supply for µA741. Add 0.1 µF decoupling capacitors near the supply pins.

**Step 4 — Slew-Rate Check:**

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{pk}} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 0.1} \approx 796 \, \text{kHz}$$

Since the operating frequency (1 kHz) is far below $f_{max}$, **slew-rate distortion will not occur**. The design is valid.

**Step 5 — Expected Output:** A half-wave rectified sine wave with peak amplitude 100 mV, frequency 1 kHz, on a 0 V baseline.

**Step 6 — Practical Tip:** Add a **10 kΩ offset null potentiometer** between pins 1 and 5 of the µA741, with the wiper to $V^-$ supply (pin 4), to null any residual output during the $V_{in} > 0$ half-cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **precision full-wave rectifier (absolute value circuit)** using two op-amps. Derive the output expression assuming all resistors are equal to $R$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A precision full-wave rectifier uses **two op-amps**:
- **Op-amp 1** is a precision half-wave rectifier with gain = 1 (using $R_1 = R_f = R$).
- **Op-amp 2** is a summing/difference amplifier.

**For $V_{in} > 0$:**
- Op-amp 1 output = 0 V (since $D_1$ is ON, $D_2$ is OFF, virtual ground at the load).
- Op-amp 2 output = $-(V_{in}) - 2(0) = -V_{in}$ (negative).
- Adding a final inverting buffer with unity gain gives $+V_{in}$ at the output.

**For $V_{in} < 0$:**
- Op-amp 1 output = $-(-V_{in}) = +V_{in}$ (positive, since the inverting amplifier gives positive output for negative input).
- Op-amp 2 output = $-(V_{in}) - 2(+V_{in}) = -3V_{in}$. 
  *(Note: Resistor values in the summing stage must be chosen carefully — typically $R_2 = R$, $R_3 = R/2$ — to produce $V_{out} = +\vert V_{in} \vert$.)*

**Final Piecewise Transfer Function (with proper resistor scaling):**

$$V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert \quad \text{for all } V_{in}$$

**Output Waveform:** A full-wave rectified sine wave — both halves of the input appear as positive humps at the output, with no gap between successive pulses. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Compare the performance of a **precision rectifier** with that of a **conventional diode rectifier** for the following parameters: (i) minimum input amplitude, (ii) distortion, (iii) temperature stability, (iv) frequency response, (v) complexity, and (vi) cost. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| **Parameter** | **Conventional Rectifier** | **Precision Rectifier** |
|---------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| (i) Min. input amplitude | ~0.6 V (silicon diode drop) | Few mV (limited by op-amp offset) |
| (ii) Distortion | High near threshold; non-linear | Very low; linear due to high loop gain |
| (iii) Temperature stability | Poor (V_F varies ~ –2 mV/°C) | Good (V_F / A_OL effect negligible) |
| (iv) Frequency response | Excellent (limited only by diode recovery) | Limited by op-amp slew rate and GBW |
| (v) Circuit complexity | Simple (1 diode + load) | Complex (1 or 2 op-amps + diodes + Rs) |
| (vi) Cost | Very low | Moderate to high (op-amp + components) |

**Conclusion:** Precision rectifiers are indispensable for **low-level signal rectification** in instrumentation, while conventional rectifiers remain the workhorse for **power-supply applications** where high voltages and currents are involved. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Evaluations:**
> 1. **Drawing the diode in the wrong direction** — students frequently place $D_1$ with anode at the inverting input and cathode at the op-amp output. The correct polarity is the **opposite** (for the standard inverting half-wave configuration).
> 2. **Forgetting the role of $D_2$** — many students omit $D_2$ in their diagram. This guarantees a **2-mark deduction** under KTU valuation schemes because $D_2$ is essential to prevent op-amp saturation.
> 3. **Not specifying the half-cycle** — when deriving $V_{out}$, students often write only $V_{out} = -(R_f/R_1) V_{in}$ without mentioning that this expression is valid **only for $V_{in} < 0$**. The piecewise transfer function is **mandatory**.
> 4. **Omitting the slew-rate check** — when designing for a specific frequency, the examiner expects a verification that $f < f_{max} = SR / (2\pi V_{pk})$. Omitting this loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing precision half-wave and full-wave** — the precision *half-wave* rectifier uses **one op-amp**, while the precision *full-wave* rectifier uses **two op-amps**. Mixing these up is a frequent error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Precision Rectifier = Op-amp + Diode in Feedback Loop** — the active component cancels the diode's $V_F$ drop.
- **Effective Threshold Voltage:** $V_{th,eff} = V_F / A_{OL} \approx 3 \, \mu V$ for µA741.
- **Half-Wave Transfer Function:**
  $$V_{out} = 0 \text{ for } V_{in} \geq 0; \quad V_{out} = -(R_f / R_1) \cdot V_{in} \text{ for } V_{in} < 0.$$
- **Full-Wave Output:** $V_{out} = \text{abs}(V_{in})$ after proper resistor scaling.
- **Function of $D_2$:** Prevents op-amp saturation during the off half-cycle; must never be omitted.
- **Function of $D_1$:** Acts as the "super diode" — it is the active rectifying element inside the feedback loop.
- **Slew-Rate Limit:** $f_{max} = SR / (2\pi V_{pk})$ — choose a faster op-amp (TL081, LF351) for high-frequency signals.
- **Choose $R_f \leq 100$ kΩ** to keep input bias current errors below a few mV.
- **Always use $\pm 15$ V dual supply** for µA741 and add 0.1 µF decoupling capacitors at pins 7 and 4.
- **Typical Lab Test Values:** $V_{in} = 100$ mV peak, $f = 1$ kHz, $R_1 = R_f = 10$ kΩ, expected $V_{out,peak} = 100$ mV (half-wave) or 100 mV (full-wave after scaling).
- **CRO/DSO Connection:** CH1 → input, CH2 → output, set to **DC coupling** to see the rectified waveform's true baseline.
- **Applications to remember:** ECG rectification, AC voltmeters, AM demodulators, lock-in amplifiers, sensor signal conditioning.
- **Key formulas at a glance:**
  $$V_{out,HW} = 0 \;\; \text{or} \;\; -\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_{in};\quad V_{out,FW} = \vert V_{in} \vert;\quad f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{pk}}.$$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Op-Amp Oscillators - RC Phase shift and Wien Bridge Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL308 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Op-Amp Oscillators - RC Phase shift and Wien Bridge Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Op-Amp Oscillators?

An **Op-Amp Oscillator** is a self-sustaining electronic circuit that uses an operational amplifier along with passive frequency-selective components (resistors and capacitors) to generate a continuous, periodic waveform (typically sinusoidal) **without any external AC input signal**. The output is produced entirely from the DC power supply, governed by the principle of regenerative positive feedback that satisfies the **Barkhausen Criterion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *An oscillator is an electronic circuit that converts DC energy from the power supply into AC energy in the form of periodic waveforms (sine, square, or triangular), without requiring an external triggering signal. The condition for sustained oscillation is governed by the Barkhausen Criterion.*

---

## 1.2 The Two Oscillators Covered in This Experiment

### A. RC Phase Shift Oscillator
The **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** uses an op-amp in an **inverting configuration** combined with a **cascade of three identical RC lead/lag networks**. Each RC section contributes a phase shift, and together they produce exactly **180°** of phase lag. Since the inverting op-amp itself provides the remaining **180°**, the total loop phase shift becomes **360° (= 0°)**, satisfying the Barkhausen phase condition.

### B. Wien Bridge Oscillator
The **Wien Bridge Oscillator** uses an op-amp in a **non-inverting configuration** along with a **Wien Bridge network** (a series RC arm and a parallel RC arm) as the frequency-determining positive feedback element. It is the most widely used audio-frequency (20 Hz to 20 kHz) sine wave generator.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Perpetual Swing"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Playground Swing**
> Imagine pushing a child on a swing. For the swing to oscillate forever, you must:
> 1. **Push at the right time** (timing/phase match) — this is the **phase condition** (loop phase shift = 0° or 360°).
> 2. **Push with the right amount of force** to compensate friction — this is the **gain condition** ($|A\beta| \geq 1$).
>
> In an oscillator, the **RC network decides WHEN to push** (sets the frequency), and the **op-amp decides HOW HARD to push** (sets the gain). If both conditions are met perfectly, the swing never stops — just like a sustained oscillation!

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Phase contributed per RC section (RC Phase Shift) | $\phi$ | **60°** (each), Total = **180°** |
| Phase contributed by inverting op-amp | $\phi_A$ | **180°** |
| Phase contributed by non-inverting op-amp | $\phi_A$ | **0°** |
| Minimum closed-loop gain (RC Phase Shift) | $\vert A_v \vert$ | **29** |
| Minimum closed-loop gain (Wien Bridge) | $A_v$ | **3** |
| Standard frequency range (Wien Bridge) | $f_0$ | **1 Hz to 1 MHz** |
| Quality factor (Wien Bridge at resonance) | $Q$ | **1/3** |

---

## 1.5 Why These Oscillators Matter in Engineering

- **RC Phase Shift**: Used in low-cost, low-frequency tone generators, function generators, and audio test equipment.
- **Wien Bridge**: The industry standard for **audio signal generators**, distortion analyzers, and high-purity sine wave sources in laboratory instruments.
- Both are excellent pedagogical circuits to understand **positive feedback, frequency selectivity, and amplitude stabilization** — core concepts tested in the KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Barkhausen Criterion — Foundation of All Oscillators

For any sinusoidal oscillator to produce sustained oscillations, **two simultaneous conditions** must be met:

> [!NOTE]
> **Barkhausen Criterion (KTU High-Yield Topic):**
> 1. **Loop Gain Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must be unity or greater.
>
> $$\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$$
> 2. **Loop Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be **0° or an integer multiple of 360°**.
>
> $$\angle A\beta = 0^{\circ} \text{ (or } n \times 360^{\circ}\text{)}$$

Where $A$ = amplifier gain, $\beta$ = feedback factor (transfer function of RC network).

---

## 2.2 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Operational Logic

The circuit uses a **three-stage RC ladder network** in the feedback path of an **inverting op-amp** (gain $A = -R_f / R_1$).

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Op-Amp Stage:** The inverting configuration provides exactly **180°** phase shift between input and output.
2. **RC Network Stage:** The three cascaded RC sections act as a frequency-selective attenuator. At one specific frequency, the network provides **180° of phase lag** and an attenuation of $\frac{1}{29}$.
3. **Loop Phase:** Total phase = $180^{\circ} \text{ (op-amp)} + 180^{\circ} \text{ (RC network)} = 360^{\circ} \equiv 0^{\circ}$ ✓
4. **Loop Gain:** To compensate the network's attenuation, the op-amp gain must satisfy $\vert A \vert \geq 29$.
5. **Result:** A sustained sine wave is generated at the **frequency of oscillation**.

---

## 2.3 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Operational Logic

The circuit uses a **non-inverting op-amp** (gain $A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$) with a **Wien Bridge network** providing positive feedback, and a **resistive voltage divider** providing negative feedback for gain stabilization.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Op-Amp Stage:** Non-inverting configuration — phase shift = **0°**.
2. **Wien Network:** At one specific frequency, the positive feedback network's phase shift = **0°** (i.e., the network is purely resistive at that frequency).
3. **Loop Phase:** Total phase = $0^{\circ} + 0^{\circ} = 0^{\circ}$ ✓
4. **Loop Gain:** At that frequency, $\beta = \frac{1}{3}$, so the op-amp must provide $A \geq 3$.
5. **Amplitude Stabilization:** A **non-linear element** (e.g., back-to-back Zener diodes, thermistor, or JFET) is included in the negative feedback path to dynamically adjust the gain and stabilize the amplitude.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Condition / Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Frequency of RC Phase Shift Oscillator | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}$ | Hz |
| 2 | Angular frequency of RC Phase Shift | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$ | rad/s |
| 3 | Minimum gain (RC Phase Shift) | $\vert A \vert \geq 29$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Feedback factor (RC Phase Shift at $f_0$) | $\vert \beta \vert = \dfrac{1}{29}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Frequency of Wien Bridge Oscillator | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz |
| 6 | Angular frequency of Wien Bridge | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | rad/s |
| 7 | Minimum gain (Wien Bridge) | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} \geq 3$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Maximum gain (Wien Bridge) | $A_v \leq 3$ for amplitude stability | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Feedback factor (Wien Bridge at $f_0$) | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Phase shift per RC section (at $f_0$) | $\phi = 60^{\circ}$ | Degrees |
| 11 | Total RC network phase shift (at $f_0$) | $\phi_{RC} = 180^{\circ}$ | Degrees |
| 12 | Op-amp phase shift (RC Phase Shift circuit) | $\phi_A = 180^{\circ}$ | Degrees |
| 13 | Op-amp phase shift (Wien Bridge circuit) | $\phi_A = 0^{\circ}$ | Degrees |
| 14 | Quality factor of Wien Bridge | $Q = \dfrac{1}{3}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize that the magic numbers **29** (RC Phase Shift) and **3** (Wien Bridge) appear almost every year. The frequency formulas are derived directly from the loop transfer function analysis.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Oscillator | Engineering Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| RC Phase Shift | Low-frequency function generators, electronic organs, audio test oscillators, instrumentation signal sources. |
| Wien Bridge | Laboratory audio signal generators, distortion analyzers, carrier generation in communication transmitters, Wien-bridge-based capacitance meters. |
| Both | Core building blocks in **Phase-Locked Loops (PLL)**, **function generator ICs** (e.g., ICL8038, XR2206), and **automatic gain control (AGC)** systems. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Frequency of RC Phase Shift Oscillator

### Circuit Setup
The RC phase shift network consists of three identical RC sections cascaded together. The op-amp is in inverting configuration, so the total gain of the amplifier is:

$$A = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

The loop transfer function is $A\beta$, where $\beta$ is the transfer function of the **three-stage RC ladder network** loaded by the amplifier's input impedance (assumed infinite, so loading is neglected).

### Step 1: Transfer Function of a Single RC Section (Loaded)

For one RC low-pass section (with the next stage as the load), using the voltage divider rule and the standard approach for a *ladder network* (assuming the input to the next stage draws no current, i.e., infinite input impedance of the op-amp), the transfer function of each section is:

$$H_1(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC} \quad \text{(this is a high-pass / lead type)}$$

However, the standard textbook analysis uses the **loaded three-stage ladder** and writes the total feedback factor $\beta$ as a ratio of two complex polynomials. After applying KCL/KVL at each node of the ladder, the resulting expression is:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{1}{\left(1 - 5\omega^2 R^2 C^2\right) + j\left(6\omega RC - \omega^3 R^3 C^3\right)}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Phase Condition (Imaginary Part = 0)

The loop phase shift of the RC network must be exactly **180°** to cancel the **180°** shift of the inverting op-amp. Mathematically, the **imaginary part** of the denominator must be zero:

$$\text{Im}\left[\text{Denominator}\right] = 0$$

$$6\omega RC - \omega^3 R^3 C^3 = 0$$

### Step 3: Factor and Solve for $\omega$

Factor out the common term $\omega RC$:

$$\omega RC \left(6 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2\right) = 0$$

Since $\omega \neq 0$ (we want a real oscillation frequency), we set:

$$6 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 0$$

$$\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 6$$

$$\omega^2 = \frac{6}{R^2 C^2}$$

Taking the square root:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}} \quad \text{rad/s}$$

### Step 4: Convert to Frequency $f_0$

Using the relationship $f_0 = \dfrac{\omega_0}{2\pi}$:

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}} \quad \text{Hz}}$$

### Step 5: Determine the Minimum Gain Requirement

Substitute $\omega_0$ back into the real part of the denominator:

$$\text{Re}\left[\text{Denominator}\right] = 1 - 5\omega_0^2 R^2 C^2 = 1 - 5(6) = 1 - 30 = -29$$

Therefore the magnitude of the feedback factor is:

$$\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29}$$

### Step 6: Apply the Barkhausen Gain Condition

For sustained oscillation, $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$:

$$\vert A \vert \cdot \frac{1}{29} = 1 \implies \vert A \vert = 29$$

Since the op-amp is in inverting mode, $A = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$, so the magnitude condition becomes:

$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} \geq 29 \quad \text{(typically } R_f = 29R_1 \text{ for exact oscillation)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Frequency of Wien Bridge Oscillator

### Circuit Setup
The Wien Bridge network consists of a **series RC combination** (arm 1) and a **parallel RC combination** (arm 2). The op-amp is in **non-inverting configuration** with gain $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$.

### Step 1: Impedance of the Two Arms

**Series arm** (let's call it $Z_1$):
$$Z_1 = R - \frac{j}{\omega C} = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$

**Parallel arm** (let's call it $Z_2$):
$$Z_2 = \frac{R \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### Step 2: Feedback Factor of Wien Network

The output of the Wien network (which feeds the non-inverting input of the op-amp) is across $Z_2$, so by the voltage divider rule:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{\frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{\left(R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}\right) + \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

### Step 3: Simplify (Multiply numerator and denominator by $1 + j\omega RC$)

**Numerator becomes:**
$$R$$

**Denominator becomes:**
$$\left(R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}\right)(1 + j\omega RC) + R$$

Expanding the first term:
$$R(1 + j\omega RC) + \frac{1}{j\omega C}(1 + j\omega RC) + R$$

$$= R + j\omega R^2 C + \frac{1}{j\omega C} + R + R$$

Using $\dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = \dfrac{-j}{\omega C}$ and combining real parts:
$$= 2R + R + j\omega R^2 C - \frac{j}{\omega C}$$

$$= 3R + j\left(\omega R^2 C - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

### Step 4: Full Expression for $\beta$

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{R}{\left(3R + j\left(\omega R^2 C - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)\right)}$$

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

### Step 5: Apply the Phase Condition

For zero phase shift in the feedback network (needed since op-amp phase is 0°), the **imaginary part** of the denominator must be zero:

$$\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC} = 0$$

$$\omega RC = \frac{1}{\omega RC}$$

$$\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 1$$

$$\omega RC = 1$$

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \text{rad/s}$$

### Step 6: Convert to Frequency $f_0$

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \quad \text{Hz}}$$

### Step 7: Determine the Minimum Gain Requirement

Substitute $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ back into the expression for $\beta$:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3 + j(1 - 1)} = \frac{1}{3}$$

The feedback factor is real and equal to $\dfrac{1}{3}$. By Barkhausen:

$$A \beta = 1 \implies A \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 1 \implies A = 3$$

Since $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ for non-inverting op-amp:

$$1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \implies \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2 \implies \boxed{R_f = 2R_1}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | RC Phase Shift Oscillator | Wien Bridge Oscillator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Op-Amp Configuration | Inverting | Non-Inverting |
| Phase shift by Op-Amp | **180°** | **0°** |
| Phase shift by RC Network | **180°** (3 sections × 60°) | **0°** |
| Frequency of Oscillation | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| Feedback Factor $\beta$ | $\dfrac{1}{29}$ | $\dfrac{1}{3}$ |
| Required Gain $A$ | $\geq 29$ | $\geq 3$ |
| Gain Expression | $A = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ |
| Frequency Stability | Moderate | Excellent (high Q at resonance) |
| Output Waveform Purity | Moderate distortion | Very low distortion (with AGC) |
| Typical Frequency Range | 1 Hz – 100 kHz | 1 Hz – 1 MHz |
| Amplitude Stabilization | Not essential (gain fixed slightly above 29) | Essential (JFET, thermistor, Zener diodes) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — RC Phase Shift Oscillator

```mermaid
graph LR
    DC1[DC Supply +/-15V] --> opampA[Op-Amp IC 741]
    opampA --> nodeOut
    nodeOut --> RC1[RC Section 1]
    RC1 --> RC2[RC Section 2]
    RC2 --> RC3[RC Section 3]
    RC3 --> fbIn[Feedback to Inverting Input]
    fbIn --> opampA
    nodeOut --> sinOut[Sinusoidal Output f0]
    sinOut --> scope[Connected to CRO]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Wien Bridge Oscillator

```mermaid
graph LR
    DC2[DC Supply +/-15V] --> opampB[Op-Amp IC 741 Non-Inverting]
    opampB --> nodeB
    nodeB --> seriesRC[Series RC Arm Z1]
    seriesRC --> parallelRC[Parallel RC Arm Z2]
    parallelRC --> ground1[Ground]
    nodeB --> sinB[Sinusoidal Output f0]
    sinB --> croB[Connected to CRO]
    sinB --> negFB[Negative Feedback to Inverting Input]
    negFB --> gainControl[Gain Control Rf and R1]
    gainControl --> opampB
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Feedback Loop in Oscillators

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[DC Power Supply] --> ampA[Amplifier Stage A Op-Amp]
    ampA --> fsA[Frequency Selective Network beta]
    fsA --> checkA{Condition 1 Phase Shift equals 0 deg or 360 deg}
    checkA --> okA[Yes]
    checkA --> noA[No - Recalculate Network]
    ampA --> checkB{Condition 2 Loop Gain times beta greater than or equal 1}
    checkB --> okB[Yes]
    checkB --> noB[No - Increase Amplifier Gain]
    okA --> sustainA[Sustained Oscillation at f0]
    okB --> sustainA
    sustainA --> outA[Sinusoidal Output Waveform]
    noA --> fsA
    noB --> ampA
```

## 4.4 Pin-Level Op-Amp 741 Connections

```mermaid
graph LR
    pin2[Pin 2 Inverting Input] --> invNet[RC Network or R1]
    pin3[Pin 3 Non-Inverting Input] --> nonInvNet[Wien Network or Ground]
    pin4[Pin 4 Vee minus 15V] --> vneg[Negative Supply]
    pin7[Pin 7 Vcc plus 15V] --> vpos[Positive Supply]
    pin6[Pin 6 Output] --> load[Load Resistor and CRO]
    pin1[Pin 1 Offset Null] --> offsetPot[10 kOhm Potentiometer]
    pin5[Pin 5 Offset Null] --> offsetPot
    pin8[Pin 8 NC] --> nc1[Not Connected]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State and explain the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Barkhausen criterion states that for sustained oscillations in a feedback oscillator, two conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Loop Gain Condition:** The magnitude of the product of amplifier gain $A$ and feedback factor $\beta$ must be equal to or greater than unity.
$$\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$$
*(1 Mark)*

2. **Loop Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be an integer multiple of $360°$ (or equivalently, $0°$).
$$\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } n \times 360°$$
*(1 Mark)*

**Practical Note:** In practice, $\vert A\beta \vert$ is kept slightly greater than 1 at start-up (to build up oscillations from noise) and an automatic gain control (AGC) loop brings it back to exactly 1 for stable amplitude. *(1 Mark)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of RC network in an RC phase shift oscillator?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The RC phase shift network consists of **three cascaded RC sections** that act as the **frequency-determining feedback element** in the oscillator circuit. *(1 Mark)*
- Each RC section provides a phase shift, and together the three sections produce a **total phase shift of 180°** at the **frequency of oscillation** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$. *(1 Mark)*
- The network also introduces an **attenuation of 1/29**, which the inverting op-amp must compensate by providing a minimum gain of $\vert A \vert \geq 29$. *(1 Mark)*

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Most common mark-loss area #1:** Students forget the negative sign in the gain expression for RC Phase Shift oscillator and write $A = R_f/R_1$ instead of $A = -R_f/R_1$. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> - **Most common mark-loss area #2:** Confusing the feedback factor between the two oscillators (writing $\beta = 1/3$ for RC Phase Shift or $\beta = 1/29$ for Wien Bridge). **(Lose 1 mark)**
> - **Most common mark-loss area #3:** Forgetting to state the **Barkhausen criterion explicitly** before solving. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> - **Most common mark-loss area #4:** Not drawing the circuit diagram (always draw it — easy 2 marks). **(Lose 2 marks)**
> - **Most common mark-loss area #5:** Derivation mistakes in the imaginary-part-equals-zero step. **(Lose 2–3 marks)**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**A.** (a) Draw the circuit diagram of an **RC phase shift oscillator using op-amp 741** and explain its working. **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the condition for sustained oscillations. Show that the gain must be at least 29. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Circuit Diagram:**

The circuit consists of an op-amp 741 in **inverting configuration** with:
- Input resistor $R_1$ between the inverting input (pin 2) and ground.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ between the output (pin 6) and inverting input.
- Non-inverting input (pin 3) connected to ground.
- **Three RC sections** in the feedback path from output to inverting input.

```
        R1
  o----/\/\/\----o
                  |
                  |  Pin 2 (-)
  +Vcc (7)         \  
  -Vee (4)    +----[ Op-Amp 741 ]----+---- Output (6) ----o----> Sine wave
                /                     |
  Pin 3 (+) ---+                      |
                \                     |
                 +---------------------+
                 |
                 R   C       R   C       R   C
       o----/\/\/--||--/\/\/--||--/\/\/--||----o
                                                  
        (Output)                              (Pin 2, inverting input)
        Three identical RC sections in cascade
```

**Working Explanation:**

1. At power-on, thermal noise in the resistors and op-amp input stage contains all frequencies. This noise acts as the "seed" signal. *(1 Mark)*
2. The op-amp amplifies this noise. The **inverting configuration** introduces a 180° phase shift. *(1 Mark)*
3. The **RC ladder network** selectively passes only the frequency $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ with 180° phase lag. *(1 Mark)*
4. Total phase shift = 180° (op-amp) + 180° (RC network) = 360° → positive feedback. *(1 Mark)*
5. The amplitude builds up exponentially until non-linearities in the op-amp limit the growth, and a **stable sine wave** is established at the output. *(1 Mark)*
6. Steady-state amplitude is maintained when $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, i.e., when the loop gain is exactly unity. *(1 Mark)*
7. Frequency of oscillation is set by R and C values: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$. *(1 Mark)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

The complete derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student must write:

1. The feedback factor $\beta(j\omega)$ for the three-stage RC ladder:
$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{1}{(1 - 5\omega^2R^2C^2) + j(6\omega RC - \omega^3 R^3 C^3)}$$ *(1 Mark)*

2. Phase condition: imaginary part = 0:
$$6\omega RC - \omega^3 R^3 C^3 = 0 \implies \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$$ *(2 Marks)*

3. Frequency of oscillation:
$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}} \text{ Hz}$$ *(1 Mark)*

4. Magnitude of $\beta$ at $\omega_0$:
$$\vert\beta\vert = \frac{1}{29}$$ *(1 Mark)*

5. Gain condition from Barkhausen:
$$\vert A\beta\vert = 1 \implies \vert A\vert = 29$$ *(1 Mark)*

6. Final expression:
$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} \geq 29$$ *(1 Mark)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**B.** (a) Draw the circuit diagram of a **Wien Bridge oscillator using op-amp 741** and explain its working. **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the minimum gain required for sustained oscillations. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Circuit Diagram:**

The circuit uses an op-amp 741 in **non-inverting configuration**:
- Resistors $R_1$ and $R_f$ form the negative feedback path from output to inverting input (pin 2).
- The **Wien Bridge network** is connected between the output and the non-inverting input (pin 3).
- The Wien network consists of a **series RC arm** ($R_s$ and $C_s$) and a **parallel RC arm** ($R_p$ and $C_p$).

```
                       Rf
          +-----------/\/\/\--------+
          |                         |
          |    Pin 2 (-)            |
   Pin 3  +o----[ Op-Amp 741 ]----+---- Output (6) ----o
   (+)   /                         |                   |
         /                          |                   |
        /  Series RC   Parallel RC  |                   |
       o---/\/\/---+----/\/\/---+  |                   |
              |    |        |   |  |                   |
              C    C        R   R  |                   |
              |    |        |   |  |                   |
              =    =        =   =  |                   |
              |    |        |   |  |                   |
             GND  GND      GND GND |                   |
                                                  R1
                                  +-----------/\/\/\---+
                                  |                    |
                                  +---- Pin 2 --------+
                                                      
                                  +---- Pin 3 --------+
                                  |  (Wien Network output)
```

**Working Explanation:**

1. The Wien bridge network is a **frequency-selective positive feedback** element. It consists of a series RC arm and a parallel RC arm. *(1 Mark)*
2. At the resonant frequency $f_0$, the network's output is **in phase** with the input (zero phase shift) and maximum in amplitude. *(1 Mark)*
3. The op-amp is in **non-inverting configuration**, providing 0° phase shift with gain $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$. *(1 Mark)*
4. The total loop phase is 0° + 0° = 0°, satisfying the Barkhausen phase condition. *(1 Mark)*
5. The negative feedback through $R_f$ and $R_1$ sets the gain to maintain oscillation; the positive feedback from the Wien network provides the regenerative action. *(1 Mark)*
6. **Amplitude stabilization** is achieved using a non-linear element (e.g., back-to-back Zener diodes or a JFET) in the negative feedback path to dynamically control the gain. *(1 Mark)*
7. Frequency of oscillation: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ Hz. *(1 Mark)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

The complete derivation is given in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The student must write:

1. The impedances of the two arms:
$$Z_1 = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}, \quad Z_2 = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ *(1 Mark)*

2. The feedback factor:
$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$ *(2 Marks)*

3. Phase condition (imaginary part = 0):
$$\omega_0 RC - \frac{1}{\omega_0 RC} = 0 \implies \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}$$ *(2 Marks)*

4. Frequency of oscillation:
$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \text{ Hz}$$ *(1 Mark)*

5. Minimum gain requirement:
$$\beta = \frac{1}{3}, \quad A\beta = 1 \implies A = 3 \implies R_f = 2R_1$$ *(1 Mark)*

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (MUST MEMORIZE)**

### ⭐ Core Concepts
- [ ] **Barkhausen Criterion:** $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$ AND $\angle A\beta = 0°$ (or $n \times 360°$).
- [ ] **RC Phase Shift** uses **inverting** op-amp; **Wien Bridge** uses **non-inverting** op-amp.
- [ ] RC Phase Shift has **three** RC sections; Wien Bridge has **two** RC arms.

### ⭐ Key Magic Numbers
- [ ] RC Phase Shift: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$; minimum gain = **29**; $\beta = \dfrac{1}{29}$.
- [ ] Wien Bridge: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$; minimum gain = **3**; $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$; $R_f = 2R_1$.

### ⭐ Phase Analysis
- [ ] RC Phase Shift total phase: $180° \text{ (op-amp)} + 180° \text{ (network)} = 360°$.
- [ ] Wien Bridge total phase: $0° \text{ (op-amp)} + 0° \text{ (network)} = 0°$.
- [ ] Each RC section of the phase shift network contributes **60°** at $f_0$.

### ⭐ Practical Lab Points
- [ ] Use **op-amp IC 741** (or any general-purpose op-amp like µA741, LM741).
- [ ] Power supply: **±15V DC** dual supply.
- [ ] Observe the output on a **CRO** (Cathode Ray Oscilloscope) — verify sinusoidal waveform.
- [ ] Measure the **frequency** of oscillation using the CRO and compare with the theoretical value.
- [ ] For Wien Bridge, the **amplitude stabilization circuit** (Zener diodes, JFET, or incandescent lamp) is critical to prevent saturation/clipping.
- [ ] **Design trade-off:** For RC Phase Shift, choose $R_f$ slightly greater than $29R_1$ (e.g., $30R_1$) to ensure reliable start-up of oscillation.
- [ ] **Decoupling capacitors** (0.1 µF) should be placed close to the power supply pins to prevent high-frequency noise.

### ⭐ Common Mistakes to Avoid
- [ ] Don't confuse the formula $\sqrt{6}$ in RC Phase Shift with anything else.
- [ ] Don't write $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ for RC Phase Shift or $\dfrac{1}{29}$ for Wien Bridge.
- [ ] Always state Barkhausen criterion **before** starting the derivation.
- [ ] Always include the **circuit diagram** — it carries easy marks (typically 2 marks).
- [ ] Mention **amplitude stabilization** for the Wien Bridge — it is a frequently asked sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Op Amp Oscillator - LC Oscillators - Colpitts or Hartley Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL308 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Op Amp Oscillator - LC Oscillators - Colpitts or Hartley Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of LC Oscillators

An **LC Oscillator** is a sinusoidal signal generator that uses a **tank circuit** (a parallel combination of an inductor **L** and a capacitor **C**) as the frequency-selective feedback network. When augmented with an active amplifying element such as an **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)**, the circuit converts DC power from the supply rails into a sustained AC sinusoidal output without any external AC input signal. The tank circuit determines the frequency of oscillation, while the active device compensates for the energy lost in the resistive elements of the circuit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An oscillator is an electronic circuit that converts DC energy into AC energy without any external triggering signal. The output frequency is determined by the energy storage elements (L and C) in the feedback network.

The two most prominent LC oscillator topologies are:
- **Colpitts Oscillator** — Uses a **capacitive voltage divider** (two capacitors in series, $C_1$ and $C_2$, with the junction grounded) and a single inductor.
- **Hartley Oscillator** — Uses an **inductive voltage divider** (two inductors in series, $L_1$ and $L_2$, with the junction grounded) and a single capacitor.

Both are **negative-resistance-sustaining oscillators** where the Op-Amp provides the necessary loop gain to overcome losses and maintain steady oscillations as dictated by the **Barkhausen Criterion**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **child on a swing**. The swing is the oscillating system, gravity provides the restoring force, and the child occasionally pumps their legs to add just enough energy to replace what is lost to air drag and friction. If the child pumps too little, the swing dies down; pump too much, the swing goes wild. Only at the *exact* right pumping rate (timing) and amplitude does the swing maintain a steady, continuous arc.

In an LC oscillator:
- The **L and C tank** is the swing — it stores and exchanges energy between magnetic (inductor) and electric (capacitor) fields.
- The **resistors and parasitic losses** are the air drag that dissipates energy.
