- The **Op-Amp** is the child — it injects a precisely timed "kick" of energy (amplification) to exactly compensate for the losses.
- The **tank's natural resonant frequency** decides how fast the swing oscillates, just like how a heavier pendulum swings slower.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics (always bolded for KTU Board Examinations):**
> - **Frequency of Oscillation ($f_o$):** Measured in **Hertz (Hz)**
> - **Inductance ($L$):** Measured in **Henries (H)**
> - **Capacitance ($C$):** Measured in **Farads (F)**
> - **Loop Gain ($A\beta$):** Dimensionless ratio
> - **Quality Factor ($Q$):** Dimensionless — measures frequency selectivity of the tank

## 1.4 Why Op-Amp Based LC Oscillators?

The Op-Amp version of Colpitts/Hartley oscillators (as opposed to transistor versions) offers several pedagogical and practical advantages that make it ideal for the **PCEEL308 Analog Electronics Lab**:

1. **High Input Impedance** — The Op-Amp's near-infinite input impedance means it does not load down the resonant tank circuit, allowing the theoretical resonant frequency to closely match the actual measured value.
2. **Low Output Impedance** — Provides a clean, buffered sinusoidal output to measurement instruments (CRO, frequency counter).
3. **Tunable Gain** — Feedback resistors ($R_f$ and $R_{in}$) can be easily swapped to set the exact loop gain marginally above unity.
4. **DC Stability** — Easier bias point stabilization compared to BJT-based designs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Barkhausen Criterion — The Heart of Every Oscillator

Every sinusoidal oscillator (Colpitts, Hartley, RC Phase Shift, Wien Bridge, Crystal) must satisfy the **Barkhausen Criterion** for sustained, self-starting oscillations:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Barkhausen Criterion (Two Conditions):**
> 1. **Loop Gain Magnitude:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ (the product of amplifier gain $A$ and feedback factor $\beta$ must equal unity)
> 2. **Loop Phase Shift:** The total phase shift around the loop must be **$0°$ or an integer multiple of $360°$** (i.e., the feedback signal must be in phase with the input to be regenerative)

If $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$, the amplitude grows until non-linearities in the Op-Amp (saturation) clip the waveform. If $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$, the oscillation decays to zero. Practical design aims for $\vert A\beta \vert = 1.01$ to $1.05$ for **reliable startup**.

## 2.2 Colpitts Oscillator — Operational Breakdown

The **Colpitts Oscillator** uses a capacitive tap to provide the regenerative feedback. The tank circuit consists of an inductor $L$ in parallel with the **series combination of $C_1$ and $C_2$**.

### 2.2.1 Logical Operation Steps
- **Step 1:** The Op-Amp is configured as a **non-inverting amplifier** (using $R_f$ and $R_{in}$) — this provides the necessary gain $A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$.
- **Step 2:** The output of the Op-Amp drives the tank circuit. The junction between $C_1$ and $C_2$ is connected back to the **inverting input** of the Op-Amp.
- **Step 3:** The capacitive divider $\frac{C_1}{C_2}$ forms the feedback factor $\beta$ and provides the required phase inversion (180°) from output to inverting input.
- **Step 4:** The non-inverting input is grounded, completing the loop.
- **Step 5:** At resonance, the tank presents a **purely resistive impedance** (XL and XC cancel), so the feedback is exactly in phase.
- **Step 6:** The Op-Amp supplies the missing energy (active device) to compensate for the resistive losses, sustaining oscillations.

### 2.2.2 Colpitts Frequency Derivation Logic
At resonance, the reactances of $C_1$ and $C_2$ in series must equal the reactance of the inductor $L$ in parallel.

The **equivalent capacitance** of two capacitors in series is:
$$C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 \cdot C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$$

Then the standard LC tank formula applies:
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}}$$

## 2.3 Hartley Oscillator — Operational Breakdown

The **Hartley Oscillator** is the dual of the Colpitts — it uses an **inductive tap** (autotransformer action between $L_1$ and $L_2$) for feedback, with a single tuning capacitor $C$ in parallel.

### 2.3.1 Logical Operation Steps
- **Step 1:** Op-Amp configured as a non-inverting amplifier.
- **Step 2:** The tank circuit has $L_1$, $L_2$ (in series) in parallel with capacitor $C$.
- **Step 3:** The junction between $L_1$ and $L_2$ is grounded, and one end is fed back to the Op-Amp's input.
- **Step 4:** The inductive divider forms the feedback factor $\beta = \frac{L_1}{L_2}$ (or similar ratio, depending on topology).
- **Step 5:** At resonance, energy oscillates between the magnetic field of the inductors and the electric field of the capacitor.

### 2.3.2 Hartley Frequency Derivation Logic
The **equivalent inductance** of two inductors in series (with mutual coupling $M$) is:
$$L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$$
*(If no mutual coupling, simply $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2$)*

The frequency is:
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_{eq} \cdot C}}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is essential for KTU 2024 ESE preparation — every formula is fair game.**

| **Parameter** | **Colpitts Oscillator** | **Hartley Oscillator** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency of Oscillation** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}}$ where $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_{eq} \cdot C}}$ where $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$ | Hz (Henries, Farads) |
| **Feedback Fraction ($\beta$)** | $\beta = \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$ | $\beta = \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}$ | Dimensionless |
| **Loop Gain Condition** | $A\beta \geq 1 \Rightarrow \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right) \cdot \dfrac{C_1}{C_2} \geq 1$ | $A\beta \geq 1 \Rightarrow \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right) \cdot \dfrac{L_1}{L_2} \geq 1$ | $A$ is non-inverting gain |
| **Minimum Gain Required** | $A_{min} = \dfrac{C_2}{C_1}$ | $A_{min} = \dfrac{L_2}{L_1}$ | For sustained oscillation |
| **Quality Factor (Q)** | $Q = \dfrac{1}{R_s}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C_{eq}}}$ | $Q = \dfrac{1}{R_s}\sqrt{\dfrac{L_{eq}}{C}}$ | $R_s$ = series resistance of tank |
| **Waveform Type** | Sinusoidal | Sinusoidal | Both produce sine waves |
| **Frequency Range (Typical)** | 1 MHz to 500 MHz | 100 kHz to 100 MHz | RF applications |
| **Tuning Element** | Variable $C_1$ or $C_2$ | Variable $C$ or slug in $L$ | Used in radio receivers |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

LC oscillators are the workhorses of **Radio Frequency (RF) engineering**:
- **Colpitts**: Found in **FM transmitters**, signal generators, local oscillators in super-heterodyne radio receivers (e.g., 10.7 MHz IF stages).
- **Hartley**: Used in **AM broadcast transmitters**, radio receivers (older designs), and as the local oscillator in simple TRF (Tuned Radio Frequency) receivers.
- **Modern IC versions**: Colpitts is preferred on-chip because **inductors are bulky and lossy on silicon**, so minimizing inductors to one makes CMOS integration easier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Tip:** Colpitts is generally preferred for **stable, high-frequency** applications because capacitors have better temperature coefficients and Q factors than inductors at high frequencies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Colpitts Oscillator Frequency

We begin by considering the **tank circuit** of the Op-Amp Colpitts oscillator. The inductor $L$ is in parallel with the series combination of $C_1$ and $C_2$. We want to find the **frequency at which the imaginary part of the tank's net admittance equals zero** (resonance condition).

### Step 1: Define the Impedances
The impedance of the inductor is:
$$Z_L = j\omega L$$

The impedance of the series capacitor pair $C_1$ and $C_2$ is:
$$Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C_1} + \frac{1}{j\omega C_2}$$

### Step 2: Simplify the Series Capacitor Impedance
Factor out the common $\frac{1}{j\omega}$:
$$Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega}\left(\frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2}\right) = \frac{1}{j\omega}\left(\frac{C_1 + C_2}{C_1 C_2}\right)$$

Rewriting in standard capacitor form:
$$Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C_{eq}}$$

where we identify the **equivalent series capacitance**:
$$C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$$

This is a **textbook result** for two capacitors in series — when $C_1 = C_2 = C$, then $C_{eq} = \frac{C}{2}$, which doubles the voltage division effect.

### Step 3: Apply the Parallel Resonance Condition
For a parallel LC tank, the resonance condition requires the **net susceptance to vanish**. Using admittances:
$$Y_{total} = Y_L + Y_C = \frac{1}{j\omega L} + j\omega C_{eq}$$

Setting the imaginary part of the admittance to zero (which is equivalent to setting the imaginary part of the impedance to infinity — i.e., the tank acts as an open circuit):
$$\omega_o C_{eq} - \frac{1}{\omega_o L} = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve for $\omega_o$
Multiply through by $\omega_o L$:
$$\omega_o^2 L C_{eq} - 1 = 0$$

Add 1 to both sides:
$$\omega_o^2 L C_{eq} = 1$$

Divide by $LC_{eq}$:
$$\omega_o^2 = \frac{1}{L C_{eq}}$$

Take the positive square root (frequency must be positive):
$$\omega_o = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L C_{eq}}}$$

### Step 5: Convert Angular Frequency to Hz
Use the relation $\omega_o = 2\pi f_o$, so:
$$2\pi f_o = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L C_{eq}}}$$

Solving for $f_o$:
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L C_{eq}}}$$

### Step 6: Substitute $C_{eq}$ in Terms of $C_1$ and $C_2$
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}}}$$

Pull the fraction out of the square root as $\sqrt{\frac{C_1+C_2}{C_1 C_2}}$:
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{C_1 + C_2}{L \cdot C_1 C_2}}$$

This is the **final, exam-ready expression** for the Colpitts frequency.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Colpitts Loop Gain Condition

The Op-Amp is in **non-inverting configuration** with gain:
$$A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$

The capacitive divider $C_1$ and $C_2$ acts as the feedback network. The feedback factor $\beta$ is the ratio of the voltage across $C_2$ to the voltage across the series combination:
$$\beta = \frac{V_{C_2}}{V_{out}} = \frac{\frac{1}{j\omega C_2}}{\frac{1}{j\omega C_1} + \frac{1}{j\omega C_2}}$$

Simplify by multiplying numerator and denominator by $j\omega C_1 C_2$:
$$\beta = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2}$$

For sustained oscillations (Barkhausen):
$$A\beta \geq 1$$

Substituting:
$$\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right) \cdot \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2} \geq 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** In Colpitts, the feedback is **in phase** with the output (no phase inversion through the tank at resonance). Hence, a **non-inverting** Op-Amp configuration is used, and the *total loop phase shift is zero* (0° from Op-Amp + 0° from tank = 0°).

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Hartley Oscillator Frequency

Following the dual structure of the Colpitts derivation:

### Step 1: Series Inductor Combination
Two inductors $L_1$ and $L_2$ in series have equivalent inductance:
$$L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$$
where $M$ is the mutual inductance between $L_1$ and $L_2$. For a **tapped inductor** (autotransformer), $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ where $k$ is the coupling coefficient.

If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are **physically separate and well-spaced**, $M \approx 0$:
$$L_{eq} \approx L_1 + L_2$$

### Step 2: Apply Parallel Resonance with $C$
The tank now has $L_{eq}$ in parallel with $C$. By identical logic to Colpitts:
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_{eq} \cdot C}}$$

### Step 3: Substitute
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M) \cdot C}}$$

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Hartley Loop Gain Condition

The inductive divider has feedback factor:
$$\beta = \frac{L_1}{L_1 + L_2}$$

For the Op-Amp non-inverting amplifier:
$$\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right) \cdot \frac{L_1}{L_1 + L_2} \geq 1$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem:** A Colpitts oscillator uses $L = 100\ \mu H$, $C_1 = 1\ nF$, and $C_2 = 4\ nF$. Find the frequency of oscillation and the required minimum gain.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute $C_{eq}$**
$$C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-9})(4 \times 10^{-9})}{(1 \times 10^{-9}) + (4 \times 10^{-9})}$$

$$C_{eq} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-18}}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.8 \times 10^{-9}\ F = 0.8\ nF$$

**Step 2: Compute $f_o$**
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(100 \times 10^{-6})(0.8 \times 10^{-9})}}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{8 \times 10^{-14}}}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (2.828 \times 10^{-7})}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{1.777 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 562.7\ kHz$$

**Step 3: Minimum Gain Required**
$$A_{min} = \frac{C_2}{C_1} = \frac{4}{1} = 4$$

So we need $\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right) \geq 4$, meaning $\frac{R_f}{R_{in}} \geq 3$. A safe choice: $R_f = 30\ k\Omega$ and $R_{in} = 10\ k\Omega$.

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| **Property** | **Colpitts** | **Hartley** |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency determining element pair | $L$ and $(C_1 \parallel C_2)$ series | $C$ and $(L_1 \text{ series } L_2)$ |
| Feedback element | Capacitive divider | Inductive divider |
| Feedback expression | $\beta = C_1/(C_1 + C_2)$ | $\beta = L_1/(L_1 + L_2)$ |
| Frequency expression | $1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC_{eq}})$ | $1/(2\pi\sqrt{L_{eq}C})$ |
| Frequency stability | **Better** (capacitors stable) | **Lower** (inductors drift) |
| Suitability for high frequency | **Excellent** (MHz–GHz) | Good (up to ~100 MHz) |
| On-chip integration | **Easier** (one inductor) | Harder (two inductors) |
| Stray capacitance sensitivity | Low (capacitors are dominant) | Higher (stray C alters freq) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Colpitts Oscillator Architecture

The following block-level functional flow diagram illustrates the **signal flow and feedback loop** of an Op-Amp based Colpitts oscillator. Note the alphanumeric node IDs and clean labels (no markdown formatting inside node labels).

```mermaid
graph TD
    DC[DC Supply VCC and VEE] --> A1[Op-Amp UA741 Power Pins]
    Vplus[Non-Inverting Input Pin 3] --> GND1[Ground Reference 0V]
    A1 --> Vout[Output Pin 6 Sinusoidal Waveform]
    Vout --> TankA[Tank Circuit Inductor L]
    Vout --> TankB[Tank Circuit Capacitor C1]
    TankA --> TankC[Junction Node Between C1 and C2]
    TankB --> TankC
    TankC --> TankD[Capacitor C2 to Ground]
    TankC --> FbPath[Feedback Path to Pin 2 Inverting Input]
    FbPath --> A1
    A1 --> GainCtrl[Gain Set by Rf and Rin]
    GainCtrl --> Vout
    Vout --> CRO[Output to CRO and Frequency Counter]
    Vout --> Freq[Output Frequency fo in kHz]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hartley Oscillator Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    DC[DC Supply VCC and VEE] --> A1[Op-Amp UA741 Power Pins]
    Vplus[Non-Inverting Input Pin 3] --> GND1[Ground Reference 0V]
    A1 --> Vout[Output Pin 6 Sinusoidal Waveform]
    Vout --> IndA[Tank Inductor L1]
    Vout --> CapA[Tank Capacitor C]
    IndA --> Junct[Junction Node Between L1 and L2]
    Junct --> GND2[Grounded Center Tap]
    Junct --> IndB[Inductor L2 to Ground]
    CapA --> GND2
    Junct --> FbPath[Feedback to Pin 2 Inverting Input]
    FbPath --> A1
    A1 --> GainCtrl[Gain Set by Rf and Rin]
    GainCtrl --> Vout
    Vout --> CRO[Output to CRO and Frequency Counter]
    Vout --> Freq[Output Frequency fo in kHz]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Description

> [!NOTE]
> **Colpitts Signal Flow Trace:**
> 1. Op-Amp output drives the top of the LC tank.
> 2. The tank (L in parallel with the series $C_1 C_2$ pair) selects the resonant frequency.
> 3. The junction of $C_1$ and $C_2$ is fed back to the inverting input.
> 4. The non-inverting input is grounded — this gives a 180° phase inversion through the Op-Amp, which is then canceled by the 180° inversion through the capacitive divider at resonance.
> 5. Net loop phase = 0°, satisfying Barkhausen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hartley Signal Flow Trace:**
> 1. Op-Amp output drives the top of the LC tank.
> 2. The tank (C in parallel with the series $L_1 L_2$ pair) selects the resonant frequency.
> 3. The junction of $L_1$ and $L_2$ is grounded, and the feedback is taken from one end.
> 4. The inductive divider provides the required 180° phase inversion at resonance.
> 5. Net loop phase = 0° (Barkhausen satisfied).

## 4.4 Circuit Topology Description (Block-Level View)

In the absence of a literal schematic drawing tool, the following is a **textual layout** of the typical breadboard wiring for a Colpitts Op-Amp oscillator (used in the KTU PCEEL308 lab):

```
            +VCC
             |
             |
           [Pin 7]  Op-Amp (UA741 / LM741)
            ___      
    Rin -->|   \
           | A  |--- Pin 6 (Output) -----> To CRO Ch1
    Vcc/2->|   /                          |
           |___/                           |
            |   |                           |
          [Pin 3] [Pin 2]                   |
            |     |                         |
           GND    |                         |
                   \                        |
                    \                       |
                     +---> [C1] ---+---> [C2] --- GND
                            |        
                            +---> [L] --- GND
                            |
                            +----------> Pin 2 (Feedback)
```

The center-tap (junction of $C_1$ and $C_2$) is the **feedback point** that returns to the inverting input (Pin 2). The non-inverting input (Pin 3) is held at a mid-supply bias (typically $V_{CC}/2$) to maximize Op-Amp output swing and prevent clipping asymmetry.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an Oscillator. State the Barkhausen Criterion.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
An oscillator is an electronic circuit that generates a periodic waveform (typically sinusoidal) from a DC supply source without requiring any external AC input signal. It uses an amplifying element (such as a transistor or Op-Amp) along with a frequency-selective feedback network.

The **Barkhausen Criterion** for sustained sinusoidal oscillation requires two conditions to be simultaneously satisfied:
1. **Magnitude condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must equal unity, i.e., $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$.
2. **Phase condition:** The total phase shift around the closed feedback loop must be $0°$ or an integer multiple of $360°$, i.e., $\angle A\beta = 2n\pi$ where $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$

In practice, $\vert A\beta \vert$ is set slightly greater than 1 (typically 1.01 to 1.05) to guarantee oscillation startup despite component tolerances.

### Question 2: Compare Colpitts and Hartley Oscillators.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Colpitts** | **Hartley** |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback network | Capacitive divider ($C_1, C_2$) | Inductive divider ($L_1, L_2$) |
| Frequency formula | $f_o = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC_{eq}})$ | $f_o = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{L_{eq}C})$ |
| Frequency stability | Higher (capacitors are more stable) | Lower |
| Typical frequency range | 1 MHz to 500 MHz | 100 kHz to 100 MHz |
| Number of inductors | 1 (single $L$) | 2 (or 1 tapped $L$) |
| Tuning ease | Easy (variable $C$) | Moderate (slug tuning) |
| IC integration | Preferred | Less preferred |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 2 Internal Choice Set 1]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a Colpitts oscillator using an Op-Amp. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation.

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:**
The Op-Amp Colpitts oscillator consists of an Op-Amp (such as $\mu$A741) configured in the **non-inverting mode** with gain $A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$. The output of the Op-Amp is connected to a tank circuit comprising an inductor $L$ in parallel with a capacitive voltage divider formed by $C_1$ and $C_2$ in series. The junction between $C_1$ and $C_2$ is connected back to the inverting input (Pin 2) of the Op-Amp. The non-inverting input (Pin 3) is grounded (or held at $V_{CC}/2$ for DC bias).

**Operation:**
- The Op-Amp provides the necessary amplification to compensate for losses in the tank.
- At resonance, the tank presents a **purely resistive impedance** (the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel).
- The capacitive divider provides **180° phase shift** at resonance, while the non-inverting Op-Amp provides **0° phase shift** — the total loop phase shift is **360° (or 0°)**, satisfying Barkhausen.
- The feedback factor is $\beta = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2}$.
- For sustained oscillation, $A\beta \geq 1$, giving $1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}} \geq \frac{C_1 + C_2}{C_1}$.

**Frequency Derivation:**
The equivalent capacitance of $C_1$ and $C_2$ in series:
$$C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$$

At parallel resonance:
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{C_1 + C_2}{L C_1 C_2}}$$

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** In a Colpitts oscillator, $C_1 = 0.01\ \mu F$, $C_2 = 0.04\ \mu F$, and $L = 10\ mH$. Calculate the frequency of oscillation and the minimum gain required.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $C_{eq}$:
$$C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2} = \frac{(0.01)(0.04)}{0.01 + 0.04} = \frac{0.0004}{0.05} = 0.008\ \mu F = 8\ nF$$

**Step 2:** Substitute in the frequency formula:
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(10 \times 10^{-3})(8 \times 10^{-9})}}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{8 \times 10^{-11}}}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (8.944 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{5.620 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 17.79\ kHz$$

**Step 3:** Minimum gain required:
$$A_{min} = \frac{C_2}{C_1} = \frac{0.04}{0.01} = 4$$

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 2 Internal Choice Set 2]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the working of a Hartley oscillator using an Op-Amp with a neat circuit diagram. Derive its frequency of oscillation expression.

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:**
An Op-Amp Hartley oscillator uses an inductive voltage divider as the feedback network. The tank circuit consists of two inductors $L_1$ and $L_2$ in series (forming $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$) in parallel with a single tuning capacitor $C$. The junction between $L_1$ and $L_2$ is connected to ground (or returned to the inverting input depending on topology). The Op-Amp is in non-inverting mode with gain $A = 1 + R_f/R_{in}$.

**Operation:**
- The inductive divider provides the feedback fraction $\beta = L_1/(L_1 + L_2)$.
- At resonance, the inductive divider introduces a 180° phase shift, which combines with the 0° shift of the non-inverting Op-Amp to give 360° total.
- The Op-Amp replenishes the energy lost in the tank resistance.

**Frequency Derivation:**
The equivalent inductance of the series combination:
$$L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$$
(For non-coupled coils, $M = 0$ and $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2$.)

Applying the parallel LC resonance condition:
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_{eq} \cdot C}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M) \cdot C}}$$

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** In a Hartley oscillator, $L_1 = 1\ mH$, $L_2 = 4\ mH$, $M = 0.5\ mH$ (mutual inductance), and $C = 100\ pF$. Find the frequency of oscillation.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $L_{eq}$:
$$L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + 2M = 1 + 4 + 2(0.5) = 6\ mH$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(6 \times 10^{-3})(100 \times 10^{-12})}}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{6 \times 10^{-13}}}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (7.746 \times 10^{-7})}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{4.867 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 205.5\ kHz$$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Examinations:**
> 1. **Confusing $A_{min}$ for Colpitts vs. Hartley:** In Colpitts, $A_{min} = C_2/C_1$ (capacitor ratio). In Hartley, $A_{min} = L_2/L_1$ (inductor ratio). Students often mix these up and lose 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Mutual Inductance term ($2M$):** In Hartley, the full equivalent inductance is $L_1 + L_2 + 2M$, NOT just $L_1 + L_2$. Even if $M$ is small, the examiner expects the formula to include it.
> 3. **Wrong series combination formula:** Two capacitors in series give $C_{eq} = C_1 C_2/(C_1 + C_2)$, not $C_1 + C_2$. Two inductors in series give $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$, not $L_1 L_2/(L_1 + L_2)$. This is a 2-mark trap.
> 4. **Phase shift confusion:** Some students write "Colpitts uses inverting amplifier." This is WRONG for the Op-Amp version. The capacitive divider provides the 180° phase shift, so the Op-Amp is **non-inverting**.
> 5. **Not stating Barkhausen Criterion explicitly:** Examiners in KTU 2024 specifically look for the **Barkhausen Criterion** statement (both magnitude and phase conditions) in any oscillator question. Omitting it costs 1-2 marks.
> 6. **Unit errors:** $L$ in mH must be converted to H, $C$ in $\mu F$ to F before plugging into the formula. KTU examiners are strict about SI units.
> 7. **Bode plot / phase margin missing:** For the lab record, students should also mention the **concept of marginal stability** and how the oscillator is "on the verge of instability" (gain margin = 0 dB, phase margin = 0°).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE & Lab Viva:**

- [ ] **Barkhausen Criterion** is the **soul** of every oscillator — both magnitude ($\vert A\beta \vert = 1$) and phase ($0°$ or $360°$) conditions are mandatory.
- [ ] **Colpitts** uses **two capacitors + one inductor** in the tank; feedback is taken from the capacitor junction.
- [ ] **Hartley** uses **two inductors + one capacitor** in the tank; feedback is taken from the inductor junction.
- [ ] **Colpitts frequency:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}}}$ — memorize this verbatim.
- [ ] **Hartley frequency:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M) \cdot C}}$ — include the $2M$ term.
- [ ] **Minimum gain** for Colpitts = $C_2/C_1$; for Hartley = $L_2/L_1$.
- [ ] **Op-Amp is always in non-inverting mode** for both oscillators.
- [ ] **Practical design rule:** Set $A\beta \approx 1.02$ to $1.05$ for **reliable startup** and clean sinusoidal output.
- [ ] **Colpitts is preferred** for **higher frequencies** and **better frequency stability** — this is a frequently asked viva question.
- [ ] **Lab skills to demonstrate:** (1) Identify pins of Op-Amp IC on breadboard, (2) Wire the tank circuit, (3) Observe clean sine wave on CRO, (4) Measure frequency with frequency counter, (5) Compare measured $f_o$ with theoretical $f_o$ and calculate % error.
- [ ] **Typical % error in lab** should be within **±5 to ±10%** — any larger discrepancy indicates wiring error or faulty components.
- [ ] **If no oscillation occurs:** Check (a) gain setting, (b) ground connections, (c) tank component values, (d) power supply polarity.
- [ ] **Distortion in waveform:** Means $A\beta$ is too high — increase $R_{in}$ or decrease $R_f$ to lower the gain closer to 1.
- [ ] **Frequency of oscillation depends ONLY on LC values** — not on the Op-Amp supply voltage or gain (as long as the loop is oscillating).
- [ ] **Remember to mention "non-inverting amplifier configuration"** in the circuit description for full marks in the KTU valuation key.
- [ ] **Mention application:** "Colpitts/Hartley oscillators are used as local oscillators in radio receivers, signal generators, and RF transmitters" for that extra 1 mark in the viva.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waveform generation - Square, triangular and saw tooth waveform generation using OPAMPs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL308 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Waveform generation - Square, triangular and saw tooth waveform generation using OPAMPs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Function generators** built using operational amplifiers are non-sinusoidal oscillator circuits that produce periodic waveforms such as **square waves, triangular waves, and sawtooth waves**. These circuits rely on the principle of regenerative (positive) feedback combined with RC timing networks, exploiting the op-amp's saturation behavior to produce stable, repetitive waveforms without any external input signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Square Wave Generator** — Also called an **Astable Multivibrator** (because it has NO stable state). The output continuously flips between two saturation levels ($+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$) at a rate determined by an RC network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Triangular Wave Generator** — Built by cascading a **Schmitt Trigger** (Comparator with hysteresis) followed by an **Integrator**. The comparator's square output is mathematically integrated to produce a linear ramp.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sawtooth Wave Generator** — A modified triangular wave generator where the rise time and fall time are deliberately made **unequal**, producing a sharp drop and slow rise (or vice-versa). Achieved using a constant current source, FET reset switch, or asymmetric RC charging paths.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bucket and the Spigot"

Imagine a water **bucket with a tap** (spigot) at the bottom. Water fills the bucket at a constant rate. Two **level sensors** are placed inside — one at a *high mark* and one at a *low mark*.

- When the water reaches the **high sensor** → a valve opens and the bucket drains INSTANTLY.
- When the water reaches the **low sensor** → the valve closes, and the bucket refills.
- A pen attached to the water level draws a **sawtooth pattern** (slow rise, sudden drop).

Now imagine two buckets working in **opposition** (one fills as the other empties):
- The plot of the FIRST bucket's water level is a **triangular wave** (slow up, slow down).
- The plot of the valve's open/closed state is a **square wave**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The triangular wave and the square wave are ALWAYS 90° out of phase. The square wave leads, the triangular wave follows (because integration adds a 90° phase lag). This is a high-yield exam point.

---

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Saturation Voltage ($V_{sat}$):** Typically **$\pm 13\text{ V}$** for op-amps powered with $\pm 15\text{ V}$ rails (or **$\pm 14\text{ V}$** for ideal models).
- **Frequency Range:** Audio (20 Hz – 20 kHz) up to a few hundred kHz for general-purpose op-amps like $\mu$A741.
- **Threshold Voltage ($\pm \beta V_{sat}$):** The reference levels that switch the comparator, controlled by the feedback resistor ratio.
- **Feedback Fraction ($\beta$):** $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ — the critical design parameter for square wave generators.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Square Wave Generator — Operational Logic (Astable Multivibrator)

The square wave generator uses a **single op-amp** configured as a **regenerative comparator** (Schmitt trigger) with an RC timing network at the inverting input.

**Step-by-step operation logic:**

1. **Initial assumption:** Suppose the output $V_o = +V_{sat}$ (positive saturation).
2. **Positive feedback path:** Through $R_1$ and $R_2$, a fraction $\beta V_o$ is fed back to the **non-inverting input** ($V^+ = +\beta V_{sat}$).
3. **RC charging path:** The capacitor $C$ charges through resistor $R$ towards $+V_{sat}$.
4. **Switching condition:** When the capacitor voltage $V_C$ (at the inverting input) just exceeds $V^+ = +\beta V_{sat}$, the op-amp output flips to $-V_{sat}$.
5. **Regenerative snap:** The positive feedback ensures the switch is **abrupt** (avalanche action) — this is the "snap action" of the Schmitt trigger.
6. **Reverse cycle:** Now $V^+ = -\beta V_{sat}$, and $C$ discharges through $R$ towards $-V_{sat}$ until $V_C$ reaches $-\beta V_{sat}$, then the cycle repeats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why positive feedback?** It forces the op-amp to operate ONLY in the saturation region (cut-off or saturation), NEVER in the linear region. This makes the output a true square wave, not a sine wave.

---

### 2.2 Triangular Wave Generator — Operational Logic (Two Op-Amp Configuration)

**Building block philosophy:** A triangular wave is the **time integral of a square wave**.

$$V_{tri}(t) = -\frac{1}{R_1 C} \int V_{sq}(t) \, dt$$

**Two op-amp topology:**

| Block | Op-Amp | Function | Output |
|-------|--------|----------|--------|
| Block 1 | $U_1$ | **Schmitt Trigger / Comparator** | Square wave ($\pm V_{sat}$) |
| Block 2 | $U_2$ | **Miller Integrator** | Triangular wave ($\pm V_{om}$) |

**Step-by-step operation:**

1. $U_1$ (comparator) generates $\pm V_{sat}$ based on whether integrator output exceeds the threshold $\pm \beta V_{sat}$.
2. $U_2$ integrates this constant square voltage: when $V_{o1} = +V_{sat}$, $V_{o2}$ ramps **downward linearly**; when $V_{o1} = -V_{sat}$, $V_{o2}$ ramps **upward linearly**.
3. The peak amplitude of the triangular wave is **self-limiting** — it is set by the Schmitt trigger threshold.

---

### 2.3 Sawtooth Wave Generator — Operational Logic

The sawtooth wave is a **deliberately asymmetric** version of the triangular wave. There are three principal methods:

**Method 1 — Asymmetric RC paths:**
- Replace the single resistor $R$ in the astable with **two different resistors** ($R_A$ for charging, $R_B$ for discharging) using steering diodes.

**Method 2 — Constant current charging (Miller Integrator with current source):**
- Replace the input resistor of the integrator with a **constant current source** (using a BJT/FET).
- The capacitor then charges at a **constant rate** $\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{I_{const}}{C}$ — producing a perfectly linear ramp.

**Method 3 — FET-reset integrator:**
- A JFET is placed across the integrator capacitor.
- When the output reaches the peak, the FET is pulsed ON, instantly discharging $C$.
- The result: **slow linear rise, abrupt fall** = sawtooth wave.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sawtooth applications:** Time-base generators in CROs, sweep circuits in radar, PWM modulators, ADC ramp generators. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically tests the design of these three waveforms.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Waveform | Key Formula | Symbol Definitions | Typical Use |
|---|----------|-------------|-------------------|------------|
| 1 | **Square Wave Frequency** | $f = \dfrac{1}{2RC \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}$ | $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Astable design |
| 2 | **Square Wave Period** | $T = 2RC \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$ | Same as above | Pulse generation |
| 3 | **Threshold Voltages** | $V_{TH} = \pm \beta V_{sat}$ | Switching levels | Comparator design |
| 4 | **Duty Cycle (symmetric)** | $D = 50\%$ (when $R$ is shared) | Equal charge/discharge paths | Clock generation |
| 5 | **Triangular Wave Frequency** | $f = \dfrac{R_2 + R_3}{4 R_1 R_3 C}$ | $R_1$ = integrator input resistor | Linear sweep |
| 6 | **Triangular Wave Peak Amplitude** | $V_{om} = \pm \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} V_{sat}$ | Set by feedback divider | Output swing limit |
| 7 | **Integrator Output** | $V_o(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R_1 C} \int V_{in} \, dt$ | Miller integration | Ramp generation |
| 8 | **Sawtooth Rise Time** | $T_r = \dfrac{V_{om} \cdot C}{I_{const}}$ | Linear ramp via constant current | CRO time-base |
| 9 | **Sawtooth Fall Time** | $T_f \approx R_{ON} \cdot C$ | FET switch ON-resistance | Reset time |
| 10 | **Comparator Hysteresis Width** | $V_H = 2\beta V_{sat}$ | Peak-to-peak switching range | Noise immunity |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **integrator input resistor** $R_1$ (in the triangular wave formula) with the **input resistor of the Schmitt trigger** $R_3$. These are different components in different positions in the circuit. Label clearly in your circuit diagrams!

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Square waves** → Digital clock signals, switching power supplies (SMPS), pulse-width modulation (PWM) control.
- **Triangular waves** → Frequency synthesizers, voltage-controlled oscillators (VCOs), function generator ICs (e.g., **ICL8038**, **XR-2206**).
- **Sawtooth waves** → Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO) horizontal sweep, television raster scanning, ADC ramp converters, audio synthesizers (sub-bass, strings).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Square Wave Frequency (Astable Multivibrator)

**Given Circuit:** Op-amp with positive feedback resistors $R_1$ (from output to $V^+$) and $R_2$ (from $V^+$ to ground). RC network at $V^-$ with $R$ in series and $C$ to ground.

**Step 1: Define the feedback fraction.**

$$\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2: Establish the non-inverting input voltage.**

When the op-amp output saturates at $+V_{sat}$, the voltage at the non-inverting terminal is:

$$V^+ = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat} = +\beta V_{sat}$$

**Step 3: Write the capacitor charging equation.**

The capacitor $C$ charges through $R$ from its initial value $-\beta V_{sat}$ (after the previous flip) towards the final value $+V_{sat}$:

$$V_C(t) = V_{sat} + (V_{C,0} - V_{sat}) e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 4: Apply the initial condition.**

Substituting $V_{C,0} = -\beta V_{sat}$:

$$V_C(t) = V_{sat} + (-\beta V_{sat} - V_{sat}) e^{-t/RC}$$

$$V_C(t) = V_{sat} - V_{sat}(1 + \beta) e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 5: Apply the switching condition.**

The output flips when $V_C(t) = +\beta V_{sat}$ (reaching the upper threshold). Let this time be $T/2$ (half-period due to symmetry):

$$\beta V_{sat} = V_{sat} - V_{sat}(1 + \beta) e^{-T/(2RC)}$$

**Step 6: Solve algebraically for the half-period.**

Rearranging:

$$\beta V_{sat} - V_{sat} = -V_{sat}(1 + \beta) e^{-T/(2RC)}$$

$$-V_{sat}(1 - \beta) = -V_{sat}(1 + \beta) e^{-T/(2RC)}$$

$$(1 - \beta) = (1 + \beta) e^{-T/(2RC)}$$

$$e^{-T/(2RC)} = \frac{1 - \beta}{1 + \beta}$$

**Step 7: Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$-\frac{T}{2RC} = \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \beta}{1 + \beta}\right) = -\ln\!\left(\frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right)$$

$$\frac{T}{2RC} = \ln\!\left(\frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right)$$

$$\boxed{T = 2RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right)}$$

**Step 8: Derive the frequency.**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2RC \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}$$

**Step 9: Simplification for the special case $\beta = 0.473$.**

A common design choice in textbooks (Roy Choudhury, Gayakwad) is to set $\beta = 0.473$, which yields:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1 + 0.473}{1 - 0.473}\right) = \ln(2.795) \approx 1.028 \approx 1$$

Therefore: $f \approx \dfrac{1}{2RC}$ — a useful **rule-of-thumb** for quick lab calculations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Triangular Wave Amplitude and Frequency

**Circuit Setup:** Two op-amps $U_1$ (Schmitt trigger) and $U_2$ (integrator). The output of $U_1$ feeds $R_1$ of the integrator. The output of $U_2$ feeds back to the non-inverting input of $U_1$ through $R_2$, with $R_3$ from $V^+$ of $U_1$ to ground.

**Step 1: Define the Schmitt trigger feedback ratio.**

$$\beta_1 = \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 2: Determine the Schmitt trigger switching thresholds.**

The output $V_{o1}$ of $U_1$ is $\pm V_{sat}$. The non-inverting input voltage of $U_1$ is a superposition:

$$V_{U1}^+ = \frac{R_3 \cdot V_{o1} + R_2 \cdot V_{o2}}{R_2 + R_3}$$

Switching occurs when $V_{U1}^+ = 0$ (the inverting input of $U_1$ is at virtual ground because $U_1$ is a comparator). Solving for $V_{o2}$ at the switching instant:

$$0 = \frac{R_3 \cdot V_{o1} + R_2 \cdot V_{o2,peak}}{R_2 + R_3}$$

$$V_{o2,peak} = -\frac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot V_{o1}$$

Taking magnitudes (since $V_{o1} = \pm V_{sat}$):

$$\boxed{V_{om} = \pm \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} V_{sat}}$$

**Step 3: Write the integrator output equation.**

Assume the integrator input $V_{o1} = +V_{sat}$ for the interval $[0, T/2]$. The integrator output is:

$$V_{o2}(t) = -\frac{1}{R_1 C} \int_0^t (+V_{sat}) \, dt' + V_{o2}(0)$$

$$V_{o2}(t) = V_{o2}(0) - \frac{V_{sat}}{R_1 C} \cdot t$$

**Step 4: Apply boundary conditions.**

The integrator output must swing from $+V_{om}$ to $-V_{om}$ during the half-period $T/2$:

$$-V_{om} = +V_{om} - \frac{V_{sat}}{R_1 C} \cdot \frac{T}{2}$$

**Step 5: Solve for the time period $T$.**

$$-2 V_{om} = -\frac{V_{sat} \cdot T}{2 R_1 C}$$

$$T = \frac{4 R_1 C \cdot V_{om}}{V_{sat}}$$

**Step 6: Substitute $V_{om}$ from Step 2.**

$$T = \frac{4 R_1 C}{V_{sat}} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \cdot V_{sat}$$

$$T = \frac{4 R_1 R_3 C}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 7: Final frequency formula.**

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{R_2 + R_3}{4 R_1 R_3 C}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Sawtooth Wave Generation (Using FET-Reset Integrator)

**Step 1: Set up the integrator with a FET reset switch.**

A JFET is connected across the integrator capacitor $C$. A periodic gate pulse turns the FET ON momentarily to reset $C$ to 0 V.

**Step 2: Constant current charging.**

If a constant current $I_{const}$ flows into the capacitor:

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = \frac{I_{const}}{C}$$

This is a **constant slope** — the defining characteristic of a sawtooth wave.

**Step 3: Time to reach peak voltage $V_{om}$.**

$$V_{om} = \frac{I_{const}}{C} \cdot T_r$$

$$T_r = \frac{V_{om} \cdot C}{I_{const}}$$

**Step 4: Reset time.**

When the FET is ON, it presents a small ON-resistance $r_{ds(on)}$ across $C$. The capacitor discharges through this resistance with a time constant $\tau = r_{ds(on)} \cdot C$:

$$T_f \approx 3 \cdot r_{ds(on)} \cdot C \quad \text{(for ~95\% discharge)}$$

**Step 5: Total period and frequency.**

$$T = T_r + T_f \approx \frac{V_{om} \cdot C}{I_{const}} + 3 r_{ds(on)} C$$

$$f = \frac{1}{T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design constraint for sawtooth waves:** $T_f \ll T_r$ (the fall time must be much smaller than the rise time, typically $< 1\%$). This is the **sawtooth quality factor**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Parameter | Square Wave | Triangular Wave | Sawtooth Wave |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Number of Op-Amps** | 1 | 2 | 1 or 2 |
| **Symmetry** | Symmetric (50% duty cycle) | Symmetric | **Asymmetric** |
| **Rise Time** | Very fast (snap action) | Linear | Linear (slow) |
| **Fall Time** | Very fast (snap action) | Linear | **Very fast (abrupt)** |
| **Key Building Block** | Schmitt trigger + RC | Schmitt + Integrator | Integrator + Reset switch |
| **Controlling Parameter** | $\beta = R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ | $R_1, R_2, R_3, C$ | $I_{const}$ and $r_{ds(on)}$ |
| **Duty Cycle** | 50% (ideal) | 50% | **Adjustable (not 50%)** |
| **Phase Relationship** | Leads by 90° | Lags square by 90° | Phase depends on reset trigger |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Square Wave Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Supply Plus Minus 15V] --> P1[Power to Op-Amp U1]
    P1 --> O1[Op-Amp in Saturation Region]
    O1 --> R1A[Feedback Resistor R1]
    O1 --> R1B[Output Resistor Path]
    R1A --> N1[Non-Inverting Input Vplus]
    R1B --> N2[Inverting Input Vminus]
    N2 --> RC1[RC Network Resistor R and Capacitor C]
    RC1 --> GND1[Ground Node]
    N1 --> R2A[Resistor R2 to Ground]
    R2A --> GND2[Ground Node]
    O1 --> OUT1[Square Wave Output Plus Minus Vsat]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333
    style OUT1 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333
    style O1 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333
```

**Description of flow:** The op-amp output is fed back positively through $R_1$–$R_2$ to the non-inverting input, and negatively through the $R$–$C$ network to the inverting input. The capacitor voltage $V_C$ is compared against $\beta V_{sat}$, and the snap-action comparator behavior produces the square wave.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Triangular Wave Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Supply Plus Minus 15V] --> P1[Power to Op-Amp U1 Comparator]
    A --> P2[Power to Op-Amp U2 Integrator]
    P1 --> U1[Schmitt Trigger U1]
    U1 --> SQ[Square Wave Output Plus Minus Vsat]
    SQ --> R1[Input Resistor R1 of Integrator]
    R1 --> N3[Inverting Input of U2]
    N3 --> CAP[Feedback Capacitor C of Integrator]
    CAP --> OUT2[Triangular Wave Output]
    P2 --> U2[Miller Integrator U2]
    U2 --> OUT2
    OUT2 --> R2FB[Feedback Resistor R2 to U1 non-inverting input]
    R2FB --> N4[Non-Inverting Input of U1]
    N4 --> R3G[Resistor R3 to Ground]
    R3G --> GND3[Ground Node]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333
    style U1 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333
    style U2 fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333
    style SQ fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333
    style OUT2 fill:#a9cce3,stroke:#333
```

**Description of flow:** $U_1$ generates a square wave based on the feedback from $U_2$'s output. The square wave feeds $U_2$'s integrator, producing a triangular wave whose peak amplitude is self-limited by $U_1$'s threshold.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sawtooth Wave Generator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[DC Supply Plus Minus 15V] --> P1[Power to Op-Amp U1]
    P1 --> U1[Integrator Op-Amp U1]
    U1 --> CC[Constant Current Source Iconst]
    CC --> CAP[Integrator Capacitor C]
    CAP --> OUT3[Sawtooth Waveform Output]
    U1 --> N5[Inverting Input of U1]
    N5 --> RIN[Input Resistor Rinput]
    RIN --> TRIG[Trigger Pulse Source]
    TRIG --> FET[JFET Reset Switch across C]
    FET --> CAP
    OUT3 --> FBK[Feedback to next stage or display]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333
    style U1 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333
    style CC fill:#f5cba7,stroke:#333
    style TRIG fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333
    style OUT3 fill:#a9cce3,stroke:#333
```

**Description of flow:** The constant current source charges $C$ linearly. When the trigger pulse arrives, the JFET turns ON, shorting $C$ and producing a sharp drop. The waveform has a slow rise (linear ramp) and abrupt fall — the sawtooth signature.

---

### 4.4 Timing Relationship Between Square and Triangular Waveforms

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Time as Time Axis
    participant SQ as Square Wave V_o1
    participant TRI as Triangular Wave V_o2
    Time->>SQ: T equals 0, V_o1 jumps to plus Vsat
    SQ->>TRI: Constant plus Vsat input
    TRI->>TRI: V_o2 ramps DOWN linearly
    Time->>SQ: T equals T by 2, V_o1 switches to minus Vsat
    SQ->>TRI: Constant minus Vsat input
    TRI->>TRI: V_o2 ramps UP linearly
    Time->>SQ: T equals T, cycle repeats
    Note over SQ,TRI: Square wave LEADS triangular wave by 90 degrees
```

> [!NOTE]
> The square wave output and the triangular wave output are **in quadrature** (90° out of phase). This is an important KTU viva question: *"Why is the triangular wave lagged by 90° relative to the square wave?"* — **Answer:** Because integration introduces a 90° phase lag.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define an astable multivibrator using an op-amp. Why is it called "astable"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An astable multivibrator using an op-amp is a square wave generator circuit employing an op-amp with **positive feedback** (through a resistor divider $R_1$, $R_2$) and an **RC timing network** at the inverting input. The op-amp operates in saturation, switching between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$ as the capacitor voltage $V_C$ crosses the threshold $\pm \beta V_{sat}$. It is called **"astable"** because it has **no stable state** — the output continuously oscillates between the two saturation levels without any external trigger pulse. The frequency is given by $f = \dfrac{1}{2RC \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}$ where $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between a triangular wave and a sawtooth wave. List one application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Triangular Wave | Sawtooth Wave |
|---------|----------------|---------------|
| **Symmetry** | Symmetric (equal rise and fall time) | Asymmetric (unequal rise and fall time) |
| **Generation** | Schmitt trigger + Integrator | Integrator + Reset switch (FET/Transistor) |
| **Shape** | Linear up, linear down | Linear ramp + sudden drop |
| **Application** | Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO), function generator ICs | CRO time-base generator, TV raster scanning, PWM modulators |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Design a square wave generator using op-amp $\mu$A741 to produce a frequency of **1 kHz** with **50% duty cycle**. Assume $C = 0.01\ \mu F$ and $V_{sat} = \pm 13\ V$. Calculate the values of $R_1$, $R_2$, and $R$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the designed circuit, calculate the **threshold voltages** and the **peak-to-peak output swing**. Sketch the output waveform and the capacitor voltage waveform, clearly labeling all amplitudes and time periods. *(7 marks)*

---

**Question A — Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Design Procedure:**

**Step 1:** For 50% duty cycle, the time spent in $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$ must be equal. This is naturally achieved in a symmetric astable where charge and discharge paths use the same $R$ and $C$.

**Step 2:** For 50% duty cycle, the condition is $\beta = 0.5$ (i.e., $R_1 = R_2$). Let's verify: When $\beta = 0.5$:

$$T = 2RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1 + 0.5}{1 - 0.5}\right) = 2RC \ln(3) \approx 2.197 RC$$

Hmm, this gives $T \neq 2RC$. The 50% duty cycle is inherent to the **symmetric** RC path (single R, single C), regardless of $\beta$. The duty cycle is 50% because $T_1 = T_2$ (by symmetry of charge and discharge through the same resistor). The $\beta$ value only affects the **frequency**.

**Step 3:** Use the rule-of-thumb $\beta = 0.473$ for $f \approx \dfrac{1}{2RC}$.

For exact calculation, use $\beta = 0.462$ (to give $T = 2RC$):

$$T = 2RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1.462}{0.538}\right) = 2RC \ln(2.717) = 2RC \cdot 1 = 2RC$$

So $R_1 = R_2 \cdot \dfrac{1 - \beta}{\beta} = R_2 \cdot \dfrac{0.538}{0.462} \approx 1.164\,R_2$

Choose $R_2 = 10\ k\Omega$, then $R_1 \approx 11.64\ k\Omega$ (use **$R_1 = 12\ k\Omega$** standard value).

**Step 4:** Calculate $R$ for $f = 1\ kHz$:

$$f = \frac{1}{2RC} \implies R = \frac{1}{2fC} = \frac{1}{2 \times 1000 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 50{,}000\ \Omega = 50\ k\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_1 = 12\ k\Omega,\quad R_2 = 10\ k\Omega,\quad R = 50\ k\Omega,\quad C = 0.01\ \mu F}$$

**Part (b) — Threshold and Output Calculations:**

**Threshold voltages:**

$$V_{TH}^{+} = +\beta V_{sat} = +0.462 \times 13 = +6.01\ V$$

$$V_{TH}^{-} = -\beta V_{sat} = -0.462 \times 13 = -6.01\ V$$

**Peak-to-peak output swing:**

$$V_{o,pp} = V_{sat} - (-V_{sat}) = 13 - (-13) = 26\ V$$

**Time period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\ ms$$

$$T_{high} = T_{low} = 0.5\ ms = \frac{T}{2}$$

**Sketch of waveforms:**

```
V_o (Square Wave)
   +13V  ┤━━━━━━━┐       ┌━━━━━━━┐       ┌─────
         │       │       │       │       │
       0 ┼───────┼───────┼───────┼───────┼─────► t
         │       │       │       │       │
   -13V  ┤       └───────┘       └───────┘
         0    0.5ms  1ms  1.5ms  2ms

V_C (Capacitor Voltage)
  +6.01V ┤       ┌───────╲
         │      ╱        ╲
       0 ┼─────╱──────────╲──────────────► t
         │    ╱            ╲
  -6.01V ┤───╱              └─────
         0   0.25ms  0.5ms  0.75ms  1ms
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently **swap the direction of the capacitor voltage ramp** in their waveform. When $V_o = +V_{sat}$, the capacitor charges TOWARDS $+V_{sat}$, so $V_C$ ramps **UP**. When $V_o = -V_{sat}$, $V_C$ ramps **DOWN**. The threshold crossings ($+\beta V_{sat}$ and $-\beta V_{sat}$) trigger the switch. **Loses 2 marks** if the direction is wrong.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module Internal Choice Alternate]**

**(a)** With neat circuit diagrams, explain the working of a **triangular wave generator** using two op-amps. Derive the expressions for the **output frequency** and the **peak output amplitude** in terms of the circuit components. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** How is a **sawtooth waveform** generated using an op-amp integrator and a JFET reset switch? Derive the expression for the **rise time** and explain how the **fall time** is controlled. Why is the sawtooth wave preferred for CRO time-base applications? *(7 marks)*

---

**Question B — Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Triangular Wave Generator:**

**Circuit Description:** The triangular wave generator consists of two op-amps (refer to the block diagram in SECTION_4). Op-amp $U_1$ acts as a **Schmitt trigger** (regenerative comparator), with feedback resistors $R_2$ and $R_3$ setting the threshold. Op-amp $U_2$ acts as a **Miller integrator**, with input resistor $R_1$ and feedback capacitor $C$.

**Working Principle:**

1. Assume the output of $U_1$ is $V_{o1} = +V_{sat}$.
2. This constant positive voltage is applied to the integrator $U_2$ through $R_1$.
3. The integrator output $V_{o2}$ ramps **downward linearly** at a rate $\dfrac{V_{sat}}{R_1 C}$.
4. As $V_{o2}$ decreases, the voltage at the non-inverting input of $U_1$ (which is a weighted sum of $V_{o1}$ and $V_{o2}$) also decreases.
5. When $V_{o2}$ reaches the lower threshold $-\beta V_{sat}$, $U_1$ switches, and $V_{o1}$ snaps to $-V_{sat}$.
6. Now the integrator input is $-V_{sat}$, so $V_{o2}$ ramps **upward** until it reaches $+\beta V_{sat}$, completing one full cycle.

**Frequency Derivation (as derived in SECTION_3.2):**

$$f = \frac{R_2 + R_3}{4 R_1 R_3 C}$$

**Peak Amplitude Derivation:**

$$V_{om} = \pm \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} V_{sat}$$

**Design Example:** For $f = 500\ Hz$, $V_{om} = 5\ V$, $V_{sat} = 13\ V$, $C = 0.1\ \mu F$:

From $V_{om} = 5\ V$: $\dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \dfrac{5}{13} = 0.385$
Choose $R_3 = 5\ k\Omega$, then $R_2 = 8\ k\Omega$.

From $f = 500\ Hz$: $R_1 = \dfrac{R_2 + R_3}{4 f R_3 C} = \dfrac{13{,}000}{4 \times 500 \times 5000 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{13{,}000}{1} = 13\ k\Omega$.

**Part (b) — Sawtooth Wave Generator with JFET Reset:**

**Circuit Configuration:** A non-inverting integrator is built using an op-amp with the input resistor replaced by a **constant current source** (typically a BJT or JFET biased in the saturation region). The feedback capacitor $C$ is shunted by a **reset JFET** whose gate is driven by a periodic trigger pulse train.

**Working Principle:**

1. The constant current source $I_{const}$ charges the capacitor $C$ at a **constant rate**, producing a linear rising ramp at the output.
2. The output voltage grows linearly: $V_o(t) = \dfrac{I_{const}}{C} \cdot t$.
3. When the output reaches the desired peak $V_{om}$, the trigger circuit sends a pulse to the JFET gate.
4. The JFET turns ON (its channel resistance $r_{ds(on)}$ becomes very small, typically 10–100 $\Omega$), shorting the capacitor.
5. The capacitor discharges rapidly through $r_{ds(on)}$, and the output drops sharply to zero.
6. The cycle repeats with the next trigger pulse.

**Rise Time Derivation:**

The capacitor charges from 0 to $V_{om}$ at a constant rate $\dfrac{I_{const}}{C}$:

$$V_{om} = \frac{I_{const}}{C} \cdot T_r$$

$$\boxed{T_r = \frac{V_{om} \cdot C}{I_{const}}}$$

**Fall Time Derivation:**

The discharge time constant is $\tau = r_{ds(on)} \cdot C$. For a $95\%$ discharge:

$$T_f \approx 3 \cdot r_{ds(on)} \cdot C$$

**Why Sawtooth is Preferred for CRO Time-Base:**

1. **Linear sweep:** The constant current charging ensures the **time-base is perfectly linear**, so the horizontal axis on the CRO screen is directly proportional to time.
2. **Fast retrace:** The abrupt fall (JFET reset) provides a fast **flyback** (retrace), which is blanked out by the CRO's Z-axis modulation, giving a clean trace.
3. **Adjustable sweep rate:** Changing $I_{const}$ (via a control voltage) or $C$ allows easy **time-base range switching** (microseconds to seconds).
4. **Low distortion:** Compared to a sinusoidal sweep, the linear ramp gives an **uncompressed view of the input signal** across the entire screen.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never** write $T = 2RC$ directly for the astable — you MUST include the $\ln$ term or the $0.462$ correction. **Loses 3 marks** if omitted.
> 2. For the triangular wave, students often forget that the peak amplitude depends on the **Schmitt trigger feedback ratio**, NOT on $V_{sat}$ directly. Writing $V_{om} = V_{sat}$ is **wrong** — it should be $V_{om} = \beta V_{sat}$.
> 3. The integrator output is **inverted** ($-90°$ phase). For a non-inverting integrator, an additional inverting stage or different topology is required.
> 4. **Sawtooth vs Triangular:** Don't confuse the two! Triangular = two linear slopes (symmetric). Sawtooth = one linear slope + one abrupt drop (asymmetric).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Exam Ready**

- [x] **Square wave generator = Astable multivibrator** = 1 op-amp + 2 resistors + 1 capacitor. **No external input** is needed — it is a self-oscillating circuit.
- [x] **Key formula:** $f = \dfrac{1}{2RC \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}$ with $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$.
- [x] **Rule-of-thumb:** $\beta \approx 0.473 \implies f \approx \dfrac{1}{2RC}$ (used in lab for quick calculation).
- [x] **Threshold voltages:** $V_{TH} = \pm \beta V_{sat}$ — these are the switching levels of the Schmitt action.
- [x] **Triangular wave generator = Schmitt trigger + Integrator** = 2 op-amps.
- [x] **Triangular frequency:** $f = \dfrac{R_2+R_3}{4 R_1 R_3 C}$.
- [x] **Triangular peak amplitude:** $V_{om} = \pm \dfrac{R_3}{R_2+R_3} V_{sat}$ — controlled by feedback ratio, not $V_{sat}$ directly.
- [x] **Phase relationship:** Square wave **leads** triangular wave by **$90°$** (because integration adds a $-90°$ lag).
- [x] **Sawtooth = Asymmetric triangular wave** with **slow linear rise** and **abrupt fast fall**.
- [x] **Sawtooth generation methods:** (i) Asymmetric RC paths, (ii) Constant current source integrator, (iii) JFET-reset integrator.
- [x] **Sawtooth rise time:** $T_r = \dfrac{V_{om} \cdot C}{I_{const}}$.
- [x] **Sawtooth fall time:** $T_f \approx 3 r_{ds(on)} C$ (very small).
- [x] **CRO time-base** uses sawtooth waves for linear horizontal sweep with fast retrace (flyback).
- [x] **Applications summary:** Square → digital clocks, PWM; Triangular → VCO, function generators; Sawtooth → CRO, TV, ADC.
- [x] **Lab Tip:** When designing, always keep $R \cdot C \geq 1\ \mu s$ to avoid slew-rate limitations of the op-amp (e.g., $\mu$A741 has $0.5\ V/\mu s$ slew rate).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"For KTU 2024 — memorize the three formulas, draw the three block diagrams, and remember the three phases (square leads, triangular lags, sawtooth resets)."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic comparator and Schmitt trigger circuits using Op-amp (Use comparator ICs such as LM311)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL308 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Basic comparator and Schmitt trigger circuits using Op-amp (Use comparator ICs such as LM311)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> A **comparator** is an open-loop analog circuit that compares two input voltages and produces a binary (two-level) digital output indicating which input is larger. A **Schmitt trigger** is a comparator with **positive (regenerative) feedback** that introduces two distinct switching thresholds, producing **hysteresis** in the transfer characteristic. The **LM311** is a dedicated, high-speed voltage comparator IC featuring an **open-collector output stage**, allowing the output swing to be defined by an external pull-up supply (logic-level interface to TTL, CMOS, relays, LEDs, etc.).

## 1.1 Plain-English Intuition (Real-World Analogy)

Imagine two contestants standing on a weighing scale. The moment contestant A becomes heavier than contestant B, the judge's hand points to A; the instant B becomes heavier, the hand flips to B. That is exactly what a **comparator** does with voltages. It is a 1-bit analog-to-digital converter.

Now imagine a noisy weighing machine. A tiny vibration could cause the hand to flutter back and forth between A and B many times, even when one is clearly heavier. The judge needs **hysteresis**: the "tipping point" from A→B is *higher* than the tipping point from B→A. That buffer is exactly what a **Schmitt trigger** adds — it prevents false switching when the input signal hovers near the threshold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCEEL308, Module 2):**
> The expected deliverable in the lab is the design, hardware realization, and observation of the **input-output waveform** for (a) a **zero/non-zero reference comparator** and (b) an **inverting Schmitt trigger** using LM311, including the demonstration of hysteresis on the CRO using X-Y mode.

## 1.2 The LM311 — Block-Level Identity

> [!NOTE]
> **LM311 Pinout Snapshot (8-Pin DIP / SOIC)**
> Pin 1: Ground (EMITTER OUT), Pin 2: GATE OUT, Pin 3: VCC+ (+5 to +15 V), Pin 4: V_in⁻ (inverting), Pin 5: V_in⁺ (non-inverting), Pin 6: BAL/STRB, Pin 7: VCC⁻ / GND, Pin 8: BAL/STROBE.

- **Output architecture:** **Open-collector** (also called **open-drain equivalent** for bipolar). This means the output transistor can only *pull down* to ground; the logic HIGH must be provided externally via a pull-up resistor.
- **Why this matters:** The output HIGH level can be set to any value (3.3 V, 5 V, 12 V …) by simply choosing V_CC of the pull-up rail. This is why the LM311 interfaces effortlessly to **TTL (5 V), CMOS (3.3 V/5 V), and 12 V/24 V relay logic** from a single device.
- **Strobe pin:** Allows latching of the output state (output is forced OFF) — used in noisy systems or for synchronous sampling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bare Op-Amp Comparator

When the op-amp is operated **open-loop** (no feedback, infinite open-loop gain $A_{OL} \to \infty$):

$$v_O = A_{OL}\,(v_{in}^+ - v_{in}^-)$$

Two cases arise:
- If $v_{in}^+ > v_{in}^-$ → output saturates to $+V_{sat}$ (typically $\approx +V_{CC} - 1.5\,\text{V}$ for a 741).
- If $v_{in}^+ < v_{in}^-$ → output saturates to $-V_{sat}$.

A **single threshold** exists at the point where the two inputs are equal. This is the reason why even a few microvolts of noise around this threshold will cause **chattering** (multiple false transitions) on the output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core 'Why' Question:** Why is a bare op-amp a poor comparator in noisy environments?
> **Answer:** Because the **single threshold** cannot reject noise — any input noise that crosses the threshold (in either direction) causes a false transition. The output becomes a train of spurious pulses.

## 2.2 The Schmitt Trigger Concept — Why Positive Feedback?

By adding a **positive feedback** network (a resistive divider from the output back to the **non-inverting** input), the threshold becomes **state-dependent**:

- When $v_O = +V_{sat}$, the upper threshold $V_{TH}$ becomes active.
- When $v_O = -V_{sat}$, the lower threshold $V_{TL}$ becomes active.

The difference between the two is the **hysteresis width**:

$$V_H = V_{TH} - V_{TL}$$

Any input noise of amplitude **less than $V_H/2$** cannot cause false switching. This is the entire essence of the Schmitt trigger.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Comparator & Schmitt Trigger Calculations**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Open-loop comparator output | $v_O = A_{OL}(v_{in}^+ - v_{in}^-)$ | $A_{OL}\approx 10^5$ for 741 |
| 2 | Basic zero-reference threshold | $V_{ref} = 0\,\text{V}$ | Single-threshold comparator |
| 3 | Non-zero reference comparator | $V_{ref} = V_{CC}\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ | Two-resistor divider on $+V_{CC}$ |
| 4 | **Inverting Schmitt Trigger — Upper Threshold** | $V_{TH} = +V_{sat}\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$ | Volts (V) |
| 5 | **Inverting Schmitt Trigger — Lower Threshold** | $V_{TL} = -V_{sat}\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$ | Volts (V) |
| 6 | **Hysteresis Width** | $V_H = V_{TH} - V_{TL} = 2V_{sat}\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$ | Volts (V) |
| 7 | **Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger — Upper Threshold** | $V_{TH} = V_{ref}\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} + V_{sat}\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$ | Volts (V) |
| 8 | **Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger — Lower Threshold** | $V_{TL} = V_{ref}\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} - V_{sat}\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$ | Volts (V) |
| 9 | LM311 pull-up output HIGH | $V_{OH} = V_{CC,pullup}$ | External rail (3.3 V, 5 V, 12 V …) |
| 10 | LM311 output LOW | $V_{OL} \le 0.4\,\text{V}$ at $I_{OL}=8\,\text{mA}$ | Active pull-down |
| 11 | Hysteresis normalised (feedback factor) | $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$ | Dimensionless, $0<\beta<1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** $\beta$ is the **non-inverting terminal's feedback factor**. It is **not** the standard inverting-amplifier gain-setting ratio. Mixing them up yields wrong $V_{TH}$ and $V_{TL}$ values — a 7-mark loss in the KTU valuation scheme.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Comparator:** Battery-cell voltage detection, threshold alarms, window detectors, over-voltage protection circuits, zero-crossing detectors in SMPS (Switched-Mode Power Supplies).
- **Schmitt Trigger:** Square-wave / triangle-wave **function generators** (using an op-amp integrator + Schmitt comparator pair — the classic astable), **line receivers** in RS-232/RS-485, **mechanical-switch debouncers** in digital input front-ends, **signal conditioning** for noisy sensor outputs (e.g., thermocouples, LDRs).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Inverting Schmitt Trigger Thresholds

**Circuit topology:** Input $v_{in}$ is applied to the **inverting** terminal. The **non-inverting** terminal sees a resistive divider $R_1$ (from $v_{in}$) and $R_2$ (from $v_O$) to ground. (Note: depending on textbook convention, the position of $R_1$ and $R_2$ may be swapped — the *result* is identical provided the ratio $R_1/R_2$ is taken on the feedback element. We follow the standard KTU lab-manual convention here.)

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the non-inverting node (node $V^+$).**

Assume an ideal op-amp (infinite input impedance, zero current into the $V^+$ pin). Summing currents leaving the node:

$$\frac{V^+ - v_{in}}{R_1} + \frac{V^+ - v_O}{R_2} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $V^+$.**

$$V^+\left(\frac{1}{R_1}+\frac{1}{R_2}\right) = \frac{v_{in}}{R_1} + \frac{v_O}{R_2}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^+ &= \frac{v_{in}R_2 + v_O R_1}{R_1+R_2} \\
V^+ &= v_{in}\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\right) + v_O\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Switching condition.** The output changes state exactly when $v_{in} = V^+$. This is the threshold equation:

$$v_{in,th} = v_{in}\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\right) + v_O\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Upper threshold.** For the transition from LOW to HIGH, just before the flip, $v_O = +V_{sat}$. Setting $v_{in,th} = V_{TH}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{TH} &= V_{TH}\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\right) + V_{sat}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right) \\
V_{TH}\left(1 - \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\right) &= V_{sat}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right) \\
V_{TH}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right) &= V_{sat}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right) \\
\boxed{V_{TH} = +V_{sat}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The above is a sign-convention artifact in this topology.** In the **most common KTU lab-manual inverting topology** (input to $V^-$ through $R_1$, feedback resistor $R_f$ from output to $V^-$; positive-feedback divider $R_2, R_1$ from output to $V^+$), the standard result **re-emerges** as:
> $$V_{TH} = +V_{sat}\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}, \qquad V_{TL} = -V_{sat}\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$$
> Always cross-check with the exact wiring used in your lab manual. The *physical* hysteresis width is invariant: $V_H = 2V_{sat}(R_1/(R_1+R_2))$.

**Step 5 — Lower threshold (analogous derivation).** Set $v_O = -V_{sat}$:

$$V_{TL} = -V_{sat}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Hysteresis width.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_H &= V_{TH} - V_{TL} \\
&= +V_{sat}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right) - \left[-V_{sat}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right)\right] \\
V_H &= 2V_{sat}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** $V_{sat} = \pm 13\,\text{V}$ (for op-amp powered at $\pm 15\,\text{V}$), $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 90\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 1 — Feedback factor.**

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} = \frac{10}{10+90} = 0.1$$

**Step 2 — Thresholds.**

$$V_{TH} = +13 \times 0.1 = +1.3\,\text{V}$$

$$V_{TL} = -13 \times 0.1 = -1.3\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Hysteresis width.**

$$V_H = 2 \times 13 \times 0.1 = 2.6\,\text{V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Type:** A 3-mark or 7-mark problem typically asks: *"For an inverting Schmitt trigger using 741, $V_{CC}=\pm 15\,\text{V}$, $R_1=10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2=90\,\text{k}\Omega$, find $V_{TH}$, $V_{TL}$ and $V_H$."* The above is the exact valuation template.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance Table

| Parameter | Bare Op-Amp Comparator | Inverting Schmitt Trigger | Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Feedback** | Open loop (none) | Positive, to $V^+$ | Positive, to $V^+$ |
| **Number of thresholds** | 1 | 2 ($V_{TH}, V_{TL}$) | 2 ($V_{TH}, V_{TL}$) |
| **Noise immunity** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Output shape for sine input** | Distorted chattering | Clean square wave | Clean square wave |
| **Use case** | Precision threshold detect | Square wave gen, line recvr | Reference-level detector |

> [!TIP]
> **To distinguish inverting vs. non-inverting topology at a glance:** In the **inverting** version, the input signal goes to the **inverting ($V^-$) terminal**; in the **non-inverting** version, the input goes to the **non-inverting ($V^+$) terminal** and the inverting input is grounded. The transfer characteristics are also inverted in the *phase* sense.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Physical op-amp schematic symbols are best drawn on paper or in SPICE. The Mermaid block diagrams below describe the **functional architecture** — the precise way the LM311 is wired in the KTU lab.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Basic Comparator Using LM311

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vin DC / Sine] --> B[Non-Inverting Pin 5 LM311]
    C[Vref from POT divider] --> D[Inverting Pin 4 LM311]
    B --> E[LM311 Comparator Core]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Open-Collector Output Pin 7]
    G[VCC pull-up 5V] --> H[Pull-up Resistor 4.7k]
    H --> F
    F --> I[Logic Output / LED / CRO]
    J[Strobe Pin 6] --> K[Left open or grounded for free-running]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Inverting Schmitt Trigger Using LM311

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vin Sine / Triangle] --> B[Inverting Pin 4 LM311]
    B --> C[LM311 Core with Positive Feedback]
    D[Pull-up 4.7k to 5V] --> C
    C --> E[Output Pin 7]
    E --> F1[Feedback Resistor R2 to Pin 5]
    F1 --> G[Non-Inverting Pin 5]
    H[R1 from Pin 5 to Ground] --> G
    E --> I[CRO Ch1 to view square wave]
    A --> J[CRO Ch2 to view input sine]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hysteresis Loop Formation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Output is at -Vsat] --> B{Vin crosses VTL going UP}
    B -- Yes --> C[Output flips to +Vsat]
    C --> D{Vin crosses VTH going DOWN}
    D -- Yes --> E[Output flips back to -Vsat]
    E --> A
    A -.-> F[Region of width VH is deadband, no output change]
```

## 4.4 LM311 Pin-Connectivity Block (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LM311_Device[LM311 8-Pin IC]
        P3[Pin 3 VCC+ 5 to 15V]
        P4[Pin 4 V Inverting]
        P5[Pin 5 V Non-Inverting]
        P6[Pin 6 Balance and Strobe]
        P7[Pin 7 Emitter Open-Collector Output]
        P8[Pin 8 Balance and Strobe]
        P1[Pin 1 Ground]
        P2[Pin 2 Gate Output internally connected]
    end
    VinSource[Input Signal] --> P4
    VrefNode[Reference Voltage] --> P5
    P3 --> VccRail[+12V Supply]
    P1 --> GNDRail[Ground]
    PullupRail[External 5V Logic Rail] --> PullupResistor[4.7k Pullup] --> P7
    P7 --> OutNode[Output to CRO / LED]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Wiring Tip (Examiner's Frequent Question):** If your LM311 output is "always LOW" or "always HIGH," the most common cause is a **missing pull-up resistor**. Without the external pull-up, the open-collector output simply floats and the CRO displays garbage.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Question Bank Coverage:** Part A (3-mark) recall questions + Part B (14-mark) full-derivation problems with internal choice, tagged as KTU Past-Year style.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a voltage comparator. Why is a dedicated comparator IC such as LM311 preferred over a general-purpose op-amp (e.g., 741) in high-speed applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **voltage comparator** is a circuit that compares two analog input voltages and produces a digital two-level output indicating which input is greater. (1 mark)

The LM311 is preferred over the 741 in high-speed applications because:
- The LM311 is specifically designed as a comparator — it has a **shorter propagation delay** (typically 200 ns vs. tens of microseconds for 741). (1 mark)
- It has an **open-collector output** that allows interfacing to any logic family (TTL, CMOS) by simply choosing the pull-up supply, providing **level translation** in a single device. (1 mark)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is hysteresis in a Schmitt trigger? Why is it necessary in a comparator used with noisy input signals?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Hysteresis** in a Schmitt trigger is the deliberate introduction of **two different switching thresholds** — an upper threshold $V_{TH}$ for the rising input and a lower threshold $V_{TL}$ for the falling input, separated by the **hysteresis width** $V_H = V_{TH} - V_{TL}$. (1.5 marks)

It is necessary in noisy environments because a single-threshold comparator will produce **multiple false transitions (chattering)** when the input signal hovers near the threshold. Hysteresis ensures that **noise of amplitude less than $V_H/2$ cannot cause false switching**, yielding a clean, single-transition output. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Full-Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **inverting Schmitt trigger** using an op-amp. Derive the expressions for the upper threshold voltage $V_{TH}$, lower threshold voltage $V_{TL}$, and hysteresis width $V_H$. (7 marks)

**(b)** An inverting Schmitt trigger uses an op-amp with $V_{sat} = \pm 12\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 12\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 68\,\text{k}\Omega$. Calculate $V_{TH}$, $V_{TL}$, and $V_H$. If the input is a $5\,\text{V}$ peak sine wave at $1\,\text{kHz}$, sketch the output waveform. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution (a)

**Circuit Description:** The input signal $v_{in}$ is fed to the **inverting** terminal $V^-$ via resistor $R_{in}$. A **positive feedback** network consisting of resistors $R_1$ (from output to $V^+$) and $R_2$ (from $V^+$ to ground) sets the threshold levels. (2 marks)

**Derivation of $V_{TH}$ and $V_{TL}$:**

Applying superposition at $V^+$ (ideal op-amp, no current into inputs):

$$V^+ = v_{in}^{(-)}\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\right) + v_O\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right)$$

Switching occurs when $V^+ = v_{in}^{(-)}$. For the inverting configuration, the input reaches $V^+$ scaled by the feedback path; solving for the two output saturation states yields:

$$V_{TH} = +V_{sat}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right)$$

$$V_{TL} = -V_{sat}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right)$$

**Hysteresis Width:**

$$V_H = V_{TH} - V_{TL} = 2V_{sat}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right)$$

(3 marks for derivation; 2 marks for narrative + diagram quality)

#### Model Solution (b)

**Given:** $V_{sat} = \pm 12\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 12\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 68\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 1 — Feedback factor:**

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} = \frac{12}{12+68} = \frac{12}{80} = 0.15$$

**Step 2 — Thresholds:**

$$V_{TH} = +12 \times 0.15 = +1.8\,\text{V}$$

$$V_{TL} = -12 \times 0.15 = -1.8\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Hysteresis width:**

$$V_H = 2 \times 12 \times 0.15 = 3.6\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Output sketch (narrative):** Since the input sine wave has a peak of $\pm 5\,\text{V}$, it comfortably exceeds both $\pm 1.8\,\text{V}$ thresholds. The output will be a **clean square wave of amplitude $\pm 12\,\text{V}$** in **anti-phase** with the input (hence "inverting"). Transitions occur at $v_{in} = +1.8\,\text{V}$ (falling edge of input) and $v_{in} = -1.8\,\text{V}$ (rising edge of input). (3 marks for numerical; 4 marks for waveform reasoning and sketch)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **non-inverting Schmitt trigger** circuit using an op-amp. Derive expressions for $V_{TH}$ and $V_{TL}$ when a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design a non-inverting Schmitt trigger using an op-amp with $V_{sat} = \pm 14\,\text{V}$ and $V_{ref} = +5\,\text{V}$ to obtain $V_{TH} = +8\,\text{V}$ and $V_{TL} = +2\,\text{V}$. Specify the resistor ratio. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution (a)

**Circuit Description:** In the non-inverting Schmitt trigger, the input $v_{in}$ is applied to the **non-inverting $V^+$** terminal. A reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied to the inverting $V^-$ terminal through a divider. The output is fed back to the $V^-$ terminal through a resistor divider $(R_1, R_2)$. (2 marks)

**Derivation:**

The voltage at the $V^-$ terminal is:

$$V^- = V_{ref}\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\right) + v_O\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right)$$

Switching occurs when $V^+ = V^-$ i.e. when $v_{in} = V^-$:

**Upper Threshold** (output transitions from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$, $v_O$ jumps to $+V_{sat}$):

$$V_{TH} = V_{ref}\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\right) + V_{sat}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right)$$

**Lower Threshold** (output transitions from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$, $v_O$ is $-V_{sat}$):

$$V_{TL} = V_{ref}\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\right) - V_{sat}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right)$$

**Hysteresis Width:**

$$V_H = V_{TH} - V_{TL} = 2V_{sat}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right)$$

(3 marks for derivation; 2 marks for diagram)

#### Model Solution (b)

**Given:** $V_{sat} = \pm 14\,\text{V}$, $V_{ref} = +5\,\text{V}$, $V_{TH} = +8\,\text{V}$, $V_{TL} = +2\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1 — Compute hysteresis width and feedback factor:**

$$V_H = V_{TH} - V_{TL} = 8 - 2 = 6\,\text{V}$$

$$2V_{sat}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\right) = 6 \implies \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} = \frac{6}{2 \times 14} = \frac{6}{28} = \frac{3}{14}$$

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} = 0.2143$$

**Step 2 — Find $V_{TH}$ and verify $V_{ref}$ contribution:**

$$V_{TH} = V_{ref}(1-\beta) + V_{sat}\beta$$
