$$8 = 5(1 - 0.2143) + 14(0.2143) = 5(0.7857) + 14(0.2143) = 3.9286 + 3.0002 = 6.929$$

This does not match $V_{TH} = 8\,\text{V}$. The problem indicates the *target* values. The numerical mismatch (6.93 V vs 8 V) shows that $V_{sat} = 14\,\text{V}$ with $V_H = 6\,\text{V}$ **cannot exactly satisfy** $V_{TH} = 8\,\text{V}$ and $V_{TL} = 2\,\text{V}$ simultaneously with the standard formula.

**Resolution — Recompute using the strict formulas:**

From the two equations:

$$V_{TH} + V_{TL} = 2V_{ref}\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\right) = 10 \implies \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = \frac{10}{2 \times 5} = 1$$

This implies $R_1 = 0$, which is not physically realizable. This shows the design constraints are inconsistent for the given $V_{sat}$.

**Practical Engineering Takeaway:** The student should choose $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 22\,\text{k}\Omega$ (ratio 10:22 = 5:11), giving $\beta = 10/32 = 0.3125$:

$$V_{TH} = 5(0.6875) + 14(0.3125) = 3.4375 + 4.375 = 7.8125\,\text{V} \approx 7.8\,\text{V}$$

$$V_{TL} = 5(0.6875) - 14(0.3125) = 3.4375 - 4.375 = -0.9375\,\text{V} \approx -0.94\,\text{V}$$

(4 marks for design math; 3 marks for design choice and practical resistor values)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Frequent Mark-Loss Zones (Costing 7–14 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the pull-up resistor on LM311 output** — the output will simply float and the circuit will not function. Examiners frequently mark this as "circuit not understood" and award zero marks for the output stage.
>
> 2. **Sign confusion in threshold formulas** — many students write $V_{TH} = +V_{sat}$ for an inverting Schmitt trigger and lose the feedback-ratio term entirely. Always include $\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$.
>
> 3. **Using $V_{CC}$ instead of $V_{sat}$** — the op-amp saturates a few volts below the supply rail. For a 741 at $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ supply, $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13\,\text{V}$, not $\pm 15\,\text{V}$. A common 1-mark loss in numerical problems.
>
> 4. **Confusing the inverting and non-inverting Schmitt trigger topologies** — the *formulas look* similar but the **input terminal is different** and the **$V_{ref}$ term is absent** in the inverting version. Read the question twice.
>
> 5. **Not sketching the transfer characteristic (hysteresis loop)** in the answer sheet — this is a 2-mark loss in almost every KTU answer key. Always draw the square-loop with $V_{TH}$, $V_{TL}$ labelled.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Comparator** = open-loop op-amp, **single threshold**, no noise immunity, output chatters in noisy signals.
- **Schmitt Trigger** = comparator with **positive feedback**, **two thresholds**, hysteresis band $V_H$ gives **noise immunity**.
- **LM311** = dedicated comparator IC, **open-collector output**, **external pull-up resistor mandatory** for HIGH level.
- **Inverting Schmitt trigger** — input to $V^-$, $V_{TH} = +V_{sat}\beta$, $V_{TL} = -V_{sat}\beta$, $\beta = R_1/(R_1+R_2)$.
- **Non-inverting Schmitt trigger** — input to $V^+$, thresholds include a $V_{ref}$ offset term: $V_{TH},V_{TL} = V_{ref}(1-\beta) \pm V_{sat}\beta$.
- **Hysteresis width** $V_H = 2V_{sat}\beta$ — same formula for both topologies.
- **Saturation voltage** $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 1.5\,\text{V}$ to $V_{CC} - 2\,\text{V}$ for 741; use measured value, not $V_{CC}$.
- **Open-collector output** of LM311 needs a pull-up of $1\,\text{k}\Omega$–$10\,\text{k}\Omega$ to the logic supply rail (typically 5 V).
- **Strobe pin** (Pin 6 of LM311) — leave open or grounded for free-running; tie LOW to force output OFF.
- **Hysteresis kills chatter** for input noise of amplitude $< V_H/2$ — this is the single most important design rule.
- **Lab Practical Tip:** Use CRO **XY mode** with $v_{in}$ on X-axis and $v_O$ on Y-axis to visualize the **literal rectangular hysteresis loop** on the screen — this is a guaranteed 3-mark viva question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active Filters (High Pass and Low pass - one each)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL308 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Active Filters (High Pass and Low pass - one each) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Active Filter (KTU 2024 Definition):** An active filter is a frequency-selective electronic network that employs an active device (typically an operational amplifier) in conjunction with passive components (resistors and capacitors) to achieve a specified frequency response. Unlike passive filters, active filters provide **power gain**, **isolation**, and do not require inductors, making them ideal for **integrated circuit (IC) implementation** in the audio, communication, and instrumentation domains. The two canonical first-order realizations studied in **PCEEL308 - Analog Electronics Lab, Module Experiment 2** are: 1. **First-Order Active Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Passes signals with frequencies **below** the cutoff frequency $f_c$ and attenuates signals above $f_c$. 2. **First-Order Active High-Pass Filter (HPF):** Passes signals with frequencies **above** the cutoff frequency $f_c$ and attenuates signals below $f_c$. > [!NOTE] > The **cutoff frequency** $f_c$ is universally defined as the $-3\text{ dB}$ frequency — the point at which the output power drops to exactly **half** of the passband power, corresponding to a magnitude of $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ of the maximum gain. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a **water filtration plant**: - A **Low-Pass Filter** is like a sieve that **lets small pebbles pass through** but **blocks large boulders**. In electronics, "small pebbles" are **low-frequency signals** (e.g., a steady DC bias or a slow-moving audio bass note) and "boulders" are **high-frequency noise** (e.g., hiss, RF interference). - A **High-Pass Filter** is the opposite — it **blocks slow, stagnant water** (DC offset, 50 Hz mains hum) but **lets fast-moving currents through** (treble audio notes, sharp digital edges). The **op-amp** in the circuit acts like an **amplifying pump** that boosts the pressure (signal amplitude) of whatever water (signal) the filter allows to pass. This is why these circuits are called *active* — they need an external power supply to operate, unlike a passive RC filter which only attenuates. ### 1.3 Why Use Active Filters in KTU Labs? | Property | Passive RC Filter | Active RC Filter (Op-Amp based) | |---|---|---| | **Gain** | Attenuates only ($\le 1$) | Can provide gain $> 1$ | | **Loading Effect** | Output load changes response | Op-amp provides **isolation** (high input Z, low output Z) | | **Inductor Required** | Yes, for sharp roll-off | **No inductor needed** — IC friendly | | **Cascading** | Difficult (loading) | Easy (each stage isolated by op-amp) | | **Tuning** | Bulky variable capacitors | Simple resistor change | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 First-Order Active Low-Pass Filter (LPF) — Inverting Topology **Circuit Topology:** An inverting amplifier where the input resistor $R_1$ is replaced by an RC series network $(R_1, C_1)$, and the feedback element is a pure resistor $R_f$. **Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):** - **Step 1 — Input Network:** The series combination of $R_1$ and $C_1$ forms a voltage divider. At **low frequencies** ($f \to 0$), the capacitor acts as an **open circuit**, so the input signal passes through $R_1$ with full amplitude. - **Step 2 — High-Frequency Behavior:** At **high frequencies** ($f \to \infty$), the capacitor's reactance $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$ approaches **zero**, effectively **short-circuiting** the input to ground — hence the signal is blocked. - **Step 3 — Op-Amp Action:** The op-amp in inverting configuration computes: $V_{out} = -(Z_f / Z_{in}) \cdot V_{in}$, where $Z_{in} = R_1 + 1/(sC_1)$ and $Z_f = R_f$. - **Step 4 — Cutoff Point:** The cutoff occurs when $R_1 = 1/(\omega C_1)$, giving $\omega_c = 1/(R_1 C_1)$. **Transfer Function (Inverting LPF):** $$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + s R_1 C_1}$$ **Substituting $s = j\omega$:** $$H(j\omega) = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j\omega R_1 C_1}$$ ### 2.2 First-Order Active High-Pass Filter (HPF) — Inverting Topology **Circuit Topology:** The input network is a pure resistor $R_1$ in series with a **capacitor $C_1$** to ground, while the feedback element is a parallel RC network $(R_f \parallel C_f)$. **Operational Logic:** - **Step 1 — Low-Frequency Behavior:** At **low frequencies**, the capacitor $C_1$ presents a **very high impedance** ($X_C \to \infty$), blocking the input signal — hence output $\to 0$. - **Step 2 — High-Frequency Behavior:** At **high frequencies**, $C_1$ becomes a **short circuit**, allowing the full input signal to reach the inverting node. The feedback capacitor $C_f$ also becomes a short, making the DC gain $R_f / R_1$ dominant. - **Step 3 — Cutoff Point:** The cutoff frequency is again $\omega_c = 1/(R_1 C_1)$. **Transfer Function (Inverting HPF):** $$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{s R_1 C_1}{1 + s R_1 C_1}$$ **Substituting $s = j\omega$:** $$H(j\omega) = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{j\omega R_1 C_1}{1 + j\omega R_1 C_1}$$ ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **master reference** for both university viva voce and ESE numerical problems. Memorize it thoroughly. | Parameter | Low-Pass Filter (LPF) | High-Pass Filter (HPF) | |---|---|---| | **DC / Passband Gain $A_0$** | $-R_f / R_1$ | $0$ (at $f=0$) ; high-freq gain $= -R_f / R_1$ | | **Cutoff Angular Frequency $\omega_c$** | $1 / (R_1 C_1)$ | $1 / (R_1 C_1)$ | | **Cutoff Frequency $f_c$ (Hz)** | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$ | | **Magnitude Response $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$** | $\dfrac{\vert R_f / R_1 \vert}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2}}$ | $\dfrac{\vert R_f / R_1 \vert \cdot \omega R_1 C_1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2}}$ | | **Phase Response $\angle H(j\omega)$** | $-180^{\circ} - \arctan(\omega R_1 C_1)$ | $-90^{\circ} - \arctan(\omega R_1 C_1)$ | | **Roll-off Rate** | $-20 \text{ dB/decade}$ (above $f_c$) | $-20 \text{ dB/decade}$ (below $f_c$) | | **Passband Condition** | $f \ll f_c$ | $f \gg f_c$ | | **Stopband Condition** | $f \gg f_c$ | $f \ll f_c$ | | **Output at $f = f_c$** | $A_0 / \sqrt{2} \equiv -3 \text{ dB point}$ | $A_0 / \sqrt{2} \equiv -3 \text{ dB point}$ | | **Time Constant $\tau$** | $R_1 C_1$ seconds | $R_1 C_1$ seconds | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **LPF Application:** Anti-aliasing filter before an **ADC**, audio subwoofer crossover, noise suppression in biomedical ECG/EEG equipment. - **HPF Application:** DC blocking in audio amplifiers, **50 Hz mains hum rejection**, AC coupling in oscilloscope front-ends, treble speaker crossover. - **Cascaded Use:** A LPF cascaded with a HPF produces a **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** — a key building block in radio receivers. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 ESE valuation scheme awards **full 14 marks** only when the **complete derivation** is shown — from the KCL equation at the op-amp's virtual ground, to the final s-domain transfer function, to the magnitude in dB. **Every intermediate step below is mandatory for full marks.** ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — First-Order Active LPF (Inverting) **Given:** Op-amp in inverting configuration. $Z_{in}(s) = R_1 + \dfrac{1}{sC_1}$ (series RC), $Z_f(s) = R_f$ (pure resistor). **Step 1: Identify Virtual Ground Condition** Since the op-amp is in the linear region with negative feedback, the non-inverting terminal is grounded, and by virtual-short: $$V^+ = V^- = 0 \text{ V (Virtual Ground)}$$ **Step 2: Apply KCL at the Inverting Node (Node A)** The current flowing from $V_{in}$ through the series RC into node A equals the current flowing from node A through $R_f$ to $V_{out}$ (no current enters the op-amp's input): $$I_{in} = I_{feedback}$$ **Step 3: Express Branch Currents Using Voltage Differences** The voltage at node A is **zero** (virtual ground). Therefore, the current through the input series RC network is: $$I_{in} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1 + \frac{1}{sC_1}} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1 + \frac{1}{sC_1}}$$ The current through the feedback resistor is: $$I_f = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$ **Step 4: Equate the Two Currents (KCL)** $$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1 + \frac{1}{sC_1}} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$ **Step 5: Rearrange to Obtain the Transfer Function** Rearranging to isolate the ratio $V_{out}/V_{in}$: $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1 + \frac{1}{sC_1}}$$ **Step 6: Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $sC_1$** $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f \cdot sC_1}{sR_1 C_1 + 1}$$ **Step 7: Rearrange into Standard First-Order LPF Form** Dividing numerator and denominator by $R_1 C_1$: $$\boxed{H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + sR_1 C_1}}$$ **Step 8: Identify the Cutoff Frequency** The standard form of a first-order LPF is: $$H(s) = \frac{K}{1 + s/\omega_c}$$ Comparing coefficients: $$\frac{1}{\omega_c} = R_1 C_1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_c = \frac{1}{R_1 C_1} \text{ rad/s}$$ Converting to Hz: $$f_c = \frac{\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1} \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 9: Magnitude in dB** $$\vert H(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) - 10\log_{10}\left(1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2\right)$$ ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — First-Order Active HPF (Inverting) **Given:** $Z_{in}(s) = R_1 + \dfrac{1}{sC_1}$ (series RC), $Z_f(s) = R_f \parallel \dfrac{1}{sC_f}$. **Step 1: KCL at Inverting Node (Virtual Ground at 0 V)** The current entering node A from the source through the series RC equals the current leaving through the parallel feedback network: $$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1 + \frac{1}{sC_1}} = -\frac{V_{out}}{Z_f(s)}$$ **Step 2: Compute Feedback Impedance $Z_f(s)$** For a parallel combination of $R_f$ and $C_f$: $$Z_f(s) = \frac{R_f \cdot \frac{1}{sC_f}}{R_f + \frac{1}{sC_f}} = \frac{R_f}{1 + sR_f C_f}$$ **Step 3: Substitute and Rearrange** $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{Z_f(s)}{R_1 + \frac{1}{sC_1}} = -\frac{\dfrac{R_f}{1 + sR_f C_f}}{R_1 + \dfrac{1}{sC_1}}$$ **Step 4: Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $sC_1$** $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{sR_f C_1}{(1 + sR_f C_f)(1 + sR_1 C_1)}$$ **Step 5: Standard First-Order HPF Form** If $R_f C_f \ll R_1 C_1$ (so the feedback capacitor dominates at high frequency), the pole at $sR_f C_f$ is pushed to a much higher frequency and is neglected. The simplified transfer function becomes: $$\boxed{H(s) \approx -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{sR_1 C_1}{1 + sR_1 C_1}}$$ **Step 6: Identify Cutoff Frequency** Identical to LPF: $$\omega_c = \frac{1}{R_1 C_1}, \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (LPF Design) **Design Specification:** Design a first-order active LPF with cutoff frequency $f_c = 2 \text{ kHz}$ and passband gain $A_0 = -10$. **Step 1: Choose a Convenient Capacitor Value** Following the KTU lab convention, choose: $$C_1 = 0.01 \text{ \mu F} = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$$ **Step 2: Compute $R_1$ from the Cutoff Formula** $$R_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C_1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 2000 \cdot 10^{-8}}$$ $$R_1 = \frac{1}{1.2566 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 7957.7 \text{ \Omega} \approx 7.96 \text{ k\Omega}$$ Standard value used in lab: **$R_1 = 8.2 \text{ k\Omega}$** **Step 3: Compute $R_f$ from the Gain** $$\vert A_0 \vert = \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 10 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_f = 10 \cdot R_1 = 10 \cdot 8.2 \text{ k\Omega} = 82 \text{ k\Omega}$$ **Step 4: Verify Magnitude at $f = 2 f_c$ (i.e., 4 kHz)** $$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{10}{\sqrt{1 + (2)^2}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 4.47$$ In dB: $20 \log_{10}(4.47) \approx 13.01 \text{ dB}$, which is **7 dB below** the passband gain of 20 dB — confirming the expected **$-20 \text{ dB/decade}$** roll-off. ✓ ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (HPF Design) **Design Specification:** Design a first-order active HPF with cutoff frequency $f_c = 500 \text{ Hz}$ and passband gain $A_0 = -5$. **Step 1: Choose Capacitor Value** Choose $C_1 = 0.1 \text{ \mu F} = 10^{-7} \text{ F}$ (larger capacitor needed for low-frequency HPF). **Step 2: Compute $R_1$** $$R_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C_1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 500 \cdot 10^{-7}} \approx 3183.1 \text{ \Omega}$$ Standard value: **$R_1 = 3.3 \text{ k\Omega}$** **Step 3: Compute $R_f$** $$R_f = 5 \cdot R_1 = 5 \cdot 3.3 \text{ k\Omega} = 16.5 \text{ k\Omega}$$ Standard value: **$R_f = 15 \text{ k\Omega}$ or $18 \text{ k\Omega}$** **Step 4: Verify at $f = 100 \text{ Hz}$ (which is $f_c / 5$)** $$\vert H \vert = \frac{5 \cdot (1/5)}{\sqrt{1 + (1/5)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.04}} \approx 0.98$$ In dB: $\approx -0.17 \text{ dB}$ — confirms the low-frequency attenuation. ✓ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of Active Filter Function (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Vin
Input Signal] --> nodeB[RC Network
Frequency Selector] nodeB --> nodeC[Op-Amp
Active Gain Stage] nodeC --> nodeD[Vout
Filtered Output] nodeE[DC Supply
plus minus 15V] --> nodeC nodeF[Cutoff Frequency
fc Formula] -.-> nodeB nodeG[Gain Setting
Rf over R1] -.-> nodeC ``` ### 4.2 Functional Comparison — LPF vs HPF Signal Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph LowPassChain["LOW-PASS FILTER PATH"] L1[Input Signal] --> L2[Series Resistor R1] L2 --> L3[Shunt Capacitor C1 to Ground] L3 --> L4[Op-Amp Inverting Input] L4 --> L5[Feedback Resistor Rf] L5 --> L6[Low Frequency Output] end subgraph HighPassChain["HIGH-PASS FILTER PATH"] H1[Input Signal] --> H2[Series Capacitor C1] H2 --> H3[Series Resistor R1] H3 --> H4[Op-Amp Inverting Input] H4 --> H5[Feedback Resistor Rf Parallel Cf] H5 --> H6[High Frequency Output] end ``` ### 4.3 Frequency Response (Bode Plot Logic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LPFResponse["LPF MAGNITUDE RESPONSE"] A1[DC Gain = Rf by R1] --> A2[Flat Region up to fc] A2 --> A3[minus 3 dB Drop at fc] A3 --> A4[minus 20 dB per Decade Roll Off] end subgraph HPFResponse["HPF MAGNITUDE RESPONSE"] B1[Zero Output at DC] --> B2[plus 20 dB per Decade Rise] B2 --> B3[minus 3 dB Below Passband at fc] B3 --> B4[Flat Region = Rf by R1] end ``` ### 4.4 Component-Level Schematic Representation (Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InvertingLPF["INVERTING LPF SCHEMATIC"] S1[Vin] --> S2[R1 in Series with C1] S2 --> S3[Inverting Node Pin 2] S3 --> S4[Rf Feedback] S4 --> S5[Vout] S6[Non Inverting Pin 3] --> S7[Ground] S3 --> S8[Op-Amp IC 741] end subgraph InvertingHPF["INVERTING HPF SCHEMATIC"] T1[Vin] --> T2[C1 in Series with R1] T2 --> T3[Inverting Node Pin 2] T3 --> T4[Parallel Rf and Cf Feedback] T4 --> T5[Vout] T6[Non Inverting Pin 3] --> T7[Ground] T3 --> T8[Op-Amp IC 741] end ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Q1. Define an active filter. List two advantages of active filters over passive filters.** (3 marks) **Model Answer:** An active filter is a frequency-selective network that uses an active device such as an operational amplifier along with resistors and capacitors to achieve a desired frequency response. *Two advantages:* 1. **Gain Capability:** Active filters provide signal gain greater than unity, whereas passive filters can only attenuate. 2. **No Inductors Required:** Active filters use only RC components, making them compact, lightweight, and suitable for IC fabrication. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q2. What is meant by the cutoff frequency of an active filter? Write the expression for the cutoff frequency of a first-order active low-pass filter.** (3 marks) **Model Answer:** The cutoff frequency $f_c$ is the frequency at which the magnitude of the filter's transfer function drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ (i.e., $-3 \text{ dB}$) of its maximum passband value. It marks the boundary between the passband and the stopband. For a first-order active low-pass filter: $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1} \text{ Hz}$$ --- ### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly lose **3–4 marks** in filter problems by **(a)** writing the cutoff formula without showing the KCL derivation, **(b)** confusing the **inverting and non-inverting** gain signs, and **(c)** forgetting to convert angular frequency $\omega$ to $f$ in Hz. Always write $f_c$ in Hz at the end. The phase response sign convention **must** be explicit: inverting topology gives a $180^{\circ}$ phase inversion at DC for LPF, and $90^{\circ}$ phase lead for HPF. --- #### **Question A — Active Low-Pass Filter Design** (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Derive the transfer function of a first-order active low-pass filter using an inverting op-amp configuration. Hence, obtain the expression for the cutoff frequency. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Given the inverting op-amp topology with $Z_{in}(s) = R_1 + \dfrac{1}{sC_1}$ and $Z_f(s) = R_f$. **Step 1:** Apply virtual ground: $V^- = 0$. **Step 2:** KCL at inverting node: $$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1 + \frac{1}{sC_1}} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f}$$ **Step 3:** Solving: $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1 + \frac{1}{sC_1}}$$ **Step 4:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC_1$: $$H(s) = -\frac{sR_f C_1}{1 + sR_1 C_1}$$ **Step 5:** Rewriting in standard form: $$H(s) = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + sR_1 C_1}$$ **Step 6:** Comparing with $H(s) = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + s/\omega_c}$: $$\boxed{\omega_c = \frac{1}{R_1 C_1} \text{ rad/s}, \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1} \text{ Hz}, \quad A_0 = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$ **(b)** Design a first-order active LPF with cutoff frequency $f_c = 5 \text{ kHz}$ and passband gain of $-20$. Assume the op-amp is IC 741 powered by $\pm 15 \text{ V}$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Choose Capacitor** Select $C_1 = 0.01 \text{ \mu F} = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$ (standard E12 value). **Step 2: Compute $R_1$ from Cutoff Formula** $$R_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C_1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 5000 \cdot 10^{-8}} \approx 3183 \text{ \Omega}$$ Choose standard value: **$R_1 = 3.3 \text{ k\Omega}$** **Step 3: Compute $R_f$ from Gain** $$R_f = 20 \cdot R_1 = 20 \cdot 3.3 \text{ k\Omega} = 66 \text{ k\Omega}$$ Choose standard value: **$R_f = 68 \text{ k\Omega}$** **Step 4: Verify Gain at $f = 1 \text{ kHz}$ (well below $f_c$)** $$\vert A \vert \approx 20 \equiv 26 \text{ dB} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5: Verify Attenuation at $f = 50 \text{ kHz}$ (10× cutoff)** $$\vert A \vert = \frac{20}{\sqrt{1 + (10)^2}} \approx 1.99 \equiv 6 \text{ dB}$$ This is a **$-20 \text{ dB}$ drop from passband** — confirming the $-20 \text{ dB/decade}$ roll-off. ✓ **Step 6: Component List for Lab** | Component | Value | Purpose | |---|---|---| | IC 741 | 1 no. | Active element | | $R_1$ | 3.3 k$\Omega$ | Input resistor | | $C_1$ | 0.01 $\mu$F | Shunt capacitor | | $R_f$ | 68 k$\Omega$ | Feedback resistor | | DC Supply | $\pm 15$ V | Op-amp biasing | | Function Generator | 1 no. | Input signal | | CRO / DSO | 1 no. | Output measurement | --- #### **Question B — Active High-Pass Filter Design** (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]** **(a)** Derive the transfer function of a first-order active high-pass filter using an inverting op-amp topology. Hence, derive the expression for the cutoff frequency. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Given the inverting op-amp with $Z_{in}(s) = R_1 + \dfrac{1}{sC_1}$ (series) and $Z_f(s) = R_f$ (pure resistor feedback — for the simple inverting HPF). **Step 1:** Apply virtual ground: $V^- = 0$. **Step 2:** KCL at inverting node: $$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1 + \frac{1}{sC_1}} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$ **Step 3:** Solving: $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1 + \frac{1}{sC_1}}$$ **Step 4:** Multiply top and bottom by $sC_1$: $$H(s) = -\frac{sR_f C_1}{1 + sR_1 C_1}$$ **Step 5:** Rewrite in standard form by dividing numerator and denominator by $R_1 C_1$: $$H(s) = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{sR_1 C_1}{1 + sR_1 C_1}$$ **Step 6:** Identify cutoff frequency: $$\boxed{\omega_c = \frac{1}{R_1 C_1} \text{ rad/s}, \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1} \text{ Hz}, \quad A_{high} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$ Note: At $f \to 0$, $H(s) \to 0$ (capacitor blocks DC). At $f \to \infty$, $H(s) \to -R_f/R_1$ (capacitor shorts). **(b)** For a first-order active high-pass filter, $R_1 = 10 \text{ k\Omega}$, $C_1 = 1 \text{ nF}$, and $R_f = 50 \text{ k\Omega}$. Calculate the cutoff frequency, the passband gain in dB, and the magnitude of the transfer function at $f = 200 \text{ Hz}$. Comment on whether the signal is in the passband or stopband. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Cutoff Frequency** $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 10^{-9}} \approx 15{,}915.5 \text{ Hz} \approx 15.92 \text{ kHz}$$ **Step 2: Passband Gain (in dB)** $$\vert A_{high} \vert = \frac{R_f}{R_1} = \frac{50}{10} = 5$$ $$A_{high,dB} = 20 \log_{10}(5) \approx 13.98 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 3: Magnitude at $f = 200 \text{ Hz}$** Compute normalized frequency: $$\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{200}{15915.5} \approx 0.01257$$ $$\vert H \vert = \frac{5 \cdot 0.01257}{\sqrt{1 + (0.01257)^2}} \approx \frac{0.0628}{\sqrt{1.000158}} \approx 0.0628$$ $$\vert H \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.0628) \approx -24.04 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 4: Passband/Stopband Comment** Since $f = 200 \text{ Hz} \ll f_c = 15.92 \text{ kHz}$, the signal is well within the **stopband** (attenuation region) of the high-pass filter. The signal is attenuated by approximately **$-24 \text{ dB}$** relative to the passband level of $+13.98 \text{ dB}$, which is a total drop of about **$38 \text{ dB}$**. This confirms that the HPF effectively blocks low-frequency components, such as DC offset and mains hum. ✓ --- ### 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read Before Entering the Exam Hall** - **Core Formula (Universal):** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ — applies to BOTH LPF and HPF first-order active filters. - **LPF Passband:** $f < f_c$ — output magnitude $\approx A_0 = -R_f / R_1$ (inverting). - **HPF Passband:** $f > f_c$ — output magnitude $\approx A_0 = -R_f / R_1$ (inverting). - **$-3 \text{ dB}$ Point:** The cutoff frequency is ALWAYS the $-3 \text{ dB}$ frequency; do not confuse with the half-power frequency wording in numericals. - **Roll-off Slope:** First-order filters roll off at **$-20 \text{ dB/decade}$** (or equivalently $-6 \text{ dB/octave}$). Second-order = $-40 \text{ dB/decade}$. - **Phase Shift:** LPF phase $\to -180^{\circ}$ at infinity; HPF phase $\to -90^{\circ}$ at DC and $-180^{\circ}$ at infinity. - **Op-Amp Choice:** IC 741 is standard for KTU labs; bandwidth ($\sim 1 \text{ MHz}$) must be $\ge 10 \times f_c$ to avoid slew-rate and gain-bandwidth limitations. - **Capacitor Selection Rule:** Choose $C \ge 1 \text{ nF}$ to minimize parasitic effects; $C \le 10 \text{ \mu F}$ to keep $R$ in practical k$\Omega$ range. - **Standard Component Values (E12 series):** Always use nearest standard value ($1.0, 1.2, 1.5, 1.8, 2.2, 2.7, 3.3, 3.9, 4.7, 5.6, 6.8, 8.2$). - **Cascade Bonus:** LPF + HPF cascaded = **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** with $f_{L} = f_{c,HPF}$ and $f_{H} = f_{c,LPF}$. - **Lab Viva Favorite:** *"Why is the op-amp used in active filters?"* — Answer: To provide **gain, isolation, and buffering**, eliminating loading effects between stages and removing the need for bulky inductors. - **Critical Lab Mistake to Avoid:** Connecting the op-amp's **non-inverting terminal to ground** instead of a proper DC bias; this causes output drift and saturation.

topics.title: Instrumentation Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL308 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Instrumentation Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA or INA)** is a precision, closed-loop differential amplifier that provides a fixed or variable gain, exceptionally high **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**, ultra-high input impedance, and very low output impedance. It is specifically engineered to extract and amplify small differential signals that are superimposed on large common-mode noise — making it the de-facto signal-conditioning front end in measurement and instrumentation systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** An instrumentation amplifier is a type of differential amplifier that has been specifically designed to have a very high common-mode rejection ratio, high input impedance, and a gain that can be easily adjusted by a single external resistor, while rejecting any common-mode voltages present at the inputs.

The canonical implementation uses **three (3) operational amplifiers** (two input buffer/amplifier stages + one difference amplifier stage), connected in a way that the differential gain is set by a single external resistor — typically denoted as $R_G$ or $R_{gain}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Smart Hearing" Analogy)

Imagine two microphones placed in a noisy concert hall trying to listen to a faint singer's voice:

- **Differential Signal (Vin1 − Vin2)** → The singer's voice, which arrives at one mic slightly before the other.
- **Common-Mode Noise (HVAC hum, crowd noise)** → The background noise that hits **both** microphones **equally** and **simultaneously**.

A normal amplifier would amplify *both* the voice and the noise. A basic difference amplifier would subtract the two microphone signals — but only if both microphones can drive the circuit (impedance mismatch ruins the cancellation).

An **Instrumentation Amplifier** is like a pair of **"smart, isolated hearing aids"** placed at each microphone that:
1. Listen to their respective mic **without loading it** (very high input impedance).
2. Subtract a perfectly aligned replica of the noise.
3. Amplify **only the residual differential voice signal** by a user-defined amount.
4. Deliver a clean, strong, single-ended output to the recording device.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Standard Metrics)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Gain | $A_d$ | **1 to 1000** (adjustable) | Set by one external resistor |
| Common-Mode Gain | $A_{cm}$ | **< 0.001** | Ideally zero |
| Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | **CMRR** | **80 dB to 120 dB** | The headline figure of merit |
| Input Impedance | $Z_{in}$ | **> 10⁹ Ω** | Prevents source loading |
| Output Impedance | $Z_{out}$ | **< 1 Ω** | Drives heavy loads easily |
| Bandwidth | BW | **kHz to MHz** | Application dependent |
| Input Offset Voltage | $V_{os}$ | **µV range** | Critical for DC precision |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most important metric in KTU problems is the **CMRR**, defined as:
> 
> $$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right) \text{ dB}$$
> 
> High CMRR (typically **≥ 80 dB**) is mandatory for any sensor-amplifier interface (ECG, strain gauge, thermocouple, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Standard Difference Amplifier is Insufficient

A textbook **single-op-amp difference amplifier** has a serious flaw: the two input signals see *unequal* impedances (one sees a voltage divider, the other does not). This causes:

- **Input impedance mismatch** → poor common-mode noise rejection when source impedances are not perfectly equal.
- **Gain change** when feedback resistors are trimmed to vary gain.
- **Loading of the source** when the source impedance is high (e.g., a Wheatstone bridge with $\vert Z_s \vert > 1 \text{ k}\Omega$).

The **three op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier** solves all three problems elegantly.

## 2.2 The Three Op-Amp Topology — Block-by-Block Logic

- **Stage 1 — Input Buffer / Pre-Amplifier (A1 & A2):**
  - Both op-amps are configured as non-inverting amplifiers.
  - Their non-inverting inputs are tied to the source signals $V_{in1}$ and $V_{in2}$.
  - The inverting inputs are tied together through a single **gain-setting resistor $R_G$**.
  - This guarantees **symmetric, very high (ideally infinite) input impedance** on both inputs.

- **Stage 2 — Difference Amplifier (A3):**
  - A standard difference amplifier with four precision-matched resistors ($R_1$ to $R_4$).
  - Subtracts the two outputs of Stage 1 and provides a **single-ended output** $V_{out}$.
  - This stage **rejects** all common-mode voltage that survived Stage 1.

- **The Single-Resistor Gain Control:**
  - Only $R_G$ is external / variable. All other resistors are internal and laser-trimmed.
  - Changing $R_G$ varies the **differential gain** without disturbing the **common-mode rejection**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-memorize** for the 2024 KTU ESE. Carefully note that absolute-value / magnitude symbols are rendered as `\vert` to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Differential Gain of Input Stage (A1, A2) | $A_{d1} = 1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G}$ | $R$ = internal feedback resistor ($\Omega$) |
| 2 | Differential Gain of Difference Stage (A3) | $A_{d2} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}$ | Matched-pair condition $R_1 = R_3$, $R_2 = R_4$ |
| 3 | Total Differential Gain of IA | $A_d = \left( 1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G} \right) \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \vert$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 5 | Output Voltage Expression | $V_{out} = \left( 1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G} \right) \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot (V_{in1} - V_{in2})$ | Volts (V) |
| 6 | Input Resistance | $R_{in,IA} \approx 2 \cdot R_{in,opamp} \rightarrow \infty$ | $\Omega$ (ideal) |
| 7 | Required $R_G$ for Desired Gain $A_d$ | $R_G = \dfrac{2R}{\left( \dfrac{A_d \cdot R_1}{R_2} \right) - 1}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | Offset Voltage at Output (with $V_{os}$ input) | $V_{out,os} = A_d \cdot V_{os,total}$ | V (amplified offset) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Instrumentation amplifiers are the **universal sensor front-end** across industries:

1. **Biomedical Instrumentation** — ECG, EEG, EMG, patient monitoring (rejects 50/60 Hz mains hum).
2. **Industrial Process Control** — Thermocouple amplifiers, RTD signal conditioning, 4–20 mA loops.
3. **Strain-Gauge Bridges** — Load cells, weighing scales, pressure transducers.
4. **Audio Engineering** — Balanced audio line receivers, microphone pre-amplifiers.
5. **Automotive Sensors** — Accelerometers, gyroscopes, airbag crash sensors.
6. **Data Acquisition Systems (DAQ)** — First stage before the ADC in every modern DAQ card.

> [!TIP]
> **Monolithic IA ICs** (the lab-relevant tip): KTU students should be familiar with industry-standard INA ICs — **INA128, INA129, AD620, AD8221, INA333** — all of which are three-op-amp topologies in a single package with a single external $R_G$ pin.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Three Op-Amp IA Output Voltage

### Circuit Assumptions (KTU Standard)

- All three op-amps are **ideal**: $V^+ = V^-$, $I^+ = I^- = 0$, $A_{OL} \to \infty$.
- Internal resistors: $R_1 = R_3$, $R_2 = R_4$, with feedback resistors of A1 and A2 equal to $R$.
- External gain-setting resistor between the two inverting nodes is $R_G$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Virtual Short at the Input Buffers

Because op-amps A1 and A2 are in non-inverting configuration, the voltages at their inverting inputs equal the voltages at the non-inverting inputs:

$$
V^-_{A1} = V_{in1}
$$

$$
V^-_{A2} = V_{in2}
$$

This is the *virtual short* property of an ideal op-amp.

### Step 2 — Compute the Voltage Across the Gain-Setting Resistor

The resistor $R_G$ is connected between the two inverting inputs. Hence the voltage drop across it is:

$$
V_{R_G} = V^-_{A1} - V^-_{A2} = V_{in1} - V_{in2}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Current Through $R_G$

Applying Ohm's law to $R_G$:

$$
I_{R_G} = \frac{V_{in1} - V_{in2}}{R_G}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Nodes (Currents Cannot Enter the Op-Amp)

Since no current flows into the ideal op-amp inputs, the current $I_{R_G}$ must continue through the two feedback resistors $R$ (one above A1, one above A2) in series. Therefore:

$$
I_R = I_{R_G} = \frac{V_{in1} - V_{in2}}{R_G}
$$

### Step 5 — Express the Outputs of A1 and A2

The output of A1 is the voltage at the inverting input plus the voltage drop across the upper feedback resistor $R$:

$$
V_{out1} = V_{in1} + I_R \cdot R = V_{in1} + \left( \frac{V_{in1} - V_{in2}}{R_G} \right) R
$$

Similarly, the output of A2 (current flows the *other way* through its feedback resistor):

$$
V_{out2} = V_{in2} - I_R \cdot R = V_{in2} - \left( \frac{V_{in1} - V_{in2}}{R_G} \right) R
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Differential Output of the Input Stage

Subtracting the two equations to eliminate the inverting-input voltages:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out2} - V_{out1} &= \left[ V_{in2} - \left( \frac{V_{in1} - V_{in2}}{R_G} \right) R \right] - \left[ V_{in1} + \left( \frac{V_{in1} - V_{in2}}{R_G} \right) R \right] \\
&= V_{in2} - V_{in1} - 2R \left( \frac{V_{in1} - V_{in2}}{R_G} \right) \\
&= (V_{in2} - V_{in1}) \left[ 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G} \right] \\
&= -(V_{in1} - V_{in2}) \left( 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Apply the Difference Amplifier Equation for Stage 2

The output of op-amp A3 (with matched resistors $R_1 = R_3$ and $R_2 = R_4$) is the standard difference-amp gain times the differential input applied to it:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot (V_{out2} - V_{out1})
$$

Substituting the result of Step 6:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot \left[ -(V_{in1} - V_{in2}) \left( 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G} \right) \right] \\
&= -\left( 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G} \right) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot (V_{in1} - V_{in2})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Final Master Equation (with Sign Convention)

The magnitude of the output is what matters for sensor applications. The negative sign simply indicates polarity inversion (which can be fixed by swapping $V_{in1}$ and $V_{in2}$ at the input, or by using a unity-gain inverter):

$$
V_{out} = \left( 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G} \right) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot (V_{in1} - V_{in2})
$$

This is the **KTU master formula** for the three op-amp instrumentation amplifier.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Lab-Typical Values)

**Given:** $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_1 = R_3 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = R_4 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_G = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{in1} = 2.05 \text{ V}$, $V_{in2} = 2.00 \text{ V}$ (50 mV differential superimposed on 2 V common-mode).

**Step 1 — Differential Gain of Input Stage:**

$$
A_{d1} = 1 + \frac{2 \times 10\text{k}}{1\text{k}} = 1 + 20 = 21
$$

**Step 2 — Gain of Difference Stage:**

$$
A_{d2} = \frac{10\text{k}}{10\text{k}} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Total Gain:**

$$
A_d = 21 \times 1 = 21
$$

**Step 4 — Output Voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= 21 \times (2.05 - 2.00) \\
&= 21 \times 0.05 \\
&= 1.05 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The 2.00 V common-mode component that is present equally in both inputs is **completely rejected** by the IA — only the 50 mV differential signal is amplified to 1.05 V.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Three Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

The following **Mermaid `flowchart`** provides a safe, board-exam-ready block-level architecture of the IA, showing signal flow from the differential sensor source all the way to the single-ended output, with the **single-resistor gain control** clearly highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sig1["DIFFERENTIAL INPUT V_IN1 FROM SENSOR"] --> buf1["BUFFER AMPLIFIER A1 NON INVERTING"]
    sig2["DIFFERENTIAL INPUT V_IN2 FROM SENSOR"] --> buf2["BUFFER AMPLIFIER A2 NON INVERTING"]
    rint1["FEEDBACK RESISTOR R TOP OF A1"] --- buf1
    rint2["FEEDBACK RESISTOR R BOTTOM OF A2"] --- buf2
    rgain["EXTERNAL GAIN SETTING RESISTOR R_G BETWEEN INVERTING INPUTS"] --- buf1
    rgain --- buf2
    buf1 --> diffamp["DIFFERENCE AMPLIFIER A3 SUBTRACTION STAGE"]
    buf2 --> diffamp
    rone["MATCHED RESISTOR R1"] --- diffamp
    rthree["MATCHED RESISTOR R3"] --- diffamp
    rtwo["MATCHED RESISTOR R2"] --- diffamp
    rfour["MATCHED RESISTOR R4"] --- diffamp
    diffamp --> sout["SINGLE ENDED OUTPUT V_OUT TO ADC OR NEXT STAGE"]
    cmrr["VERY HIGH CMRR TYPICALLY 80 TO 120 DB REJECTS COMMON MODE NOISE"] -.-> diffamp
    hiz["VERY HIGH INPUT IMPEDANCE 10 TO THE POWER 9 OHM PREVENTS SENSOR LOADING"] -.-> buf1
    hiz -.-> buf2
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stageA["STAGE 1 INPUT BUFFERS A1 AND A2 PROVIDE HIGH INPUT IMPEDANCE AND DIFFERENTIAL PRE GAIN"] --> stageB["STAGE 2 DIFFERENCE AMPLIFIER A3 PROVIDES COMMON MODE REJECTION AND SINGLE ENDED OUTPUT"]
    rgainBlock["SINGLE EXTERNAL RESISTOR R_G SETS THE DIFFERENTIAL GAIN OF STAGE 1"] --> stageA
    stageB --> finalOut["FINAL OUTPUT V_OUT = 1 PLUS 2R BY R_G TIMES R2 BY R1 TIMES V_IN1 MINUS V_IN2"]
```

## 4.3 How to Read the Diagrams During the Exam

| Block | Real Circuit Element | Exam Expectation |
|---|---|---|
| `buf1`, `buf2` | Op-amps A1 and A2 | Non-inverting configuration; must mention infinite input impedance |
| `rgain` | External resistor $R_G$ | The **only** resistor a designer changes to alter gain |
| `diffamp` | Op-amp A3 + 4 matched resistors | Standard difference-amp; matched pairs $R_1 = R_3$, $R_2 = R_4$ |
| `cmrr` | Dotted annotation | Always state the typical CMRR value (≥ **80 dB**) |
| `sout` | Output node | Single-ended, low-impedance, ready for ADC input |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define an instrumentation amplifier. List any two of its salient features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An instrumentation amplifier is a precision differential amplifier that has high input impedance, high common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR), and a gain that can be varied by changing a single external resistor $R_G$.

**Two salient features (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Very high CMRR** (typically 80–120 dB): It rejects common-mode noise (such as 50 Hz mains hum) that appears equally on both inputs, while amplifying only the differential signal of interest.
2. **Very high input impedance** (typically $> 10^9 \ \Omega$): Both inputs see nearly infinite impedance, which prevents loading of high-impedance sources such as Wheatstone bridges, thermocouples, and biopotential electrodes.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is CMRR? Why is it critical in instrumentation amplifier applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio)** is defined as the ratio of the differential-mode gain $A_d$ to the common-mode gain $A_{cm}$ of the amplifier, expressed in decibels (1 mark):

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left \vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right \vert \text{ dB}
$$

**Critical Importance (2 marks):** In real-world sensor applications, the desired signal (e.g., ECG waveform, strain-gauge output) is a *small differential* signal superimposed on a *large common-mode* voltage (e.g., electrode half-cell potentials, ground-loop noise). A high CMRR ensures that this large common-mode noise is rejected and only the small differential signal of interest is amplified, preserving measurement accuracy.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the output voltage of a three op-amp instrumentation amplifier. Hence, design the value of $R_G$ to obtain a gain of 100 when the internal resistors are $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_1 = R_3 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $R_2 = R_4 = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Output Voltage (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Assume ideal op-amps:** $V^+ = V^-$, $I^+ = I^- = 0$.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Voltage across $R_G$:**

$$
V_{R_G} = V_{in1} - V_{in2}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Current through $R_G$:**

$$
I_{R_G} = \frac{V_{in1} - V_{in2}}{R_G}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — The same current flows through the two feedback resistors $R$:**

$$
V_{out2} - V_{out1} = -(V_{in1} - V_{in2})\left(1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}\right)
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Output of difference amplifier A3 (with $R_1 = R_3$, $R_2 = R_4$):**

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}(V_{out2} - V_{out1})
$$

**Step 6 (2 marks) — Substitute and simplify to obtain the final master equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{out} = \left( 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G} \right) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot (V_{in1} - V_{in2})\,}
$$

#### Part (b) — Design of $R_G$ for $A_d = 100$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Total gain formula:**

$$
A_d = \left( 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G} \right) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Substitute given values:**

$$
100 = \left( 1 + \frac{2 \times 10\text{k}}{R_G} \right) \cdot \frac{100\text{k}}{1\text{k}}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Simplify the right-hand side:**

$$
100 = \left( 1 + \frac{20\text{k}}{R_G} \right) \cdot 100
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Divide both sides by 100:**

$$
1 = 1 + \frac{20\text{k}}{R_G}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Solve for $R_G$:**

$$
\frac{20\text{k}}{R_G} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_G \to \infty
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Practical Interpretation & Alternative Design:**

The given resistor choice yields a difference-stage gain of 100 by itself, leaving no room for input-stage gain from $R_G$. To obtain a *useful* gain of 100 with a finite $R_G$, the difference-stage gain should be **unity** ($R_1 = R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ for example). Recomputing with $R_1 = R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$:

$$
100 = \left( 1 + \frac{20\text{k}}{R_G} \right) \cdot 1
$$

$$
R_G = \frac{20\text{k}}{99} \approx 202.02 \ \Omega
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Choose $R_G \approx 202 \ \Omega$ for $A_d = 100$ with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and unity-gain difference stage. The given problem with $R_2/R_1 = 100$ is a *trap question* — students who don't check the numbers will incorrectly claim $R_G = 0$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the working of a three op-amp instrumentation amplifier with a neat block diagram. Compare it with a simple difference amplifier and state any three applications.**

#### Part (a) — Working Explanation with Block Diagram (7 Marks)

**Stage 1 — Input Buffers (3 marks):**
The two input signals $V_{in1}$ and $V_{in2}$ are fed to the non-inverting terminals of op-amps A1 and A2 respectively. These op-amps are configured as non-inverting amplifiers, providing very high input impedance. A single external resistor $R_G$ is connected between the inverting terminals of A1 and A2. The voltage developed across $R_G$ is the differential input voltage $V_{in1} - V_{in2}$, and the resulting current is forced through the two internal feedback resistors $R$. This provides a differential pre-gain of:

$$
A_{d1} = 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}
$$

**Stage 2 — Difference Amplifier (3 marks):**
The outputs of A1 and A2 are fed to a precision difference amplifier built around op-amp A3, using four precisely matched resistors ($R_1 = R_3$ and $R_2 = R_4$). This stage subtracts the two outputs, rejects all common-mode voltages, and provides a single-ended output:

$$
V_{out} = \left( 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G} \right) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot (V_{in1} - V_{in2})
$$

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Use the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 of these notes, or draw the standard three-op-amp schematic showing the two input buffers feeding the difference amplifier, with $R_G$ highlighted between the inverting terminals.

#### Part (b) — Comparison & Applications (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | Simple Difference Amplifier | Three Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Op-Amps | 1 | 3 |
| Input Impedance | Low and **unequal** (depends on source) | Very high and **equal** (symmetric) |
| CMRR | Moderate (≈ 40–60 dB) | Very high (≥ 80 dB) |
| Gain Adjustment | Requires changing **multiple** resistors | Only **one** resistor ($R_G$) |
| Source Loading | Significant for high-impedance sources | Negligible |
| Common-mode rejection with mismatched sources | Poor | Excellent |

**Three Applications (1 mark each, 3 marks total):**

1. **ECG / EEG Signal Amplifiers** — extract millivolt-level biopotentials from a noisy hospital environment.
2. **Strain-Gauge / Load-Cell Bridges** — amplify the small differential output of a Wheatstone bridge in weighing systems.
3. **Thermocouple Signal Conditioning** — amplify microvolt-level signals in industrial temperature monitoring.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Frequently Deductions):**
> 
> 1. **Sign Error in Derivation** — Students often write $V_{out1} - V_{out2}$ instead of $V_{out2} - V_{out1}$. The master equation sign depends on which input is fed to A1 vs A2. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting KCL at the Inverting Node** — You must explicitly state that no current enters the ideal op-amp, hence the same current flows through $R_G$, the upper $R$, and the lower $R$. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing $R_G$ with Feedback Resistor** — $R_G$ is connected *between* the inverting terminals, **not** as a feedback resistor of A1 alone. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Not Mentioning CMRR** — Every IA answer must end with a statement on CMRR ≥ 80 dB to receive full credit. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 5. **Units in Design Problems** — Always carry units through derivations; if $R_G$ comes out as a finite ohms value, double-check the difference-stage gain assumption. (Loss: 1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (read this 30 minutes before entering the exam hall):**

- The **three op-amp IA** consists of two input buffers (A1, A2) and one difference amplifier (A3).
- The **only resistor** the user changes to set the gain is the **external $R_G$** — all other resistors are internal and matched.
- The **master equation** is:
  $$V_{out} = \left( 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G} \right) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot (V_{in1} - V_{in2})$$
- **Differential gain of input stage:** $A_{d1} = 1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G}$
- **Differential gain of difference stage:** $A_{d2} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$
- **CMRR** is the **ratio** of differential gain to common-mode gain, in decibels: $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10} \left \vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right \vert$.
- **Key advantage over a basic difference amplifier:** symmetrical, very high input impedance on **both** terminals.
- **Single-resistor gain control** is a hallmark feature — used in monolithic ICs like **INA128, AD620, INA333**.
- For a gain of **100** with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and unity difference stage: $R_G \approx 202 \ \Omega$.
- Typical CMRR target: **≥ 80 dB** for sensor front-ends; **100 dB+** for medical/biomedical use.
- **CMRR = 0 dB** means the amplifier cannot distinguish between signal and noise — useless for instrumentation.
- **Lab experiment goal:** vary $R_G$, measure $V_{out}$ for a small differential input, verify the gain matches theory, and observe the elimination of any common-mode component.
- Always state **assumptions** (ideal op-amp) explicitly at the start of any derivation.
- Always state the **practical applications** (ECG, load cell, thermocouple) when asked to "explain" rather than just "derive."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Astable and Monostable circuit using 555IC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL308 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Astable and Monostable circuit using 555IC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The 555 Timer IC — Formal Definition

The **NE555 (or LM555)** is a highly stable, monolithic timing circuit first introduced by Signetics in 1972. It is an 8-pin analog/digital hybrid device capable of functioning as a precise timer, pulse generator, oscillator, and Schmitt trigger. Internally, it contains two open-collector comparators, a bistable SR flip-flop, a passive NMOS discharge transistor, and a resistive voltage divider of **three equal 5 kΩ resistors** (hence the name "555").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEEL308):** The 555 timer is studied here strictly from an *applications perspective* — designing Astable Multivibrators (AMV) and Monostable Multivibrators (MMV) using external RC networks and verifying the output waveform and timing parameters on a CRO/DSO.

### 1.2 Pin Configuration

| Pin | Name | Function |
|:----|:-----|:---------|
| 1 | GND | Ground reference (**0 V**) |
| 2 | TRIG (Trigger) | Lower comparator input; activates the flip-flop when voltage falls below **VCC/3** |
| 3 | OUT | Output pin; sources/sinks up to **200 mA** |
| 4 | RESET | Active-low master reset; tied to VCC in normal operation |
| 5 | CTRL (Control Voltage) | Externally modulates the threshold levels (default **2·VCC/3** and **VCC/3**) |
| 6 | THR (Threshold) | Upper comparator input; resets the flip-flop when voltage exceeds **2·VCC/3** |
| 7 | DIS (Discharge) | Open-collector discharge path to GND for the timing capacitor |
| 8 | VCC | Positive supply (**+5 V to +15 V**, typically **+5 V**) |

### 1.3 Internal Block Architecture

The internal circuit consists of:

1. **Voltage Divider Network:** Three equal 5 kΩ resistors creating reference voltages of **VCC/3** and **2·VCC/3**.
2. **Comparator 1 (Lower):** Non-inverting input is TRIG (pin 2); inverting input is VCC/3. Output SETS the flip-flop when V_TRIG < VCC/3.
3. **Comparator 2 (Upper):** Inverting input is THR (pin 6); non-inverting input is 2·VCC/3. Output RESETS the flip-flop when V_THR > 2·VCC/3.
4. **SR Flip-Flop (Latch):** Stores the state; output Q drives the output buffer.
5. **Discharge Transistor (Q1):** NMOS connected between DIS (pin 7) and GND; turned ON when flip-flop is RESET.
6. **Output Buffer (Totem-Pole):** Provides high current drive at pin 3.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket and Faucet" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a water bucket with a small tap at the bottom (the discharge transistor) and a refill faucet (charging current through resistors). Two water-level sensors sit at **1/3 height** (lower) and **2/3 height** (upper). The bucket's level dictates the output:
> - **Astable:** The tap and faucet keep playing "fill-drain" tag-of-war forever — the bucket never settles, oscillating continuously. The output is a perfect **square wave**.
> - **Monostable:** The bucket sits happily at empty (stable state). The moment a hand pushes a trigger button (negative pulse), the faucet opens, the bucket fills, and the tap drains it. After a fixed time, the bucket is empty again — back to the *one* stable state.

### 1.5 Astable vs. Monostable — A Quick Comparison

| Parameter | Astable (AMV) | Monostable (MMV) |
|:----------|:--------------|:-----------------|
| Number of stable states | **0** (no stable state) | **1** (output normally LOW) |
| Number of quasi-stable states | 2 | 1 |
| Trigger required? | **No** — self-oscillating | **Yes** — negative pulse on pin 2 |
| Output waveform | Continuous rectangular wave | Single pulse per trigger |
| Used as | Clock generator, LED flasher, PWM | Timer, missing pulse detector, debouncer |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ASTABLE MULTIVIBRATOR (AMV) — Free-Running Oscillator

In Astable mode, the 555 acts as a relaxation oscillator. Pins 2 and 6 are tied together so the timing capacitor C sees both the threshold and trigger sensing circuits simultaneously.

#### Circuit Connections

- **R1** is connected between VCC and pin 7.
- **R2** is connected between pin 7 and the C node (junction of pins 2, 6, and capacitor).
- **C** charges through **(R1 + R2)** and discharges through **R2** only (since pin 7 short-circuits R1 to GND via the internal transistor during discharge).

#### Phase 1: Charging (Output HIGH)

The discharge transistor is OFF. Capacitor C charges from **VCC/3** to **2·VCC/3** through **(R1 + R2)**.

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - V_{CC}/3) \cdot e^{-t/(R_1 + R_2)C}$$

The charging ends when V_C reaches **2·VCC/3**, at which instant the upper comparator RESETS the flip-flop, turning the discharge transistor ON.

#### Phase 2: Discharging (Output LOW)

The discharge transistor is ON, providing a path to ground through **R2**. Capacitor C discharges from **2·VCC/3** to **VCC/3**.

$$V_C(t) = \frac{V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t/R_2 C}$$

When V_C drops to **VCC/3**, the lower comparator SETS the flip-flop, turning the discharge transistor OFF, and the cycle repeats.

### 2.2 MONOSTABLE MULTIVIBRATOR (MMV) — One-Shot Timer

In Monostable mode, the 555 produces a single output pulse of fixed width in response to a trigger.

#### Circuit Connections

- **R** is connected between VCC and pin 7.
- **C** is connected between pins 6, 2 (tied) and GND.
- Pin 2 is normally held HIGH (> VCC/3) by a pull-up resistor. An external **negative trigger pulse** momentarily pulls it below VCC/3.

#### Operation

- **Stable State:** Flip-flop is RESET → output is LOW → discharge transistor is ON → C is held near **0 V**.
- **Trigger Event:** When V_TRIG drops below VCC/3, the flip-flop SETs → output goes HIGH → discharge transistor turns OFF → C begins charging through R.
- **Quasi-Stable State:** Output remains HIGH while C charges from **0 V to 2·VCC/3**. The instant V_C crosses 2·VCC/3, the flip-flop RESETS → output returns LOW → C rapidly discharges through pin 7 to GND → circuit returns to stable state.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Astable Formula | Monostable Formula | Unit |
|:----------|:----------------|:-------------------|:-----|
| Time HIGH (tH) | $0.693 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) \cdot C$ | $1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ | seconds (s) |
| Time LOW (tL) | $0.693 \cdot R_2 \cdot C$ | — (returns to stable) | seconds (s) |
| Total Period (T) | $0.693 \cdot (R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C$ | $1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ | seconds (s) |
| Frequency (f) | $\dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C}$ | $\dfrac{0.91}{R \cdot C}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| Duty Cycle (D) | $\dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\%$ | — | Percent (%) |
| Min D (D_min) | $\approx 50\%$ (when $R_1 \ll R_2$) | — | Percent (%) |
| Max D (D_max) | $\approx 100\%$ (when $R_2 \ll R_1$) | — | Percent (%) |
| Upper threshold | $2 \cdot V_{CC} / 3$ | $2 \cdot V_{CC} / 3$ | Volts (V) |
| Lower threshold | $V_{CC} / 3$ | $V_{CC} / 3$ | Volts (V) |
| Charging constant | $(R_1 + R_2) \cdot C$ | $R \cdot C$ | seconds (s) |
| Discharging constant | $R_2 \cdot C$ | — | seconds (s) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** 555 timers are ubiquitously deployed in **PWM motor drivers, function generators, traffic light controllers, DC-DC converters, tone generators, and missing-pulse detectors** across industrial and consumer electronics — making this experiment a foundational skill for any embedded/IoT engineer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Astable Frequency Formula

#### Step 1 — Capacitor Charging Equation

The capacitor charges from an initial voltage of **VCC/3** toward the supply **VCC** through the series resistance **(R1 + R2)**. The general RC charging equation is:

$$V_C(t) = V_{final} - (V_{final} - V_{initial}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

Substituting $V_{final} = V_{CC}$, $V_{initial} = V_{CC}/3$, and $\tau = (R_1 + R_2) \cdot C$:

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \frac{V_{CC}}{3}\right) \cdot e^{-t/(R_1+R_2)C}$$

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t/(R_1+R_2)C}$$

#### Step 2 — Solve for Charging Time (tH)

The charging terminates when $V_C(t_H) = 2 V_{CC} / 3$. Substituting:

$$\frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} - \frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_H/(R_1+R_2)C}$$

Subtract $V_{CC}$ from both sides:

$$-\frac{V_{CC}}{3} = -\frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_H/(R_1+R_2)C}$$

Divide both sides by $-V_{CC}/3$:

$$1 = 2 \cdot e^{-t_H/(R_1+R_2)C}$$

$$e^{-t_H/(R_1+R_2)C} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\frac{t_H}{(R_1+R_2)C} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln 2$$

$$\boxed{t_H = (R_1 + R_2) \cdot C \cdot \ln 2 = 0.693 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) \cdot C}$$

#### Step 3 — Capacitor Discharging Equation

The capacitor discharges from **2·VCC/3** toward **0 V** through resistance **R2** (pin 7 is grounded internally):

$$V_C(t) = V_{initial} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_d} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t/R_2 C}$$

#### Step 4 — Solve for Discharging Time (tL)

Discharge ends when $V_C(t_L) = V_{CC}/3$:

$$\frac{V_{CC}}{3} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_L/R_2 C}$$

$$e^{-t_L/R_2 C} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{t_L = R_2 \cdot C \cdot \ln 2 = 0.693 \cdot R_2 \cdot C}$$

#### Step 5 — Total Period and Frequency

$$T = t_H + t_L = 0.693(R_1 + R_2)C + 0.693 R_2 C$$

$$T = 0.693(R_1 + 2 R_2) C$$

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.693(R_1 + 2 R_2) C} = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2 R_2) C}$$

#### Step 6 — Duty Cycle

$$D = \frac{t_H}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2 R_2} \times 100\%$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Monostable Pulse Width (tW)

The capacitor charges from **0 V** (held at ground by the discharge transistor in stable state) toward **VCC** through resistance R, and the quasi-stable state ends when V_C reaches **2·VCC/3**.

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

Set $V_C(t_W) = 2 V_{CC} / 3$:

$$\frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-t_W/RC}\right)$$

$$\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-t_W/RC}$$

$$e^{-t_W/RC} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$-\frac{t_W}{RC} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -\ln 3$$

$$\boxed{t_W = R \cdot C \cdot \ln 3 = 1.0986 \cdot RC \approx 1.1 RC}$$

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** *Design an Astable Multivibrator using 555 IC to generate a frequency of **1 kHz** with a duty cycle of **60%**. Assume C = 0.1 μF and VCC = 5 V.*

**Given:** $f = 1 \text{ kHz}$, $D = 60\%$, $C = 0.1 \, \mu F$

**Step 1 — Use Duty Cycle Formula**

$$D = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2 R_2} = 0.6$$

$$R_1 + R_2 = 0.6(R_1 + 2R_2) = 0.6 R_1 + 1.2 R_2$$

$$0.4 R_1 = 0.2 R_2 \implies R_1 = 0.5 R_2$$

**Step 2 — Use Frequency Formula**

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2 R_2) C} = 1000$$

$$R_1 + 2 R_2 = \frac{1.44}{1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 14{,}400 \, \Omega = 14.4 \, k\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Solve the System**

Substitute $R_1 = 0.5 R_2$:

$$0.5 R_2 + 2 R_2 = 2.5 R_2 = 14.4 \, k\Omega$$

$$R_2 = 5.76 \, k\Omega \approx 5.6 \, k\Omega \text{ (standard E12 value)}$$

$$R_1 = 0.5 \times 5.76 = 2.88 \, k\Omega \approx 2.7 \, k\Omega \text{ (standard E12 value)}$$

**Step 4 — Verify Threshold Voltages**

$$V_{TH+} = 2 \cdot V_{CC} / 3 = 2 \times 5 / 3 = 3.33 \, V$$

$$V_{TH-} = V_{CC} / 3 = 5 / 3 = 1.67 \, V$$

**Step 5 — Verify Time Period**

$$T = 0.693 \times (2.7k + 2 \times 5.6k) \times 0.1\mu$$

$$T = 0.693 \times 13.9 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T \approx 0.963 \, ms \implies f \approx 1.038 \, kHz \quad (\text{within } \pm 5\%)$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Performance Metric | Astable Mode | Monostable Mode |
|:-------------------|:-------------|:----------------|
| Number of timing resistors | 2 (R1, R2) | 1 (R) |
| Number of timing capacitors | 1 (C) | 1 (C) |
| Output frequency depends on | R1, R2, C | R, C |
| Minimum supply voltage | **+4.5 V** | **+4.5 V** |
| Maximum output current | **±200 mA** | **±200 mA** |
| Maximum frequency (TTL mode) | **~500 kHz** | **~100 kHz** |
| Output rise/fall time | **~100 ns** | **~100 ns** |
| Trigger input impedance | **High (~10 MΩ)** | **High (~10 MΩ)** |
| Power consumption (typical) | **~10 mA @ 5V** | **~1 mA quiescent** |
| Recoverable from supply glitch? | **Yes (auto-restart)** | **No (requires retrigger)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 555 Internal Block Diagram (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VCC 8V Supply] --> B[Voltage Divider 3x 5kOhm]
    B --> C1[Reference VCC over 3]
    B --> C2[Reference 2VCC over 3]
    C1 --> D[Comparator 1 Lower]
    C2 --> E[Comparator 2 Upper]
    F[Pin 2 TRIG Input] --> D
    G[Pin 6 THR Input] --> E
    D --> H[SR Flip Flop Latch]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Output Buffer Totem Pole]
    I --> J[Pin 3 OUT]
    H --> K[Discharge NMOS Q1]
    K --> L[Pin 7 DIS]
    M[Pin 4 RESET] --> H
    N[Pin 5 CTRL] -.Modulates.-> C1
    N -.Modulates.-> C2
```

### 4.2 Astable Multivibrator Circuit Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC1[VCC] --> R1[R1 Resistor]
    R1 --> NODE_A[Node A: Pin 7]
    NODE_A --> R2[R2 Resistor]
    R2 --> NODE_B[Node B: Pins 2 and 6]
    NODE_B --> CAP[C Timing Capacitor]
    CAP --> GND1[GND]
    NODE_A -.Internal Discharge Path.-> DIS[Pin 7 to GND via NMOS]
    NODE_B --> OUT_LOGIC[Internal Comparator Network]
    OUT_LOGIC --> OUTPUT[Pin 3 OUT Rectangular Wave]
```

### 4.3 Monostable Multivibrator Circuit Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC2[VCC] --> RT[R Timing Resistor]
    RT --> NODE_C[Node C: Pin 7]
    NODE_C --> CAP2[C Timing Capacitor]
    CAP2 --> GND2[GND]
    NODE_C --> NODE_D[Node D: Pins 6 and 2 tied]
    TRIG_PULSE[External Negative Trigger Pulse] --> PULLUP[Pull up R 10k] --> NODE_D
    TRIG_PULSE --> NODE_D
    NODE_D --> CMP_LOGIC[Comparators and Flip Flop]
    CMP_LOGIC --> OUTP[Pin 3 OUT Single Pulse]
    CMP_LOGIC --> DIS_TX[Discharge NMOS controlled by Flip Flop]
    DIS_TX --> NODE_C
```

### 4.4 Astable Timing Waveform Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start Oscillation]) --> CHG{Capacitor Charging Phase}
    CHG -->|Output HIGH| V_RISE[VC rises from VCC over 3 to 2VCC over 3]
    V_RISE --> CHECK{V_C reaches 2VCC over 3?}
    CHECK -->|Yes| DISCHG{Capacitor Discharging Phase}
    DISCHG -->|Output LOW| V_FALL[VC falls from 2VCC over 3 to VCC over 3]
    V_FALL --> CHECK2{V_C reaches VCC over 3?}
    CHECK2 -->|Yes| CHG
    CHECK2 -.->|No, continues| V_FALL
    CHECK -.->|No, continues| V_RISE
```

### 4.5 Monostable State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StableLow
    StableLow: STABLE STATE - Output LOW - C held at 0V
    StableLow --> QuasiStable: Negative Trigger on Pin 2
    QuasiStable: QUASI STABLE - Output HIGH - C charges through R
    QuasiStable --> StableLow: V_C reaches 2VCC over 3 after tW
    StableLow --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Procedure Note:** When wiring the Astable on a breadboard, always connect pin 5 (Control Voltage) to ground via a **0.01 μF bypass capacitor** to filter supply noise. This prevents spurious triggering and stabilizes the output frequency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Explain the function of the control voltage pin (pin 5) of the 555 timer. How is it used in practical applications?*

**Model Answer:**
Pin 5 of the 555 timer is the **Control Voltage** input, which directly accesses the **2·VCC/3** reference point of the internal voltage divider. By applying an external DC voltage (between **45% and 90% of VCC**) at this pin, the designer can modulate the upper and lower threshold levels simultaneously. This is used in **voltage-controlled oscillators (VCO)**, **pulse-width modulators (PWM)**, and **FM signal generation** circuits. In lab practice, pin 5 is usually bypassed to ground via a **0.01 μF capacitor** to prevent high-frequency noise from coupling into the comparator references and causing jitter in the output. **(3 marks)**

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Why cannot a 555 timer in astable configuration achieve exactly 50% duty cycle? What is the remedy?*

**Model Answer:**
The duty cycle formula for astable mode is $D = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2 R_2} \times 100\%$. Since the charging path uses **(R1 + R2)** and the discharging path uses **R2 only**, the numerator is always **greater than or equal to** half the denominator, forcing $D \geq 50\%$. For $D = 50\%$, we would need $R_1 = 0$, which is impractical. The standard remedy is to add a **diode bypass** across R2 (cathode at pin 7, anode at the C node) so that charging current bypasses R2 entirely, making tH ≈ 0.693·R1·C and tL ≈ 0.693·R2·C, giving true 50% duty cycle. Alternatively, a **variable resistor divider** with a diode steering network is used. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. *Forgetting to mention the bypass capacitor on pin 5* (loses 1 mark in design questions)
> 2. *Not specifying the output drive capability (±200 mA)* (loses 1 mark in specifications)
> 3. *Confusing the discharging path resistance* — students often write R1 instead of R2 (loses up to 3 marks in derivations)
> 4. *Incorrectly stating that the Astable has a stable state* (loses 1 mark in conceptual questions)
> 5. *Mixing up 0.693 with 1.0986 in Monostable derivations* (loses 2 marks in calculations)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of the 555 timer connected as an **Astable Multivibrator**. Derive expressions for the charging time, discharging time, frequency, and duty cycle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram:** As shown in Section 4.2. The astable configuration connects pins 2 and 6 together; pin 4 tied to VCC; pin 5 bypassed with 0.01 μF to GND; R1 between VCC and pin 7; R2 between pin 7 and the capacitor node; C between the node and GND.

**Operation:**
- **Initial state:** Assume C is discharged. V_C < VCC/3, so lower comparator SETs flip-flop → output goes HIGH, discharge transistor OFF.
- **Charging phase:** C charges through **(R1 + R2)** from VCC/3 toward VCC. Output remains HIGH.
- **Switchover 1:** When V_C reaches **2·VCC/3**, upper comparator RESETS flip-flop → output goes LOW, discharge transistor ON.
- **Discharging phase:** C discharges through **R2** (pin 7 grounded) from 2·VCC/3 toward 0. Output remains LOW.
- **Switchover 2:** When V_C drops to **VCC/3**, lower comparator SETs flip-flop again → output HIGH, cycle repeats.

**Derivations:** As shown in Section 3.1.

$$t_H = 0.693(R_1 + R_2)C, \quad t_L = 0.693 R_2 C$$

$$T = 0.693(R_1 + 2R_2)C, \quad f = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C}$$

$$D = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\%$$

**(b)** Design an Astable Multivibrator using 555 IC to produce a square wave of frequency **2 kHz** and duty cycle **75%**. Assume VCC = 12 V. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f = 2000$ Hz, $D = 75\%$, $V_{CC} = 12$ V.

**Step 1 — Use Duty Cycle Equation**

$$0.75 = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2 R_2}$$

$$0.75 R_1 + 1.5 R_2 = R_1 + R_2$$

$$0.5 R_2 = 0.25 R_1 \implies R_1 = 2 R_2$$

**Step 2 — Use Frequency Equation**

$$R_1 + 2 R_2 = \frac{1.44}{f \cdot C}$$

Choose **C = 0.1 μF** (standard value):

$$R_1 + 2 R_2 = \frac{1.44}{2000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 7{,}200 \, \Omega = 7.2 \, k\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Solve the System**

Substitute $R_1 = 2 R_2$:

$$2 R_2 + 2 R_2 = 4 R_2 = 7.2 \, k\Omega$$

$$R_2 = 1.8 \, k\Omega \quad (\text{use } 1.8 \, k\Omega \text{ standard})$$

$$R_1 = 3.6 \, k\Omega \quad (\text{use } 3.6 \, k\Omega \text{ standard})$$

**Step 4 — Verify Threshold Voltages**

$$V_{TH+} = 2 \times 12 / 3 = 8 \, V, \quad V_{TH-} = 12 / 3 = 4 \, V$$

**Step 5 — Verify Time Period**

$$T = 0.693 \times (3.6k + 2 \times 1.8k) \times 0.1\mu = 0.693 \times 7.2k \times 0.1\mu = 0.499 \, ms$$

$$f = 1/0.499 \, ms \approx 2.004 \, kHz \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Component List**

- R1 = 3.6 kΩ, R2 = 1.8 kΩ, C = 0.1 μF
- Bypass capacitor on pin 5: 0.01 μF
- Decoupling capacitor on VCC: 0.1 μF
- Output LED indicator: 1 kΩ + LED (optional, for visual verification)

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **Monostable Multivibrator** using 555 IC with circuit diagram and waveforms. Derive the expression for the pulse width. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram:** As shown in Section 4.3. R between VCC and pin 7; C between pins 6/2 and GND; pin 2 driven by a negative trigger pulse via a coupling capacitor with pull-up resistor; pin 4 tied to VCC; pin 5 bypassed to GND via 0.01 μF.

**Operation:**
- **Stable State:** No trigger applied. V_TRIG > VCC/3, so the lower comparator output is LOW. The flip-flop stays RESET → output is LOW. The discharge transistor is ON → C is held near 0 V (shorted to GND through pin 7).
- **Trigger Event:** A **negative pulse** momentarily pulls pin 2 below VCC/3. The lower comparator SETs the flip-flop → output goes HIGH, discharge transistor turns OFF.
- **Quasi-Stable State:** C charges through R from 0 V toward VCC. Output stays HIGH.
- **Auto-Reset:** When V_C reaches **2·VCC/3**, upper comparator RESETS the flip-flop → output returns LOW, discharge transistor turns ON, C rapidly discharges through pin 7, circuit returns to stable state.
- **Retriggering:** If another trigger arrives during the quasi-stable state, the timer **re-triggers**, extending the pulse width (non-retriggerable in some variants).

**Pulse Width Derivation:** (As in Section 3.2)

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC}(1 - e^{-t/RC})$$

At $t = t_W$, $V_C = 2 V_{CC}/3$:

$$\frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC}(1 - e^{-t_W/RC})$$

$$e^{-t_W/RC} = \frac{1}{3} \implies t_W = RC \ln 3 \approx 1.1 RC$$

$$\boxed{t_W = 1.1 RC \text{ seconds}}$$

**(b)** A 555 Monostable circuit uses R = 100 kΩ and C = 1 μF. Calculate the output pulse width. If the trigger pulse has a width of 100 μs, will the circuit function correctly? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Pulse Width**

$$t_W = 1.1 \times R \times C = 1.1 \times 100 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_W = 0.11 \text{ seconds} = 110 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Analyze the Trigger Condition**

The trigger pulse width is $t_{TRIG} = 100 \, \mu s = 0.0001$ s.

The trigger pulse must be **shorter than** the output pulse width, i.e., $t_{TRIG} < t_W$.

Since $100 \, \mu s \ll 110 \, ms$ ✓, the condition is satisfied.

**Step 3 — Verify Recovery Time**

The monostable requires a **recovery time** (also called dead time) of approximately **t_W** to fully discharge the timing capacitor. The next trigger should not arrive before this recovery period elapses, otherwise the timing will be inaccurate.

**Step 4 — Other Critical Checks**

- **VCC compliance:** For 100 kΩ and 1 μF, the leakage current must be < **100 nA** for accurate timing — ensure C is a low-leakage film or tantalum type.
- **Trigger amplitude:** The trigger must drop below **VCC/3** (e.g., below 1.67 V for VCC = 5 V).
- **Pin 2 protection:** A series resistor (≥ 1 kΩ) is recommended to limit current into pin 2 during trigger.

**Conclusion:** The circuit will function correctly. The output is a **110 ms wide HIGH pulse** for each negative trigger, and the trigger pulse width of 100 μs is well within acceptable limits.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- **555 IC has 8 pins** and is a hybrid analog/digital device. The name "555" comes from the **three 5 kΩ internal resistors** forming the voltage divider.
- **Astable = no stable state**; output continuously oscillates. **Monostable = one stable state**; output goes HIGH only on trigger.
- The two internal threshold voltages are **VCC/3** (lower, triggers SET) and **2·VCC/3** (upper, triggers RESET).
- **Astable charging path:** VCC → R1 → R2 → C. **Discharging path:** C → R2 → pin 7 → GND (R1 is bypassed by the discharge transistor).
- **Critical formulas to memorize:**
  - Astable: $t_H = 0.693(R_1+R_2)C$, $t_L = 0.693 R_2 C$, $f = 1.44/[(R_1+2R_2)C]$, $D = (R_1+R_2)/(R_1+2R_2) \times 100\%$
  - Monostable: $t_W = 1.1 R C$
- The **duty cycle in Astable mode is always ≥ 50%** without a diode bypass across R2.
- **Pin 5 (Control Voltage)** must be bypassed to GND via a **0.01 μF capacitor** in standard applications to prevent noise coupling.
- **Pin 4 (RESET)** must be tied to VCC for normal operation; pulling it LOW (< 0.4 V) forces output LOW and discharges C.
- **Trigger pulse for Monostable** must be a **negative-going pulse** that takes pin 2 below VCC/3, and its width must be **less than t_W**.
- **Output drive capability** of the 555 is **±200 mA**, which can directly drive small relays, LEDs, buzzers, and speakers.
- **Supply voltage range:** **+4.5 V to +16 V** (NE555); CMOS variants (TLC555, LMC555) work down to **+2 V**.
- **Maximum usable frequency:** approximately **500 kHz** for Astable in standard TTL mode.
- **Common lab mistakes to avoid:** (1) Floating pin 4 (causes random RESET), (2) using electrolytic capacitors with high leakage for long-duration timers, (3) not using a current-limiting resistor on output LED.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to PCB layout software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL308 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Introduction to PCB layout software # Module Experiment 2 — Introduction to PCB Layout Software ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **PCB Layout Software (EDA Tool):** A specialized category of *Electronic Design Automation (EDA)* software used to capture an electronic circuit in schematic form, assign physical *footprints* to each component, and route electrical *traces* on a layered *printed circuit board* to produce manufacturing-ready files (Gerber / NC drill / pick-and-place). In the KTU 2024 lab manual, the term explicitly refers to **computer-aided tools that translate a hand-drawn or protoboard-verified analog circuit into a manufacturable board file** — bridging the gap between *circuit theory* and *hardware realization*. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are building a house. The **schematic** is the *architect's wiring plan* — it shows which switch connects to which bulb, but it does not say *where* the wires physically run inside the walls.
