The **PCB layout software** is the *interior electrician + civil engineer combined* — it decides: - *Where* every component (resistor, IC, capacitor) physically sits on the board. - *Which path* the copper wire (trace) takes from pin A to pin B. - *How thick* the copper must be to safely carry the current. - *Where* to drill holes, *where* to print labels, and *where* to leave exposed copper pads. > [!IMPORTANT] > A schematic answers **WHAT is connected to WHAT**. > A PCB layout answers **WHERE it physically lives and HOW it is wired in copper**. ### 1.3 Key Standardized Terms (Must Memorize for KTU Viva) | Term | Meaning (Kerala Board Standard) | |---|---| | **EDA** | Electronic Design Automation — the umbrella class of tools. | | **Schematic** | Logical drawing of the circuit using symbols. | | **Footprint** | The physical land pattern (pads + outline) for a component. | | **Netlist** | A text list of every electrical connection (net) in the circuit. | | **Trace / Track** | A copper conductor drawn on a PCB layer. | | **Via** | A plated through-hole used to carry a signal between layers. | | **DRC** | Design Rule Check — automated verification of manufacturability. | | **Gerber File** | Industry-standard CAM output sent to the PCB fabricator. | | **Silkscreen** | White (or yellow) legend layer — component outlines & reference designators. | | **Soldermask** | Green (usually) insulating lacquer covering the copper, leaving only pads exposed. | > [!TIP] > **Standard copper weight** in hobby/educational PCBs = **1 oz/ft² ≈ 35 µm (microns)** thickness. This is the default assumption whenever KTU problems do not specify otherwise. --- ## 2. Why PCB Layout Software? — The Real Engineering Problem Before software, circuits were hand-taped onto mylar sheets at 2× or 4× scale and then photographically reduced. Modern software enforces: 1. **Precision** — sub-millimeter trace placement impossible by hand. 2. **Repeatability** — the exact same board can be fabricated thousands of times. 3. **DRC enforcement** — catches clearance violations, unconnected nets, and short circuits **before** money is spent on fabrication. 4. **Library management** — standard footprints (SOIC, TO-220, 0805, DIP-14) are pre-defined and reusable. 5. **Manufacturing output** — automatic generation of Gerber, drill, and BOM files. > [!WARNING] > A common KTU viva trap: *"Is PCB layout software the same as a circuit simulator?"* — **NO**. Simulators (Multisim, LTspice, Proteus-ISIS) solve equations. Layout software (Proteus-ARES, KiCad, EAGLE, Altium) places copper. Some suites bundle both, but their functions are distinct. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Functional Halves of Every PCB Suite Most KTU-recommended tools are **integrated schematic + layout environments**. The flow is strictly two-phase: ### Phase A — Schematic Capture (Front-End) 1. Place component symbols from a library. 2. Wire them together using the *net tool*. 3. Annotate reference designators (**R1, C3, U2**). 4. Run **Electrical Rule Check (ERC)** — detects unconnected pins, power conflicts. 5. Export a **netlist** + assign a **footprint** to every symbol. ### Phase B — PCB Layout (Back-End) 1. Import the netlist. 2. Define the **board outline** and **stack-up** (number of layers). 3. **Place** components strategically (analog away from digital, decoupling caps near IC power pins). 4. **Route** the traces — interactive or auto-router. 5. Pour **copper planes** (GND / VCC) for low-impedance returns. 6. Run **DRC** — verify clearance, trace width, via size, annular ring. 7. Generate **Gerber (RS-274X) + NC Drill** files for the fabricator. ## 2.2 Popular PCB Tools KTU Students Will Encounter | Tool | License | Common in KTU Labs? | Key Trait | |---|---|---|---| | **KiCad** | Open-source (free) | Yes (most Kerala colleges) | Industry-grade, unlimited board size, 3D viewer. | | **EAGLE** | Free for hobby / paid for pro | Yes (legacy) | Now Autodesk-owned, strict board area limits in free version. | | **Proteus (ARES module)** | Paid educational license | Yes (very common in Kerala polytechnics & some B.Tech labs) | Bundled with ISIS schematic & simulator. | | **EasyEDA** | Free, web-based | Yes (used with JLCPCB) | Browser-based, library linked to JLCPCB parts. | | **OrCAD / Allegro** | Commercial (Cadence) | Industry standard | High-speed / multi-layer enterprise design. | | **Altium Designer** | Commercial | Industry premium | Most popular in Indian product companies. | > [!IMPORTANT] > For your KTU 2024 lab record, **KiCad** is the de-facto recommendation because it is free, cross-platform (Windows/Linux), and accepted in viva. If your lab uses **Proteus**, the ARES module follows the identical conceptual flow. ## 2.3 PCB Layer Stack-Up — The Hidden Vertical World A PCB is not a flat copper sheet. It is a **sandwich of insulating dielectric layers and copper layers**. The two most common educational configurations: ### 2-Layer Board (Most KTU Lab Projects) ``` TOP SILKSCREEN (white legend, R1, C3, etc.) TOP SOLDERMASK (green lacquer, only pads exposed) TOP COPPER (1 oz/ft² ≈ 35 µm) ← signals + components DIELECTRIC CORE (FR-4, ~1.6 mm thick, εr ≈ 4.3) BOTTOM COPPER (1 oz/ft² ≈ 35 µm) ← usually GND plane BOTTOM SOLDERMASK (green lacquer) BOTTOM SILKSCREEN (white legend) ``` ### 4-Layer Board (Power, GND, Signal, Signal) ``` Layer 1 : Signal (Top) — components, short traces Layer 2 : Ground Plane — continuous GND pour Layer 3 : Power Plane — VCC / +V rail Layer 4 : Signal (Bottom) — remaining routing ``` > [!NOTE] > The **dielectric constant εr** of standard **FR-4** is **≈ 4.3 (at 1 MHz)**. This is a constant examiners love to ask in viva because it appears in both analog and digital design contexts. ## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet Although this is a lab topic, the following *engineering numbers* and *formulas* are high-yield: | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Formula | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Standard copper thickness | $t_{Cu}$ | **1 oz/ft² = 35 µm** (2 oz = 70 µm) | µm | | Standard PCB thickness | $h$ | **1.6 mm** (0.8 mm, 2.0 mm also common) | mm | | FR-4 dielectric constant | $\varepsilon_r$ | **≈ 4.3** (at 1 MHz) | — | | Trace resistance | $R_{trace}$ | $R = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{w \cdot t_{Cu}}$ | Ω | | Trace current capacity (IPC-2152 external, conservative) | $I$ | $I = k \cdot (\Delta T)^{b} \cdot A^{c}$ | A | | Annular ring (typical) | $r$ | **≥ 0.15 mm** (6 mil) for fab houses | mm | | Drill to copper clearance | — | **≥ 0.2 mm** (8 mil) | mm | | Track to track clearance | — | **≥ 0.2 mm** (8 mil) for 1 oz copper | mm | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL LaTeX RULE:** Inside any markdown table row, **never** use the vertical pipe `|`. The trace resistance formula above is written using `\cdot` and `\dfrac` to keep the table syntactically safe. The same applies to absolute-value delimiters — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|`. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility PCB layout software is the **single most critical tool** that converts an electronics engineer's design intent into a tangible product. Every smartphone, every ECU in a car, every medical implant contains a PCB designed in one of these tools. For an analog lab student, the immediate utility is: 1. Replace messy **breadboard / general-purpose PCB (Veroboard)** prototypes with a **clean, professional, single-board** solution. 2. **Eliminate wiring errors** — no more loose jumpers that fall out. 3. **Achieve repeatable performance** — parasitics are controlled because track geometry is known. 4. **Submit a board-ready Gerbers** to an online fab (PCBWay, JLCPCB, LionCircuits) for **₹100–₹500** for 5–10 boards. # Step-by-Step Walkthrough — The Canonical PCB Design Flow This section is the "exhaustive derivation" equivalent for a procedural lab topic. Every step is **explicitly** written out so you can reproduce it in your KTU lab record verbatim. ## Step 1 — Open the Project & Save Frequently 1. Launch the EDA tool (hereafter referred to generically; KiCad / Proteus ARES / EAGLE all follow this pattern). 2. `File → New Project → Project_Name` 3. **Create a dedicated project folder** — the tool will generate `.sch`, `.pcb`, library cache, and backup files. Keeping them in one folder prevents broken links when the file is moved. > [!NOTE] > In KiCad, the project is a `.kicad_pro` file; in Proteus ARES, the layout is embedded inside the `.DSN` design file. Always use the tool's **own save format**, not a generic `.txt`. ## Step 2 — Schematic Capture (Front-End) 1. From the **library browser**, place each component symbol: - `R`, `C`, `D` (diode), `Q` (transistor), `U` (op-amp IC like µA741 / LM358), `LED`, `CONN2` (connector). 2. Assign **Reference Designators**: `R1, R2, C1, U1, D1` — these appear later on the silkscreen. 3. Assign **Value**: `1k`, `10µF`, `LM358N`, `1N4148`. 4. Use the **Wire tool** (not the Line tool) to connect pins. The Wire tool creates an electrical *net*; the Line tool is purely graphical. 5. Place a **Power Port** (`VCC`, `+12V`, `GND`) on every supply pin. 6. **ERC** → `Tools → Electrical Rules Check`. Fix every error and warning. ## Step 3 — Netlist Generation & Footprint Assignment 1. `Tools → Annotate Schematic` — auto-fills all `?` reference designators. 2. For **every** symbol, open its properties and assign a **footprint**: - Resistor 1/4 W axial → footprint `AXIAL-0.3` or `0204` (KiCad) - Resistor SMD 0805 → footprint `0805` - IC µA741 (8-pin DIP) → footprint `DIP-8` or `SOIC-8` - LED 3 mm → footprint `LED-3` - Electrolytic capacitor → footprint `CP_Radial_D5.0mm` or similar. 3. Export the **netlist** (`Tools → Generate Netlist`). > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU record mistake:** Forgetting the footprint. A perfectly drawn schematic with no footprints produces a *blank* PCB layout. The board will have components in the netlist but **zero land patterns to place**. ## Step 4 — Switch to PCB Editor & Import Netlist 1. Open the **PCB editor** (KiCad PCBnew / Proteus ARES / EAGLE Board). 2. Set the **board canvas units** (mm or mil — **mm preferred for KTU**). 3. **Import Netlist** — every component appears on the right as a *ratsnest* (jumble of thin lines showing the unrouted connections). ## Step 5 — Define the Board Outline 1. On a dedicated mechanical layer (`Edge.Cuts` in KiCad), draw a rectangle (or any shape) representing the **physical edges** of the PCB. 2. Typical student project: **50 mm × 50 mm** to **100 mm × 80 mm**. ## Step 6 — Component Placement 1. Drag each component from the ratsnest onto the board. 2. **Placement rules (golden KTU viva points):** - **Decoupling capacitors (0.1 µF)** go *as close as possible* to the IC's VCC pin. - **Input on the left, output on the right** — signal flow left → right. - **Polarized components** (electrolytic caps, diodes, ICs) all face the *same direction* — easier to solder, fewer mistakes. - **Heat-generating components** (voltage regulators like 7805, power transistors) near the edge for heat-sink mounting. - **Test points** for oscilloscope probes at key nodes (input, output, mid-stage). ## Step 7 — Track Routing 1. Set **Design Rules** first: - Track width: **0.5 mm (≈ 20 mil)** for signals, **1.0 mm or more** for power rails. - Clearance: **0.2 mm** between any two tracks. - Via drill: **0.6 mm**, via diameter: **1.0 mm**. 2. Choose the **Top layer** (F.Cu) for primary routing. 3. Route critical nets **manually** (VCC, GND, op-amp inverting/non-inverting inputs). 4. Use the **auto-router** only for the leftover signal ratsnest. 5. Place a **GND copper pour** on the Bottom layer (`B.Cu`) — this acts as a ground plane and is essential for analog noise immunity. > [!TIP] > A KTU mark-winning tip: **Use 45° bends, never 90°** at corners. Right-angle bends in the *top* layer are not strictly a fabrication problem (acid traps), but they reflect signal energy at high frequencies. Examiners award bonus marks when the layout shows chamfered corners. ## Step 8 — Design Rule Check (DRC) `Tools → Design Rules Check → Run`. The tool will flag: - Clearance violations (track too close to another track or pad). - Unconnected nets (ratsnest still visible). - Copper too close to the board edge. - Drill holes missing or too small. **Fix every error** before generating outputs. DRC errors = automatic mark deduction in the KTU external evaluation. ## Step 9 — Gerber & NC Drill Export 1. `File → Fabrication Outputs → Gerber Files` — generates one `.gbr` file per layer (top copper, bottom copper, top silkscreen, top soldermask, bottom silkscreen, bottom soldermask, board outline). 2. `File → Fabrication Outputs → NC Drill Files` — generates the drill location file. 3. Zip these files. Upload to a fab house (JLCPCB, PCBWay, LionCircuits) for production. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Viva Question:** *"What is the difference between a Gerber file and a Netlist?"* > **Answer:** A netlist is a *logical* list of connections (the schematic's wire list). A Gerber file is a *physical* vector image of the copper pattern on each layer — it is what the photoplotter in the PCB fab reads to expose the photoresist. ## Step 10 — 3D Preview & BOM Export Most modern tools (KiCad 3D Viewer, Altium, EAGLE 3D) render a realistic 3-D model showing the components on the board. This is excellent for verifying mechanical fit and producing a publication-quality figure for the lab record. Export the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** as a `.csv` — required for the KTU lab record appendix. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Complete PCB Design Workflow The figure below is the canonical KTU-lab workflow. It is what an examiner expects to see in the **Design Procedure** section of your lab record. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Circuit Idea] --> B[Schematic Capture
Place symbols, wire nets] B --> C[Annotate Ref-Designators
R1, C1, U1] C --> D[Assign Footprints
0805, DIP-8, TO-220] D --> E[Run ERC
Electrical Rule Check] E --> F[Generate Netlist] F --> G[Switch to PCB Editor] G --> H[Define Board Outline
Edge.Cuts layer] H --> I[Import Netlist
Ratsnest appears] I --> J[Place Components
Decoupling caps near ICs] J --> K[Route Tracks
45 degree bends, 0.5mm] K --> L[Pour GND Plane
Bottom layer] L --> M[Run DRC
Design Rule Check] M --> N{DRC Clean?} N -- No --> K N -- Yes --> O[Add Silkscreen
R1, C1, logos] O --> P[Export Gerber Files
RS-274X] O --> Q[Export NC Drill File] O --> R[Export BOM.csv] P --> S[Upload to Fab House
JLCPCB, PCBWay] Q --> S R --> S S --> T[3D Preview & Final Review] T --> U[END: Board Ready
for Manufacturing] ``` ## 4.2 PCB Cross-Section (2-Layer Stack-Up) — Functional Block View ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph stack2L[Two-Layer PCB Stack-Up] direction TB topSilk["TOP SILKSCREEN
White legend, R1, C3, U1"] topMask["TOP SOLDERMASK
Green lacquer, exposes pads only"] topCu["TOP COPPER F.Cu
35 um 1 oz per sq ft
Signals + Components"] core["DIELECTRIC CORE
FR-4, 1.6 mm, epsilon-r 4.3"] botCu["BOTTOM COPPER B.Cu
35 um 1 oz per sq ft
GND Plane pour"] botMask["BOTTOM SOLDERMASK
Green lacquer"] botSilk["BOTTOM SILKSCREEN
Optional legend"] end topSilk --> topMask --> topCu --> core --> botCu --> botMask --> botSilk ``` ## 4.3 Schematic-to-Layout-to-Manufacturing Data Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR symLib[("Component Library
Symbols")] --> sch[Schematic .sch] fpLib[("Footprint Library
DIP-8, 0805, TO-220")] --> sch sch -->|ERC + Annotate| net[Netlist .net] net -->|Import| pcb[PCB Layout .pcb] pcb -->|DRC check| pcb pcb -->|Plot| gerber["Gerber .gbr
1 per layer"] pcb -->|Drill| drill[NC Drill .drl] pcb -->|Export| bom["BOM .csv"] gerber --> fab[("PCB Fab House
JLCPCB, PCBWay")] drill --> fab bom --> assembly["Assembly House
Pick and Place"] ``` ## 4.4 Footprint Anatomy — What a Component Land Pattern Looks Like ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph fp[Footprint of an 0805 SMD Resistor] direction LR padA["Pad 1
Copper, Gold plated

Define a PCB layout software. List any two popular PCB design tools used in industry.** > > **Model Answer:** > A *PCB layout software* is an Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tool used to convert an electronic circuit's schematic into a physically manufacturable printed circuit board by placing components, routing copper traces, and generating fabrication files (Gerber / NC drill). > Two popular tools: **KiCad** (open-source) and **Altium Designer** (commercial, industry premium). *Other accepted answers: EAGLE, OrCAD Allegro, Proteus ARES, EasyEDA, DipTrace.* --- > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > **Q2. What is a footprint in PCB design? Why is footprint assignment a critical step before moving from schematic to PCB layout?** > > **Model Answer:** > A *footprint* (also called a *land pattern* or *package*) is the physical pad geometry and outline on the PCB that matches the physical dimensions and lead configuration of a real component (e.g., DIP-8 for a µA741 op-amp, 0805 for an SMD resistor). > It is critical because **without a footprint, the layout tool cannot create solderable copper pads for the component**, so the component has no physical place to be soldered even though it exists in the netlist. The schematic symbol must be paired with a footprint during the *netlist export* stage — otherwise the resulting PCB is empty. --- ## Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice between A and B) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]** ### Question A — 14 Marks **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **complete step-by-step workflow of designing a printed circuit board** using any standard EDA tool (KiCad / Proteus ARES / EAGLE). Clearly state the input and output of every stage. **(b) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the **2-layer PCB stack-up** for a typical analog circuit board. State the standard thickness and dielectric constant of the FR-4 core and the standard copper weight used in KTU lab projects. #### Model Solution for Q.A(a): | Stage | Input | Action | Output | |---|---|---|---| | 1. Schematic Capture | Circuit idea, library symbols | Place symbols, wire with Wire tool | `.sch` file | | 2. Annotation | `.sch` | Auto-number R1, R2, U1, C1 | Annotated schematic | | 3. Footprint Mapping | Schematic + footprint library | Assign package (e.g., DIP-8) to every symbol | Symbol-to-footprint table | | 4. ERC | Schematic | Check unconnected pins, power conflicts | ERC report (errors / warnings) | | 5. Netlist | Annotated + mapped schematic | `Tools → Generate Netlist` | `.net` file | | 6. PCB Editor Setup | New PCB file | Set units (mm), grid, design rules | Empty PCB canvas | | 7. Outline Definition | Board dimensions | Draw rectangle on Edge.Cuts | Board outline | | 8. Netlist Import | `.net` | Load into PCB editor | Ratsnest + modules on canvas | | 9. Placement | Ratsnest | Drag components, follow placement rules | Placed parts | | 10. Routing | Placed parts | Route with 0.5 mm track, 45° bends | Routed board | | 11. Plane Pour | Routed board | Pour GND on B.Cu | Ground plane | | 12. DRC | Routed board | `Tools → DRC` | Clean / error report | | 13. Silkscreen | Routed board | Add R1, C1, logos on F.Silkscreen | Labeled board | | 14. Fabrication Output | Clean board | Plot Gerber + NC drill | `.gbr` + `.drl` | | 15. Fab Upload | Gerber zip | Submit to JLCPCB / PCBWay | Manufactured PCB | #### Model Solution for Q.A(b): For a typical 2-layer board used in KTU analog labs: - **Layer 1 — Top Silkscreen (F.Silkscreen):** White epoxy-ink legend. Contains reference designators (`R1, U1, C3`), component outlines, logos, version number. - **Layer 2 — Top Soldermask (F.Mask):** Green (or red/blue/black) lacquer. Covers all copper except the pads to be soldered. Prevents solder bridges. - **Layer 3 — Top Copper (F.Cu):** The signal layer. **Standard thickness = 35 µm (1 oz/ft²).** Components and most traces live here. - **Layer 4 — Dielectric Core:** **FR-4 fiberglass-epoxy**, **standard thickness 1.6 mm**, **dielectric constant εᵣ ≈ 4.3 (at 1 MHz).** - **Layer 5 — Bottom Copper (B.Cu):** Mirror layer, **35 µm copper.** Used for GND plane (recommended for analog) or additional routing. - **Layer 6 — Bottom Soldermask (B.Mask).** - **Layer 7 — Bottom Silkscreen (B.Silkscreen).** The 2-layer board is the minimum-cost, simplest-fabrication stack-up accepted by every online fab house. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following terms with a one-sentence explanation each: **(i)** Netlist, **(ii)** Footprint, **(iii)** DRC, **(iv)** Gerber file, **(v)** Silkscreen, **(vi)** Soldermask, **(vii)** Via. **(b) [7 Marks]** A 25 mm long PCB trace of width 0.5 mm is to be drawn on the top copper layer (1 oz/ft², i.e. 35 µm thick). Calculate **(i)** the cross-sectional area of the trace, **(ii)** the DC resistance of the trace (use ρ₍Cu₎ = 1.72 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m), and **(iii)** the voltage drop when it carries 1 A. #### Model Solution for Q.B(a): - **(i) Netlist:** A machine-readable text list of all electrical connections (nets) between component pins, generated from the schematic. - **(ii) Footprint:** The physical copper land pattern (pads + outline + silkscreen + courtyard) on a PCB that matches a real component's package. - **(iii) DRC (Design Rule Check):** An automated verification step in the PCB editor that flags clearance, width, via, and unconnected-net violations before fabrication. - **(iv) Gerber file:** An industry-standard vector image file (RS-274X format) that describes the copper pattern, mask, and silkscreen of each PCB layer for the photoplotter. - **(v) Silkscreen:** The non-conductive legend layer (usually white) printed on top of the soldermask for component identification. - **(vi) Soldermask:** The insulating lacquer (usually green) covering the copper, leaving only the pads exposed for soldering. - **(vii) Via:** A plated through-hole that electrically connects a trace on one layer to a trace on another layer. #### Model Solution for Q.B(b): **Given:** - Length $L = 25 \text{ mm} = 25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ - Width $w = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ - Thickness $t_{Cu} = 35 \text{ µm} = 35 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$ - $\rho_{Cu} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \text{ Ω·m}$ - Current $I = 1 \text{ A}$ **(i) Cross-sectional area:** $$A = w \times t_{Cu} = (0.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (35 \times 10^{-6})$$ $$A = 17.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2 = 1.75 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$$ **(ii) DC resistance:** $$R = \frac{\rho_{Cu} \cdot L}{A} = \frac{(1.72 \times 10^{-8}) \times (25 \times 10^{-3})}{1.75 \times 10^{-8}}$$ $$R = \frac{4.30 \times 10^{-10}}{1.75 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.02457 \text{ Ω}$$ $$\boxed{R \approx 24.57 \text{ mΩ}}$$ **(iii) Voltage drop at 1 A:** $$V_{drop} = I \cdot R = 1 \times 0.02457 = 0.02457 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{V_{drop} \approx 24.57 \text{ mV}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Read Carefully:** > 1. **Unit conversions** are the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks on numerical PCB questions. Always convert mm to m and µm to m *before* plugging into ρ. > 2. **Confusing copper weight (oz/ft²) with thickness (µm):** 1 oz/ft² = 35 µm, **NOT** 35 mm or 35 mil. Examiners set a trick question using "mil" (1 mil = 25.4 µm) to test this. > 3. **Forgetting the footprint assignment** is a guaranteed 2-mark cut in schematic-to-PCB flow questions. Always mention footprint mapping explicitly. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **PCB layout software = EDA tool** that converts schematic → manufacturable board files. - [ ] The canonical flow is: **Schematic → ERC → Footprint Mapping → Netlist → PCB Outline → Placement → Routing → Plane Pour → DRC → Silkscreen → Gerber/Drill → Fab House.** - [ ] **Schematic** = logical "what is connected to what." **PCB Layout** = physical "where and how it is wired in copper." - [ ] **Netlist** is the bridge between the schematic editor and the PCB editor — it carries connectivity, not geometry. - [ ] **Footprint** must be assigned to *every* symbol before netlist export; otherwise the PCB will be empty. - [ ] **Gerber file (RS-274X)** is the manufacturing output — one file per layer. It is *not* a netlist. - [ ] **DRC** is mandatory; submitting a board with DRC errors causes fabrication failures and mark deduction. - [ ] **2-layer board** is the standard KTU lab configuration: top copper, FR-4 core (1.6 mm, εᵣ ≈ 4.3), bottom copper. - [ ] **Standard copper thickness** for educational PCBs = **1 oz/ft² = 35 µm**. - [ ] **Track width** for signals: ≥ 0.25 mm (10 mil). **For power rails:** ≥ 0.5 mm or wider depending on current. - [ ] **Clearance** between tracks and pads: **≥ 0.2 mm** for 1 oz copper. - [ ] **45° bends, never 90°** — looks professional, avoids acid traps in fabrication. - [ ] **Decoupling capacitors** (0.1 µF) go *closest* to the IC's VCC pin — common viva question. - [ ] **GND plane** on the bottom layer is essential for analog noise immunity — never leave the bottom layer as a ratsnest of unconnected islands. - [ ] **Trace resistance formula:** $R = \dfrac{\rho_{Cu} \cdot L}{w \cdot t_{Cu}}$ — memorize it. - [ ] **Popular free tools for KTU labs:** KiCad (best for record), Proteus ARES (if your lab has a license), EasyEDA (browser-based, ties to JLCPCB). - [ ] **Viva one-liner to remember:** "A simulator *solves* the circuit; a layout tool *places* it in copper."
